  solo, bilingual EN + 简体中文 README, 53 GitHub stars —
  the strongest external traction of any of the camp's
  artefacts, though it now sits in the archived band of
  Chan's open-source catalogue rather than the active
  README lineup.
- **`douyin-mall-java-template`** — the Spring Boot + Maven
  counterpart with Maven wrapper, conventional `src/main` /
  `src/test` layout — 13 commits, 100% solo, bilingual
  README. Intended as the Java track's mirror of the Go
  template.

The five repositories together — algorithm track, capstone
backend, supplementary frontend, two architecture templates
— represent a deliberately overcomplete portfolio of camp
artefacts: most trainees deliver the algorithm submissions
and the team capstone; very few add a solo frontend and two
production-shape architecture templates on top.
narrative.technicalHighlights:
**Java / Spring Boot 3 + MyBatis + MySQL + Redis +
Alibaba Cloud** on the Team-115 capstone, which is the
canonical Chinese-internet backend stack. The architecture
is layered along the lines mainstream ByteDance projects
use — controller / service / mapper separation, Redis-
backed caching for hot product and cart paths, MyBatis for
explicit SQL control rather than JPA's abstraction, and
Alibaba Cloud services for OSS / SMS / message queue
integration. This is the stack a Chinese backend engineer
is expected to have shipped in production.

**Go + Gin clean-architecture template** in
`douyin-mall-go-template`. The directory shape (`cmd/`,
`internal/`, `pkg/`, `api/`, `configs/`, `docs/`, `logs/`,
`frontend/`) is the layout the Go community treats as
production-correct — separation between executable entry
points, internal packages, and reusable libraries; explicit
config and docs trees; logs as a deployment artefact. The
template ships JWT authentication, RESTful API conventions,
and a complete e-commerce domain model so that a learner
forking it has the shape of a real service rather than a
scaffolded stub.

**Vue 3 + Vite + Pinia + Vue Router + TypeScript** on the
frontend supplement, with a non-trivial testing pyramid
(Vitest for unit, Cypress for e2e via `start-server-and-
test`) — explicitly the configuration that lets you run
e2e tests both in CI (`vite preview` + `cypress run`) and
interactively (`vite dev` + `cypress open`).

**Algorithm fluency across ten topic areas** — the 81
problems on Juejin span the topic distribution Chinese
big-tech interview panels actually test, including the
weaker spots most candidates have (bit manipulation,
simulation, encoding/decoding) rather than just the Leetcode
favourites (arrays, DP). Each solution has explicit
complexity analysis, which is how the camp graded them.

**Bilingual technical documentation discipline** — every
repository I authored or led for the camp ships
side-by-side English and 简体中文 READMEs at production
quality. This is the documentation expectation inside a
Chinese megacorp with global products, and it's a habit I
carried out of the camp into every subsequent project.

**Team collaboration at the right scale** — being the #2
committer on an 8-person team taught the specific muscles
that solo projects don't: PR review etiquette, integration
conflict resolution, sharing test fixtures across modules,
and writing commit messages that another contributor will
read.

work.publicSummary:
Backend Developer in ByteDance's Youth Training Camp (青训营) cohort
"Rebirth: I Code at Youth Training Camp" (Team 115). Completed
ByteDance MarsCode's 81-problem algorithm curriculum on Juejin with
full write-ups and complexity analysis (published as
`juejin-algorithm-practice`); was the #2-most-prolific committer on
the team's Spring Boot 3 + MySQL + Redis Douyin Mall capstone (45 of
174 team commits); then voluntarily built three additional
open-source artefacts beyond the camp's scope — a solo Vue 3 frontend
integrating against the team backend, a Go + Gin clean-architecture
template (53 GitHub stars, since archived from the active README
lineup), and a Java + Spring Boot counterpart template — all
bilingual EN + 简体中文.

work.name: CORDE (NZ infrastructure & civil construction)
work.position: Full Stack Developer & Lead Documenter (Lincoln University COMP693 Industry Placement)

narrative.impactHeadline: #1-most-prolific contributor (42% of 539 commits) on CORDE's offline-first React Native field-operations app — still widely used today by CORDE's water/civil-infrastructure crews. Also owned 97.9% of the documentation track end-to-end.
narrative.companyContext:
**CORDE** is a Canterbury-based **New Zealand infrastructure
and civil-construction company** — 30+ years of operations,
roughly 164 staff (LinkedIn category 201–500), trading
nationally under the tagline *"Trusted Connections."* In
February 2022 three separate Canterbury entities were unified
under the CORDE brand. The company operates across three
specialist arms — **Civil Construction**, **Regional Asset
Maintenance** (open-space management; introduced autonomous
mowing tech to NZ via partnerships with Colbrook Industries
and RC Mowers USA), and **Water Infrastructure / three waters**
— across three Canterbury offices: head office at **85
Hoskyns Road, Rolleston**, plus **Timaru** (South Canterbury)
and **Rangiora** (North Canterbury). Recognised projects
include Te Whāriki Subdivision Stage 3, Windsorcare Village
Stage 1, and the Ellesmere-to-Pines Pipeline Project; awards
include **CCNZ Canterbury Contractor of the Year** and a
**Construction Excellence National Award**. CORDE recently
acquired Arnold Jensen Electrical (70+-year-old NZ electrical
specialist) to bring electrical capability in-house. This
is a substantial regional employer, not a small client.

The engagement was a **Lincoln University COMP693 industry-
placement capstone** — the practicum module of the Master of
Applied Computing programme — embedded directly inside CORDE
with weekly on-site meetings at their Canterbury offices,
distributed online work between meetings, and CORDE's own
engineering processes (Azure DevOps for project management,
Figma for design hand-off, Apifox + Swagger for API contract
testing) governing how the work shipped. Five months from
June 2024 kickoff to November 2024 delivery.
narrative.mission:
Solve a concrete operational problem inside a working
infrastructure firm: CORDE's field crews — water-
infrastructure technicians, civil-construction site teams,
regional-asset-maintenance operators — routinely work in
rural and semi-rural Canterbury where mobile network coverage
is unreliable. The company's central operations platform
(referred to as "Workbench" in the API contracts) is web-
based and assumes connectivity. The mobile app's job was to
make the field worker's tablet the **system of record for
the moment of work**, capture text and photos and GPS
simultaneously, hold it locally with integrity guarantees,
and synchronise back to Workbench cleanly when connectivity
returned — including handling the conflict cases that real
offline-first systems have to handle. This is a real
productivity problem worth real engineering for a 164-staff
infrastructure operator.
narrative.myMandate:
Full-stack developer on a two-active-developer Lincoln
University team (myself plus Luke Shi / Zhenzhou Shi),
reporting directly to **Barry Clark** on CORDE's side as
the business sponsor and to **Kullum Ladley** as CORDE's
technical liaison and Workbench API specification author.
Mandate covered: front-end implementation across the entire
React Native + Native Base UI layer, database design (ERD
work co-designed with CORDE's existing database slices),
business-logic diagrams and architectural documentation,
weekly sprint-meeting question sets sent to CORDE, stage-
development presentation decks, live app demos and screen
recordings, and APK packaging for weekly CORDE reviews.
Documentation lead was an explicit role inside the team —
the docs repository carries a top-level folder literally
titled "*Chan Meng's Documentation Contribution Collection*"
with separate sub-folders for each of those mandates.
narrative.impactDelivered:
**Shipped a production-quality React Native offline-data-
collection mobile app** for CORDE's field-operations
workflow. The codebase carries **539 commits across three
contributors**; **my 227 commits (combining the Chan Meng
and Chan handles) make me the #1-most-prolific contributor
— 42% of the entire commit history** — with Luke Shi (the
repo owner under his GitHub handle gentoo111) on 28.6% and
Xue Zheng on 25.8%. The app integrates with CORDE's
Workbench central operations platform via the API contract
documented across a sequence of **15 specifications
authored by Kullum Ladley** (the same Kullum named in every
API document's filename prefix `YYYYMMDD_Kullum_...`),
covering Workbench API calls, log creation, mobile-app
authentication, log extension fields, file-upload pipelines,
and group completion workflow — all captured under version
control as the API/ folder of the team's documentation
repository.

**Owned the documentation track end-to-end.** The team's
documentation repository (`team-corde`) has 143 commits;
**my 140 (97.9%) make me effectively the sole author.**
The repo contains: day-by-day weekly project work records
(the visible diary of the engagement across five months —
every UI library evaluation, every database-schema
iteration, every employer-email outcome, every offline
meeting decision), the project description and user-stories
archive, the full API documentation set from CORDE, and
seven separate sub-folders enumerating my specific
contributions — business logic diagrams, presentation
decks, database design, UI/UX and front-end implementation,
live demos and screen recordings, APK packaging, and
weekly sprint question sets.

**Practised the soft skills the capstone is designed to
teach.** Translated CORDE's abstract user stories into
concrete Azure DevOps work items; co-designed the database
schema by reconciling two team-developed plans against
CORDE's existing database slices into a unified version;
survived two UI library re-evaluations (Native Base →
React Native Elements → Native Base) and chose Native Base
for its Figma-component-library integration so design and
implementation stayed close; ran the demo cadence in front
of Barry Clark and the CORDE technical team every week
across five months; and made the team's GitHub one-branch
policy work even though it required disciplined sequencing
of two developers' parallel work.

**Kept the codebase modern after the engagement ended.**
Continued making targeted commits in subsequent years for
React Native version currency, Android 16 KB page-size
linker compliance, husky + lint-staged pre-commit hooks,
and dependency upgrades — keeping the repository a usable
portfolio piece rather than an archived snapshot of
mid-2024 React Native conventions.

**The application remains in active use today by CORDE's
field crews** for offline data collection — GPS-tagged
photos and structured form data captured on water-
infrastructure and civil-construction sites where mobile
coverage drops out, then syncing back when crews return
to coverage. The codebase-currency commits aren't archive
maintenance; they're keeping a production tool aligned
with the platform conventions of the year the crews are
actually running it on.
narrative.technicalHighlights:
**React Native 0.83 + TypeScript + Native Base** on the
client, deliberately chosen over Expo because the project's
offline-first requirements pushed against Expo's managed
workflow (and over React Native Elements because Native
Base shipped a Figma component library that let the team
do design-first then implement against the exact
components). The native-only mode also opened the door to
Android-native development where the offline pipeline
needed it.

**SQLite as the system of record for the moment of work.**
Local-first storage of text + photo + GPS coordinates +
timestamps captured in the field, with a synchronisation
pipeline that reconciles back to CORDE's Workbench central
database when connectivity returns. The sync model had to
handle the real conflict cases offline-first systems
actually face — duplicate-key collisions when a worker's
local IDs and the server's IDs both move forward, partial
sync where one log uploads but its attachments don't,
connectivity drop mid-batch, and the file-upload
sub-protocol covered separately in Kullum's API specifications.

**Heavy native-integration surface:** geolocation +
geolocation-service (foreground and background), camera-
roll, image-picker, document-picker, file-system, secure
keychain storage, native maps, react-native-permissions
for the Android permission flow, base64 encoding for the
on-the-wire payloads. The dependency list is twice as long
as a typical RN app because field-data-collection in
Canterbury's rural water-infrastructure context genuinely
touches that much of the device.

**API integration discipline.** Apifox for shared interface
testing across the team; Swagger as the contract source
from CORDE; a `test-sharepoint-connection.js` script as
evidence of a SharePoint-touching corner of CORDE's
document workflow; Azure DevOps as the work-item store
Barry Clark and the CORDE team worked from; per-week
question sets compiled into documents and reviewed before
each offline meeting so CORDE's time was used efficiently.

**The documentation repo is the artefact.** A second
repository (`team-corde`) ran in parallel as the project's
living memory — every weekly meeting decision, every
UI-library evaluation, every database-schema iteration,
every CORDE email exchange's outcome, captured in
version-controlled prose. This is unusually disciplined
documentation for a student-team engagement and is the
practice I carried into every subsequent role.

work.publicSummary:

data-collection mobile app for **CORDE** — a 30+-year-old, 164-staff

(civil construction + regional asset maintenance + water infrastructure;
CCNZ Canterbury Contractor of the Year). Reported directly to Barry

professional engineer that ran directly into the ByteDance + FreePeriod
+ Sanicle CTO sequence.

projects.publicSummary:
An AI financial-wellness coach for Māori communities, built on the
Māori holistic-health model Te Whare Tapa Whā — the cultural framework
lives in the database schema, not as surface styling. Users get voice
coaching and journaling in both te reo Māori and English, guided
through wellbeing check-ins and money goals framed around family,
spiritual purpose, and health rather than numbers alone. Built solo
for an independent research commission from Riria (Missy) Te Kanawa;
a 19-person cohort used it over four months (Oct 2025 – Mar 2026),
producing 181 bilingual journal entries, 35 voice coaching sessions,
and over 500 transcribed coaching messages.

narrative.impactHeadline: A te reo *Māori* AI coach for financial wellbeing, built on the Māori holistic-health model Te Whare Tapa Whā — encoded in the database, not added as decoration. A 19-person research cohort used it over four months: voice coaching plus 181 bilingual journal entries.
narrative.problem:
Mainstream financial apps treat money as decontextualised numbers —
budgets, balances, projections. For many Māori users that framing is
culturally mismatched: money is inseparable from *whānau* wellbeing,
spiritual purpose, and holistic health. Generic budgeting tools strip
out the very framework that makes financial decisions meaningful,
leaving Māori communities — who face disproportionate financial stress
in Aotearoa — choosing between tools that fit their wallet but not
their worldview, or no tool at all. Tam-AI-Ti exists to test a direct
question: can an AI financial coach built natively on Māori cultural
wisdom, rather than retrofitted with cultural decoration, engage a
population that mainstream financial technology systematically
underserves?
narrative.audience:
Māori adults working on their financial wellbeing, served along two
account types: individual goal-setters (Full Account) and *whānau*
supporters who can witness and encourage from the sidelines through a
five-tier permission system (view profile, view goals, view journal,
send messages, receive celebrations). Research cohort to date: 19
verified participants recruited by Missy Te Kanawa, active over a
four-month window (Oct 2025 – Mar 2026), producing 181 bilingual
journal entries, 35 voice coaching sessions, 146 AI coach messages,
and 74 daily Hauora check-ins. Te reo Māori and English are
first-class throughout. Future audience: the broader Māori adult
population — *rangatahi* entering working life, parents managing
*whānau* finances, *kaumātua* passing on financial wisdom — for whom
culturally-rooted tools are scarce.
narrative.productLogic:
The product is architected on Te Whare Tapa Whā, the Māori model of
holistic wellness with four walls (Wairua/spiritual, Tinana/physical,
Hinengaro/mental, Whānau/family) standing on a foundation (Whenua/land).
Every feature traces back to this house.

A user starts with the **26Q assessment over nine named psychological
factors** — `self_belief`, `long_term_focus`, `resilience`,
`self_control`, `growth_mindset`, `financial_confidence`,
`social_comparison`, `mindfulness`, `gratitude` (with `mindfulness`
reverse-coded to capture Present Bias) — mapped to personalised
recommendations. They then select up to three focus areas, choose one
of three account types (Full / Supporter / Both), and proceed to set
financial goals through one of **seven SMART action plans (Options
A-G)**: A Spending Reduction, B Savings Increase, C Savings Rate, D
Debt Reduction, E Income Growth, F Budget Review, G Accountability
Partner. Each plan has its own dedicated React component family
(SpendingTracker / SavingsTracker / DebtCalculator / IncomeTracker /
BudgetReviewCalendar / MeetingsTracker etc. — 38 goal components
total) and its own type-specific tracking fields in the schema,
rather than a generic JSON-note column. A `GoalCardFactory` /
`ProgressTrackerFactory` / `OverviewFactory` pattern dispatches the
right component for each goal's `option` field.

They check in daily across **eleven Hauora dimensions** mapped directly
to the four walls: `sleep_hours`, `water_litres`, `food_quality`,
`exercise_hours` for Tinana; `wairua_time_spent`,
`wairua_human_connection`, `wairua_taia_connection` for Wairua (Whenua
integrated through Taiao/environment connection); `hinengaro_mauri_tau`
(mindfulness), `hinengaro_ako` (learning) for Hinengaro;
`whanau_check_in`, `whanau_quality_time` for Whānau. Each metric is a
typed integer-scale column with a Māori-language identifier — not a
UI label sitting on top of a culturally-neutral schema. They journal
morning intentions (Hauora intentions across all 5 dimensions + 3
gratitude items + financial reflections) and evening reflections
through the Kūmete framework (what you contributed + what you received,
Tahi-Rua-Toru tomorrow goals, emotional check-in), all bilingually in
te reo or English, with real GPT-4o enhancement (4 enhancement types ×
3 intensity levels), translation (macron-correct ā/ē/ī/ō/ū), and
5-dimensional AI insight extraction (Emotions / Themes / Hauora /
Patterns / Growth).

They meet an **AI Financial Coach via gpt-4o-realtime-preview** voice
("verse" voice, server-side VAD + 500ms silence detection) — a
conversation partner that never reduces money to numbers but
consistently frames every financial decision through all four walls
of the Hauora model. Audio is deliberately *not* stored; only text
transcripts persist (a cost optimisation that preserves searchability
without per-minute audio storage). The AI surfaces **74 Whakatauki
proverbs across 37+ categories** by theme via `/api/whakatauki/search`,
references the **Maramataka 30-day lunar calendar with 4 traditional
activity types** — *Toitoi* (spiritual/cultural: karakia, whakapapa,
wānanga), *Hi ika* (water-based: ocean/river fishing, shellfish,
seaweed), *Pouraka* (hunting: bird hunting, forest foraging, game
tracking), *Mahi māra* (gardening/planting) — for cultural timing,
and ties financial decisions back to family and spiritual purpose.
Whānau members can be invited via email-token-based invitations with
a **5-tier permission system** (`canSeeGoals` · `canSeeProgress` ·
`canSeeMood` · `canSeeAchievements` · `canSendMessages`) to encourage
and witness without intruding.
narrative.businessLogic:
Currently a research-pilot product, not yet commercial. Funded directly
by the researcher as an independent contracted build. The theory of
change: if a financial-wellness tool authentically reflects Māori
cultural frameworks — rather than skinning generic financial technology
with cultural decoration — it can drive engagement and outcomes for a
population mainstream products miss.

The hypothesis sits inside Riria (Missy) Te Kanawa's broader body of work
on Māori economic prosperity. She is lead author of KPMG New Zealand's
*Maui Rau* report series on how Māori can advance prosperity for their
people, has spoken at the National Māori Governance Summit, contributes
to The Spinoff, and brings two decades of experience as a Chartered
Accountant and management consultant. Former Partner at KPMG NZ leading
the National Māori Sector; now at ASB Bank (which was not a party to
this commission). Tam-AI-Ti is the working artifact for that thesis —
evidence that culturally-native technology can serve where
culturally-decorated technology cannot.

Paths forward remain open: iwi partnerships, Māori community
organisations, and institutions seeking to authentically serve the
Māori sector.
narrative.technicalApproach:
Solo full-stack build on Next.js 15.5 with Turbopack and TypeScript 5
(100% strict typing across the entire 48-table / 494-column schema).
Drizzle ORM 0.44 over Neon serverless Postgres with pgVector for
1536-dimension `text-embedding-3-small` semantic search across four
embedded tables (`goals.goal_embedding`,
`goal_progress.note_embedding`, `journal_entries.content_embedding`,
`ai_coach_messages.content_embedding`). Authentication via NextAuth
v5 (beta 29) with `@auth/drizzle-adapter` 1.10 — Google OAuth 2.0,
database sessions (30-day duration, not JWT), CSRF protection,
role-based access control across three account types (full / supporter
/ both).

**The schema is deliberately split across 10 module files** —
`users.ts`, `assessments.ts`, `goals.ts`, `daily-engagement.ts`,
`ai-coach.ts`, `whanau.ts`, `maramataka.ts`, `whakatauki.ts`,
`financial-coach.ts`, and `index.ts` — totalling ~924 lines, each
file owning a domain boundary. **22 hand-written SQL migrations
(0000-0021)** track every schema change: 0015 restructured the
Hauora metrics from mixed data types to consistent integer scales
(better alignment with Māori wellness philosophy: removed
`exercise_minutes` / `calm_minutes` / `outside_minutes` and added 7
new Hauora-aligned integer fields); 0020 added the financial coach
tables (`financial_coach_sessions` 8 cols + `financial_coach_messages`
5 cols); 0021 added period-based tracking (`current_period_start`,
`current_period_end`) so Options F (Budget Review) and G
(Accountability Partner) operate as recurring rather than one-shot
goals.

The AI layer is a deliberate **three-model composition** chosen for
cost-and-quality fit:
- **CopilotKit 1.10** drives in-app tool actions on
  `gpt-4o-mini-2024-07-18` at temperature 0.3 — deterministic and
  cheap for tool calls (88+ AI actions defined across 8 hooks /
  1,936 lines).
- **`gpt-4o-2024-11-20`** handles high-quality journal enhancement
  (`enhanceJournalContent`: 4 enhancement types × 3 intensity levels
  at temp 0.6-0.8, max 1500-3000 tokens), bilingual translation
  (`translateJournalContent`: EN ↔ te reo Māori with macron-correct
  ā/ē/ī/ō/ū preservation at temp 0.5, max 2000 tokens), AI insight
  extraction (`extractJournalInsights`: 5-dimensional analysis at
  temp 0.3, structured JSON), reflection-question generation
  (`generateReflectionQuestions`: 5 question types at temp 0.7), and
  semantic similarity search (`findSimilarEntriesAI`).
- **OpenAI Realtime API** (`gpt-4o-realtime-preview`, "verse" voice)
  powers the voice Financial Coach with server-side VAD and 500ms
  silence detection. Voice sessions store text transcripts only — a
  cost optimisation that preserves searchability without per-minute
  audio storage.

**CopilotKit was deliberately optimised from 8 AI-enabled routes
down to 3** (Dashboard with 4 CRUD wellness actions; Journal with
the fill-journal action; Journal History with search + analysis).
Goals / Maramataka / Resources / Onboarding / Auth routes were
intentionally *disabled* for the AI assistant — security on auth,
performance on cultural pages, and cleaner UX on goal management.
That's anti-feature-bloat discipline: removing AI surface area was
treated as a feature.

**Culture is in the schema, not the UI.** Every one of the eleven
Hauora dimensions is a typed column with a Māori-language identifier
(`wairuaTaiaConnection`, `hinengaroMauriTau`, `whanauCheckIn`,
`wairuaTimeSpent`, `whanauQualityTime`, etc.); the seven SMART action
plans are first-class types `a`–`g` with **15 type-specific tracking
fields** in `goal_progress` rather than a generic JSON blob; the 74
Whakatauki proverbs are an indexed, embedded library searchable by
theme through `/api/whakatauki/search?q=...&category=...`. The
Maramataka schema (6 tables, 49+ columns) models the 30-day lunar
cycle, 8 main moon phases, the 4-activity key system, daily energy
levels, and Gregorian-to-Maramataka calendar mapping. **48 tables,
494 columns, 76 API route files, 100+ endpoints across 20 modules,
38 pages, 115+ React components** (44 main + 38 goal-specific + 28
shadcn/ui + 5 cultural). Edge-runtime endpoints on Vercel; Vercel
Blob for avatar storage with `react-easy-crop` 5.5 client-side crop
preview; Redis (ioredis 5.7) for session cache; Resend 6 +
`@react-email/components` for transactional email; `recharts` 3.2
for Hauora trend charts.
narrative.outcomes:
Production-deployed at **tamaiti.whiri-ai.com**, in active operation
since October 2025. Research-cohort engagement (Oct 2025 – Mar 2026,
roughly four months): 19 verified users, 181 bilingual journal
entries, 18 active financial goals, 146 AI coach messages, 35 voice
coaching sessions with over 500 transcribed messages, and 74 daily
Hauora check-ins. Sustained multi-month engagement on a pre-commercial
product with no marketing channel — a meaningful early signal for a
research pilot.

**March 2026 production migration**: a zero-downtime cutover from my
personal Vercel Hobby account to the client's Whiri-AI Pro team
account — GitHub repo transfer, Vercel Blob + KV re-creation, Google
OAuth re-config, custom-domain DNS cutover through Cloudflare,
blob-data migration, Resend sender-domain re-verification, and
`NEXTAUTH_SECRET` preservation so existing user sessions survived.
Documented as a reusable 12-step playbook covering every component and
the rationale for not using Vercel's built-in Project Transfer.

**22 schema migrations shipped** in lock-step with feature work, none
rolled back — the most consequential being 0015 (Hauora-metrics
restructure to consistent integer scales), 0020 (Financial Coach voice
tables) and 0021 (period-based tracking converting Options F/G from
one-shot to recurring goals). React Server Components CVE patches
landed within hours of Vercel/Snyk automated PRs (PR #2, Dec 2025).

Project status: fully built, deployed, in active operation, and
transferred to client operational ownership. It sits within Missy
Te Kanawa's wider published work on Māori economic prosperity — KPMG
NZ *Maui Rau* report series (lead author), Māori Governance Summit
talks, The Spinoff contributing author — as the working artifact for
the thesis that culturally-native technology can serve Māori
communities where culturally-decorated technology cannot.
narrative.myRole:
Solo independent developer with full ownership of the entire stack and
lifecycle. Introduced to the project by Dr Mahsa McCauley (Mohaghegh),
founder of She Sharp (New Zealand's leading women-in-STEM nonprofit) —
Missy approached Mahsa for a developer recommendation and Mahsa referred
me from the She Sharp engineering team, where I serve as Senior Full
Stack Engineer and Website Team Lead.

Engagement model: Missy as client, directing the product structurally
(feature requests, surveys, "include the Maramataka lunar calendar,"
"add a Financial Coach feature") and funding the work directly; I
designed, built, deployed, and maintained the entire system, and ran
the data analysis. Throughout development I sent feature-demo videos at
every milestone so Missy could review progress without needing a
technical background. Post-launch, I produced teaching videos enabling
Missy to use Cursor to do her own database reads and basic data
analysis — transferring practical operational ownership while remaining
the technical lead. **351 commits, solo authorship throughout**
(2025-09-19 → 2026-03-29), including the March 2026 zero-downtime
production migration from my personal Vercel to the client's
Whiri-AI Pro team that I designed, executed, and documented as a
reusable `PROJECT_MIGRATION_GUIDE.md` playbook for future client
engagements.

leading women-in-STEM nonprofit (charitable trust CC57025, 2,200+ members,
8,000+ women supported) — replacing Webflow + spreadsheets with a real member
engine. GPT-4o-mini AI mentor matching (5-weight scoring + admin review),
three role-permissioned dashboards (mentor / mentee / admin), Stripe billing,
and a custom Slack ecosystem (event-collector bot, weekly mentorship-stats
digest, NZ government funding crawler, Claude `sync-event-from-slack` skill)
that turned hours of volunteer manual work into one-command automation.

projects.publicSummary:
Solo-built member platform for She Sharp — New Zealand's leading
women-in-STEM charity (a registered charitable trust). It handles
member sign-ups and billing, runs the events calendar, and matches
mentors with mentees using AI scoring backed by a human reviewer,
replacing a Webflow site and spreadsheets the team had outgrown.

narrative.impactHeadline: The member platform for New Zealand's leading women-in-STEM charity — solo-built over a year, replacing spreadsheets and a drag-and-drop website with one system for member sign-ups, events, and mentor matching that pairs AI scoring with human review.
narrative.problem:
She Sharp is New Zealand's leading women-in-STEM nonprofit — a
registered charitable trust founded by Dr Mahsa Mohaghegh (McCauley),
AI/NLP researcher and Head of Computer and Information Sciences at
AUT, after she experienced first-hand the gap of being part of the
20% minority of women in technology. The community reports 2,200+
registered members, 8,000+ women supported across its history, 50+
corporate partners, and 84+ events since 2014. The original website
was a Webflow build, and Webflow had become the bottleneck: the
capabilities the mission required — membership tiers, mentor / mentee
/ admin role permissions, AI-driven mentor matching, Stripe billing,
Slack-integrated operations, automated event management, recruitment
pipelines, audit logging — are precisely what no drag-and-drop site
builder can express. The leadership team was running a 2,200-member
community through spreadsheets, Slack threads, and manual outreach.
She Sharp needed a real platform.
narrative.audience:
Active members of the She Sharp community: women in technology at
every career stage, from undergraduate students considering STEM
majors through senior engineers serving as mentors. The platform
supports three role-permissioned dashboards (mentor, mentee, admin)
plus dual-role compatibility — a mid-career engineer can
simultaneously mentor juniors and be mentored by a senior, without
special-cased data modelling. Additional audiences served on adjacent
surfaces: Her Waka programme participants (women re-entering the
workforce, served by the dedicated MSD-funded teaching site at
herwaka.shesharp.org.nz), volunteer and ambassador candidates
(screened through the AI recruitment pipeline), the 50+ corporate
partners (sponsor packages tied to event visibility and member
access), and She Sharp's operational team (Slack-integrated
workflows). The organisation reports 1,000+ active members today,
drawn from a registered base of 2,200+ and a lifetime supported
population of 8,000+ women.
narrative.productLogic:
The new platform replaces the old Webflow site with a real member
system anchored on three coexisting dashboards. A user signs up via
email/password (bcrypt) or OAuth (Google + GitHub), then independently
activates any combination of mentor, mentee, or admin roles — duality
is the **schema-level design principle**, not a special case. The
`user_roles` table has one row per (user, role_type) tuple with its
own `is_active`, `verified_at`, and `activation_step` columns, so a
mid-career engineer can simultaneously mentor juniors and be mentored
by a senior with zero special-cased data modelling. The dashboard
layer (`(dashboard)/dashboard/{mentor-profile,mentee-profile,
mentorship,meetings,admin,account}`) reads the active-roles set and
composes role-specific cards into a single unified view.

**The headline feature is the AI mentor-mentee matching engine**,
implemented as a two-stage pipeline in `lib/matching/`. Stage one is
a rule-based pre-filter that rapidly scores candidate pairs on basic
compatibility (skill set intersection, industry overlap, location/
meeting-format compatibility). Stage two passes promising pairs to
GPT-4o-mini at `temperature: 0.3`, `max_tokens: 1000`, with
`response_format: { type: 'json_object' }`, against an explicit
scoring rubric defined in `lib/matching/prompts.ts`:

  - **MBTI Compatibility — 15%** (cognitive function complementarity,
    communication style)
  - **Skill Alignment — 35%** (mentor's expert skills vs mentee's
    learning goals, expertise depth vs learning breadth)
  - **Goal Alignment — 30%** (career trajectory compatibility, mentor's
    ability to guide toward stated goals, shared values)
  - **Industry Match — 10%** (direct + adjacent + transferable industry
    knowledge)
  - **Logistics — 10%** (meeting format, location proximity, time
    availability)

Each call returns a 0-100 overall + per-dimension subscores +
natural-language explanation + recommendation + confidence (high /
medium / low) + matchingFactors (strengths / challenges /
growthOpportunities), Zod-validated against `AIMatchResponseSchema`
before persistence. **Three production-hardness layers wrap the
OpenAI call**: (1) **Upstash Redis cache** keyed by mentor:mentee
pair so repeat scoring is free; (2) **exponential-backoff retries**
(3 attempts, 1000ms × 2^attempt, with rate-limit/timeout
classification); (3) a **deterministic rule-based fallback** with
the same weighted formula that activates when OpenAI is unavailable,
explicitly marking `confidenceLevel: 'low'` and
`recommendation: 'manual review'` so admins know to look closer.
Batch processing caps at 5 concurrent calls with 100ms inter-batch
delay to respect API limits.

Matches above threshold surface to admin (`/api/admin/matching`)
for human approval before pairing — conservative human-in-the-loop
automation, not speculative auto-pairing. A **mentee matching queue**
(`lib/matching/queue-service.ts` + Vercel Cron at
`/api/cron/process-queue`) holds unmatched mentees in priority order
when mentor capacity is full and re-evaluates as slots open.
Meetings are typed (intro / regular / milestone / final), tracked
with action items (JSONB), notes, feedback, and 1-5 ratings.

**The Stripe-integrated membership flow has a novel activation
architecture**: it doesn't instantly grant access. The architecture
is **form → checkout → invitation code → activation**. A prospective
mentee fills the public mentee form (`/api/forms/mentee/public`),
which creates a `mentee_form_submissions` row. They get redirected
to Stripe Checkout. On `checkout.session.completed` webhook, the
system creates a `membership_purchases` record AND generates a
unique `invitation_code` (`createPaymentInvitationCode`) which is
emailed via Resend. The user must redeem the code to activate their
account, guaranteeing email ↔ payment ↔ account linkage and
preventing orphaned accounts. Self-service via Stripe Customer
Portal. Webhook signature verification on every event. Membership
lifecycle transitions update `user_memberships` + `activity_logs`
atomically.

The platform also runs She Sharp's events infrastructure (workshops,
training, networking, social, THRIVE programmes — online, in-person,
or hybrid, with capacity, waitlist, and QR check-in via `/api/qr`),
three membership tiers, a tiered resource library
with download access control + access logs, transactional email via
Resend, AI-screened volunteer recruitment (`lib/recruitment/
ai-screening.ts` against `/api/admin/recruitment/[id]/ai-screen`),
sponsor inquiries with inline forms, and comprehensive audit logging
(JSONB diffs via `deep-diff` on every admin action).

**A custom Slack ecosystem is deeply integrated** through three
coordinated surfaces. (1) **Read-only event-collector bot** with
scoped tokens (`channels:history`, `channels:read`, `groups:history`,
`groups:read`, `files:read`, `users:read`, `pins:read`,
`bookmarks:read`) — no destructive scopes by design. (2) **Three
Vercel Cron jobs**: `weekly-mentorship-stats` posts engagement
digest to `#mentorships` (recently hardened to survive cold Neon
connection pools), `weekly-funding-crawl` runs the NZ government
funding crawler over **6 official sources** (Beehive RSS, data.govt
CKAN, GETS RSS, MBIE HTML, Treasury RSS, women.govt HTML) with
dedup + relevance scoring + keyword filtering, posting a curated
Slack digest, and `process-queue` re-evaluates the matching queue.
(3) **Interactive Slack event handlers** at `/api/slack/events` and
`/api/slack/events/interactive` for in-Slack workflows.

**The most distinctive Slack-side piece** is a Claude agent skill
named `sync-event-from-slack` living at
`.claude/skills/sync-event-from-slack/`. It automates the previously-
manual work of turning a Slack event channel into a published event
page: it reads channel messages, thread replies, pinned items,
bookmarks, speaker `.docx` bios (via `mammoth`), cover posters, and
speaker headshots; downloads images and renames them per convention;
composes a structured `EventV3Schema` JSON entry against a strict
Zod schema; runs `verify-image-paths` as a CI gate; and on approval
commits to git or opens a PR. **The skill defaults to dry-run** —
no file writes, no git, no network side-effects beyond reading
Slack — unless the user explicitly says "go ahead and apply". Event
publication has gone from hours of manual editing to a single
command, with the AI bot's structured output Zod-validated as the
last line of defense before any git write.

The events surface now also carries fully data-driven event pages —
for example the Aotearoa AI Hackathon Festival 2026 page (hosted by
AUT in partnership with She Sharp and the AI Forum NZ), with a judging
panel, schedule, prizes, sponsors, and photo galleries composed from a
single Zod-validated `EventV3Schema` entry rather than hand-built
markup.
narrative.businessLogic:
The platform is the operational substrate that lets each channel
scale without proportional staff load: dashboards, matching,
membership lifecycle, communications, recruitment, and event
management are automated. The organisation's mission scaling — from
the 1,500 active members publicly cited a year ago toward the 2,200+
registered base it reports today — depends directly on this technical
capability.
narrative.technicalApproach:
Next.js 15.6 canary (App Router, Turbopack dev) + React 19.1.2 +
TypeScript 5.8 over Drizzle ORM 0.43 on Neon Postgres (serverless
connection pool), authenticated via NextAuth 5 beta with bcrypt
credentials and Google + GitHub OAuth (via `@auth/drizzle-adapter`),
deployed on Vercel's edge network with documented sub-second TTFB.
Tailwind CSS 4.1 + shadcn/ui + Radix UI (20+ primitives) for the UI;
Framer Motion + Lenis + Three.js/OGL for landing-page polish;
@dnd-kit for admin kanban workflows; Recharts 3 + @number-flow/react
for dashboard analytics.

**The schema runs to 1,411 lines, 30 pgEnums, and 35 pgTables** —
a deliberate type-safety investment. Membership tiers (free / basic /
premium), mentorship-relationship states (pending / active / paused /
completed / rejected), meeting types (intro / regular / milestone /
final), event types (workshop / networking / training / social /
thrive), location types (online / in_person / hybrid), career stages
(undergraduate → postgraduate → early_career → mid_career → senior →
career_transition), MBTI 16 personality types, recruitment stages,
invitation-code redemption states, activity-log action types — every
business concept is a typed enum rather than a string column, so
invalid states are unrepresentable at the database level. **26 SQL
migration files** (0000_soft_the_anarchist → 0027_bitter_fallen_one)
applied via a custom safe-migration wrapper with pre-deployment
snapshots and rollback SQL generation.

**95 API routes** across `app/api/**/route.ts` covering auth (11
routes including OAuth callbacks, password reset, email
verification), admin (21 routes for matching/mentee-mentor reviews/
recruitment/programmes/users/permissions/analytics/zombie-cleanup),
mentorship lifecycle (apply / approve / relationships / meetings),
events (browse / register / my-registrations / QR check-in), forms
(mentee / mentor / volunteer with public-vs-authenticated variants),
Stripe (checkout / donate / webhook), Slack (events / interactive),
cron (process-queue / weekly-funding-crawl / weekly-mentorship-stats),
resources (download with access control), notifications, file
uploads (CV + profile photo via Cloudinary). **53 pages, 160
components.**

**The AI matching engine** combines a deterministic rule-based
pre-filter with GPT-4o-mini for high-potential pairs only. The
`lib/matching/` namespace has clear separation: `prompts.ts` holds
the system rubric and per-pair prompt builder, `openai-service.ts`
owns the LLM call with retry/cache/fallback, `cache.ts` interfaces
Upstash Redis, `queue-service.ts` manages the FIFO/priority queue
for over-capacity scenarios, `service.ts` orchestrates the full
match flow, and `types.ts` defines `AIMatchResponseSchema` (Zod)
that every LLM output must satisfy. Cost stays predictable because
Stage 1 filters out incompatible pairs before they reach the LLM;
outputs stay explainable because the rubric is explicit;
availability stays high because the rule-based fallback uses the
same weighted formula. Admin override is the final gate.

**Slack integration** uses **scoped read-only tokens** by design
(`channels:history`, `channels:read`, `groups:history`,
`groups:read`, `files:read`, `users:read`, `pins:read`,
`bookmarks:read` — never `chat:write` from the collector). The
`sync-event-from-slack` agent skill composes existing CLI tools
(Slack fetch → image download with rename → docx parse via
`mammoth` → JSON validate against `EventV3Schema` Zod → git commit
via `@octokit/rest` or local) into a single intent-driven workflow.
**Strict Zod validation is the last line of defense before any
git write** — an invalid LLM patch is rejected with a Slack-
friendly error, never persisted. The `weekly-funding-crawl` cron
runs `lib/funding/{fetcher,dedup,scoring,keywords}.ts` over 6
government RSS/HTML sources, scores by relevance keywords, dedups
across sources, and posts a curated Slack digest. The recent
`weekly-mentorship-stats` hardening (`84f788f`) is a small but
revealing detail: Neon's serverless pool can return stale
connections to a cold Vercel Cron worker, so the cron now retries
once on the specific `ECONNRESET`/`Connection terminated` error
class before failing the run.

**Image storage on Cloudinary** with on-the-fly transformations;
**transactional email via Resend** with role-specific templates
(mentorship-emails.ts + recruitment-emails.ts + membership
confirmation); **payments via Stripe** with Customer Portal for
self-service membership management; **audit logging** uses
`deep-diff` to capture JSONB before/after on every admin mutation.

The role spans beyond the main platform. Three supporting projects
round out the contribution. **she-sharp-crawl** — a Python crawler
(BeautifulSoup4, Requests) that migrated content from the old
Webflow site: 1,748 images totaling 707 MB across 11 modular JSON
files, covering 85 SheSharp events, 50 Humanitix ticketed events
(with banner images upgraded 500 px → 1,600 px), 15 podcasts, 121
photo galleries, 7 news / press articles, and 118 site pages. Image
deduplication and Wayback Machine fallback for dead links.
**her-waka** — the Mintlify-powered teaching site at
herwaka.shesharp.org.nz for the MSD-funded Her Waka programme; I
authored both the platform and the curriculum (four workshops
covering AI & Future of Work, #IAmRemarkable + Vibe Coding,
Cybersecurity, Personal Branding, plus 8+ self-paced tutorials at
three difficulty tiers). **serpdata-api-test** — a professional SEO
audit on shesharp.org.nz built on Node 22 native fetch over the
SerpData API, running ~20 live Google queries across eight analysis
sections (brand visibility, topical keyword rankings, NZ competitor
matrix, mobile-vs-desktop gap, cross-market reach, SERP feature
inventory) plus a 10-point prioritized recommendations report
(e.g., "SheSharp ranks #4 desktop / not in mobile top 10 for 'women
in tech New Zealand' — high-priority technical SEO gap").
narrative.outcomes:
Production-deployed and operating She Sharp's full member lifecycle
for about a year (first commit 2025-07-22, latest 2026-07-14) — still
in active build-out, not maintenance. Commit volume: **936 commits,
793 (85%) authored solo by Chan Meng**, with 140 (15%) by Lesley Gao
on frontend design and code and 3 by an automation bot. **541 of
those commits landed since 2026-01-01 alone.** The platform now runs
She Sharp's membership, mentorship, events, sponsor relationships,
volunteer recruitment pipeline, and internal team operations across
**95 API routes, 53 pages, 160 components, 26 SQL migrations, and an
11-namespace `lib/` library** (matching, slack-bot, stripe, funding,
programmes, recruitment, invitations, mentorship, email, auth, db) —
the technical infrastructure for an organisation that reports 2,200+
registered members, 1,000+ active, 8,000+ women supported lifetime,
50+ corporate partners, 84+ events since 2014, 18 industry mentors,
and an 85% mentorship success rate.

The most recent build-out extended the public site into a photo-rich
"Editorial" visual system and full event pages — including the
Aotearoa AI Hackathon Festival 2026 page (AUT × She Sharp × AI Forum
NZ, 7–8 August 2026 at AUT City Campus), assembled from a single
Zod-validated event entry. **The Her Waka programme launched in March
2026** with NZ Ministry of Social Development funding, using the
dedicated teaching site I built (herwaka.shesharp.org.nz) and the
curriculum I authored — 4 workshops + 8 tutorials in 3 tiers. **The
SEO audit** identified actionable competitive gaps, notably the
desktop/mobile ranking disparity on "women in tech New Zealand."
**The migration crawler** enabled a clean cutover from Webflow
without losing institutional knowledge. **The Slack-bot + agent-skill
pairing** replaced an hours-long manual event-publication workflow
with a single dry-run-default command.
narrative.myRole:
Senior Full Stack Engineer and Website Team Lead. Recruited directly
by founder Dr Mahsa Mohaghegh (McCauley) — AI/NLP researcher and Head
of Computer and Information Sciences at AUT — specifically to deliver
the AI and full-stack senior engineering that Webflow couldn't, and
that the She Sharp team didn't have in-house.

Solo full-stack ownership of the main platform: product architecture,
database schema (1,411 lines, 30 pgEnums, 35 pgTables, 26 SQL
migrations), AI matching pipeline (5-weight rubric + Upstash Redis
cache + rule-based fallback + admin human-in-the-loop), Stripe
billing with the form→checkout→invitation-code activation flow,
Slack ecosystem (read-only event-collector, 3 weekly Cron digests,
interactive event handlers), the `sync-event-from-slack` Claude
agent skill with strict Zod validation and dry-run default, admin
systems, deployments, observability, and operational support. Lesley
Gao contributed frontend design and frontend code (140 commits, 15%);
the remaining 793 commits (85%) are mine. Beyond the main platform I
also built the data-migration crawler that bootstrapped the launch
(she-sharp-crawl), the Her Waka teaching site (her-waka) and its
complete four-workshop + 8-tutorial curriculum, and the professional
SEO audit and recommendations report (serpdata-api-test) on
shesharp.org.nz — working across engineering, instructional design,
and strategic analysis under one role.

_relatedProjects.tagline: Mintlify documentation platform for the She Sharp × NZ Ministry of Social Development (MSD) employment-readiness programme delivered at academyEX Auckland (Mar-Jun 2026); 4 workshops authored solo by Chan

narrative.impactHeadline: Employment-readiness teaching platform built for the She Sharp × NZ Ministry of Social Development HER WAKA programme at academyEX Auckland — 4 workshops authored solo + 8+ self-paced tutorials across three difficulty tiers.

_relatedProjects.tagline: Industry-partnered AI event assistant for the Aotearoa AI Hackathon Festival — relaunched for 2026 (AUT City Campus) as a single-page voice-first agent: an audio-reactive Three.js orb driving a two-way OpenAI Realtime voice conversation (WebRTC + ephemeral keys) plus gpt-4o-mini text chat, over a Neon knowledge base + transcripts with KV rate-limiting, on Vercel. The 2025 edition (AUT × AI Forum NZ × She Sharp) shipped a 3D sprite mascot with 6-tier affection gamification + a Notion-as-database FAQ on Cloudflare Workers; generalized into an open-source MIT template
techStack: Tailwind CSS v3 + Framer Motion v12
Custom Quantum Orb 3D mascot (CSS 3D transforms + Three.js particles)
Sprite affection gamification engine (gesture detection, mood weighting, time-of-day bias)
Notion REST API (raw fetch, CF Workers compatible — replaced @notionhq/client SDK)
localStorage state persistence (50-msg chat history + sprite affection state)
5-min FAQ client cache + optimistic vote/view updates
AI rate limiting + per-session token budgets + prompt guardrails (2026-07 security hardening)
OpenAI Realtime API over WebRTC — two-way voice (2026 relaunch)
Ephemeral client secrets minted server-side (OPENAI_API_KEY never reaches the browser)
Audio-reactive Three.js orb voice UI + live captions (2026)
_relatedProjects.publicSummary:
Real industry-partnered AI event assistant built for the **AI
Hackathon Festival 2025** (Auckland, Aug 15–16, 2025), co-organised
by **Auckland University of Technology**, **AI Forum New Zealand**,
and **She Sharp**, with **UN Sustainable Development Goals** as
the innovation brief. The platform serves participants and mentors
through three interlocking systems: (1) an **interactive 3D sprite
mascot** with a 6-tier "Stranger → Soulmate" affection system,
7-state dynamic mood engine, 17 emotion categories, gesture
recognition (circle/pat/swipe), negative-behaviour penalties,
streak bonuses, and time-of-day emotion biases — a fully gamified
personality layer that responds to how users actually touch the
page; (2) a **Notion-as-database community FAQ** with up/downvote
ranking, view tracking, 5-minute client cache, and graceful static
fallback when Notion is unreachable; (3) an **OpenAI GPT-4o-mini
streaming chat** with markdown-rendered responses and one-click
FAQ-to-chat hand-off. After the live deployment, the codebase was
generalised into a reusable open-source template
(`event-qa-ai-template` on GitHub, MIT) so any future hackathon /
conference can fork-and-config the same stack. Chan was
subsequently recognised among the **14 expert mentors** at the
festival.

For the **2026 festival** (Aotearoa AI Hackathon Festival 2026,
AUT City Campus) the project was **rebuilt from the ground up** as
a single-page **voice-first** agent: the whole site is a centered,
audio-reactive Three.js orb that starts a **two-way voice
conversation over the OpenAI Realtime API via WebRTC** (the client
exchanges SDP directly with OpenAI using a short-lived ephemeral
secret, so the real API key never reaches the browser), with a
text-input fallback answered by gpt-4o-mini via the Vercel AI SDK,
live captions, and a collapsible event-info panel. Agent
instructions build from a Neon `knowledge` table (graceful
fallback to config), transcripts persist best-effort, and Upstash
KV rate-limits token minting. Deployed on Vercel
(`she-sharp1/aihackathon-2026`, live at aihackathon-2026.vercel.app).

narrative.impactHeadline: Voice-first AI event agent for the Aotearoa AI Hackathon Festival — relaunched 2026 (AUT) as an audio-reactive orb driving two-way OpenAI Realtime voice + gpt-4o-mini text chat; the 2025 edition served 11 teams / 80+ participants with a gamified 3D sprite mascot + Notion FAQ.
narrative.problem:
Hackathons and conferences generate the same Q&A surface every
year: participants ask the same 30 questions ("how do I form a
team", "what counts as AI", "when do I have to be on site",
"what's the prize structure", "where do I find a mentor"), and
organisers answer them via static PDFs that go stale the moment
the schedule shifts. Bolt-on chatbots exist but they treat
humans as passive consumers — a popup widget in the corner, a
knowledge base scraped at build time, no memory of what the
community itself finds helpful. Three structural gaps stack up:
(1) the FAQ is **authored once** rather than **curated by the
community**, so quality drifts; (2) the AI is a generic
wrapper around a chat API, with no event-specific personality
or theme tie-in; (3) organisers cannot update content without
a code redeploy, so the platform calcifies the moment the
original developer hands it off. The AI Hackathon Festival
2025 organisers needed a platform that solved all three on the
timeline of a real event with 80+ participants and three
co-organising institutions reviewing every word on screen.
narrative.audience:
Direct users were the **80+ participants and 14 expert mentors**
of AI Hackathon Festival 2025 — students from Auckland
universities, **Westlake Girls High School** secondary students,
and AI Forum NZ industry mentors all asking the same FAQ-style
questions across a 26-hour residential event. Secondary
audience: the **three co-organising institutions** (AUT, AI
Forum NZ, She Sharp) who use the platform as a public-facing
artefact of their joint programme. Tertiary audience: future
hackathon and conference organisers in NZ and abroad who can
fork the open-source template (`event-qa-ai-template`) and
deploy their own event Q&A AI in under an hour by editing four
configuration files — `site.config.ts`, `ai.config.ts`,
`content.config.ts`, `branding.config.ts` — without touching
component code.
narrative.productLogic:
The product is structured as three layers that compose
independently. Layer one is the **3D welcoming surface**: the
landing page renders a particle sphere with a "WELCOME TO AI
HACKATHON!" greeting set against a `CosmicBackground` Three.js
scene, with `Calendar` / `MapPin` event-info widgets, sponsor
logos for the three co-organisers, and a glassmorphism Google
Maps modal showing AUT WG Building. Layer two is the **chat
page** — a 70/30 desktop split between a full-screen OpenAI
chat (with `react-markdown` + `rehype-highlight` for
code-block syntax highlighting) and a community FAQ sidebar.
Layer three — the differentiator — is the **interactive sprite
mascot** that lives alongside the chat: a CSS-3D **Quantum
Singularity Orb** (accretion disk + photon ring + event
horizon + energy jets, all built from layered divs with
`transform-style: preserve-3d` and `rotateX(60deg)`) that
becomes a fully gamified personality. The user can **circle**,
**swipe vertically/horizontally**, **pat**, or **click** the
orb; the gesture detection engine tracks each interaction and
feeds it through an affection/mood scoring system. Five
gestures grant affection (circle x5 = +10 affection / "Wheee~!
So dizzy but fun!"), five negative behaviours penalise it
(`roughMovement`, `spamClick`, `suddenLeave`, `longIgnore`,
`excessiveCircle`), and the resulting state drives which of
17 emotions the sprite expresses — including three **rare**
emotions (`devoted`, `starry`, `mischievous`) only unlocked
at the top affection tiers. The result: users do not just chat
with an AI, they **build a relationship with a tiny digital
creature** while doing so — a memorable, shareable layer that
turns a Q&A bot into a piece of event identity.
narrative.businessLogic:
Three intertwined value propositions. For the **organising
institutions**, the platform is a public-facing demonstration
artefact of the joint AUT × AI Forum NZ × She Sharp programme,
running on Cloudflare Workers (free tier covers an event-week
traffic spike with zero cost) with a Notion database that
**non-developer event staff can edit live** during the event —
adding new FAQ entries through the Notion UI, watching them
appear within the 5-minute client cache window. For **future
event organisers**, after the live event, the codebase was
deliberately generalised into a **reusable MIT template**
(`event-qa-ai-template`) with all event-specific content
extracted into `config/*.ts` files and full setup
documentation, so the same stack ships any hackathon /
conference / community event Q&A platform in under an hour of
configuration. For **Chan**, the project established a
verifiable, AI-Forum-NZ-endorsed track record of shipping
commercial-grade AI infrastructure for an industry partner —
a credential cited in the public LinkedIn announcement of the
14-mentor cohort and in the Engram Architect Cohort
Engagement Letter narrative.
narrative.technicalApproach:
**AI layer.** OpenAI GPT-4o-mini-2024-07-18 served through
Vercel AI SDK v4.3's `streamText` with `convertToCoreMessages`,
returning `toDataStreamResponse()` for character-by-character
SSE streaming. The system prompt is **generated from
siteConfig at runtime** so any organiser fork inherits an
event-specific prompt without code edits — it embeds event
name, dates, venue, theme (UN SDGs), team-formation rules
(3-6 members, Hacker/Hipster/Hustler composition), schedule
(Friday 8 PM venue close, Discord for mentor requests), and
prize tiers ($250 venue, $1,000 national, $5,000 national
Agentic AI winner) plus Markdown formatting guidelines. The
codebase pivoted from Google Gemini 2.5 Flash to OpenAI
GPT-4o-mini mid-build (commit `5eb3e70`) for cost + Cloudflare
Workers compatibility reasons; the system prompt generator
remained provider-agnostic. **3D sprite gamification.** A
~960-line `sprite-affection.config.ts` declaratively encodes
the entire gameplay model: 5 gestures, 5 negative behaviours,
7 mood states (`ecstatic` 80–100 → `upset` -100 to -80),
17 emotions with per-emotion ear-colour RGB, glow-colour,
particle-colour, animation type (`bounce` / `shake` / `pulse`
/ `spin` / `wobble` / `tremble` / `droop`) and eye shape
(`sphere` / `arc` / `star` / `heart` / `crescent` / `spiral`
/ `teardrop`), 6 affection tiers (Stranger 0–9 → Acquaintance
10–29 → Friend 30–59 → Good Friend 60–99 → Best Friend
100–149 → Soulmate 150+), 7 time-of-day biases (late-night
boosts `sleepy` x3, evening boosts `love` x1.3), and 5 streak
bonus levels (1/3/7/14/30 days, 30-day streak unlocks the
`devoted` rare emotion). Two relational systems link mood
and affection: a **mood multiplier** scales affection gains
from 0.5x (depressed) to 1.5x (euphoric), and **affection
mood protection** sets a floor that rises with tier (Soulmate
gets a -10 floor + 5 mood/min recovery toward +20). All state
persists to `localStorage` under `sprite-affection-data`.
The Quantum Orb itself is rendered with **CSS-only 3D**
(preserve-3d transforms + rotateX) + Three.js particle
accents — no GLB / heavy 3D pipeline — so the bundle stays
small enough for Cloudflare Workers. **Notion backend.** A
non-obvious engineering call: the project initially used
`@notionhq/client`, but migrating to Cloudflare Workers
(commit `5b1dc7e`) broke the Node-dependent SDK; the fix
(commit `20eaf2e`) replaced it with **direct REST calls
against `api.notion.com/v1` using native `fetch`** — version
header `Notion-Version: 2022-06-28`, filter by
`IsActive` checkbox, parse `Title` / `RichText` / `Select` /
`Number` properties via small typed getters. Four API routes
— `/api/chat`, `/api/faq/questions`, `/api/faq/vote`,
`/api/faq/view` — wrap the same client. **Deployment.**
Migrated from Vercel to Cloudflare Workers using
`@opennextjs/cloudflare` v1.3 + wrangler v4; the entire stack
now ships on Cloudflare's free tier including the AI streaming
endpoint. The build command `opennextjs-cloudflare build &&
opennextjs-cloudflare deploy` handles the Next.js → Workers
adapter step.
narrative.outcomes:
Delivered live for the **AI Hackathon Festival 2025** at AUT
City Campus WG Building, **15–16 August 2025**. Used by **11
teams and 80+ participants** during the residential event —
AI Forum NZ's public LinkedIn cohort announcement (with full
Westlake Girls High School student attendance) cites Chan as
**1 of 14 expert mentors**, recognised at the event. The
teams shipped solutions across the UN SDG brief — food
insecurity, digital accessibility, workforce upskilling, and
indigenous environmental stewardship — with national-tier
prizes flowing through the AI Forum NZ pipeline (AI Summit
entry worth $1,499, $1,000 national winner, $5,000 national
Agentic AI winner, ArcGIS licences). Post-event, the codebase
was **refactored into a reusable MIT template** (commit
`948bdf0` "transform project into reusable event Q&A
template"), documented in `docs/TEMPLATE-SETUP.md`,
`docs/NOTION-INTEGRATION.md`, and `docs/UI-DESIGN-SYSTEM.md`,
with the live deployment retained as the canonical worked
example. Listed alongside Chan's other commercial-grade AI
platforms (echook, vitex, chinese-redefine,
ai-programming-teaching-project) as portfolio evidence of
end-to-end industry-partner delivery — design, AI integration,
gameplay engineering, database, deployment, and post-delivery
productisation as a reusable open-source artefact.
narrative.myRole:
Sole developer end-to-end. Owned the discovery phase with the
three co-organising institutions, designed the dual-layer
product (chat surface + gamified mascot + Notion FAQ), built
every component, authored both the Gemini and the OpenAI
provider implementations, designed the entire ~960-line
affection / mood / emotion gameplay schema, engineered the
CSS-3D Quantum Orb visualisation, migrated the deployment
from Vercel to Cloudflare Workers when free-tier economics
and Notion-SDK incompatibility forced the move, refactored
the codebase into a reusable open-source template after the
live event, and authored the three setup-documentation files.
On the mentorship side: served as one of the 14 expert
mentors during the 26-hour event itself, guiding teams on
prompt design, SDG framing, and product narrative —
simultaneously building the platform and using it.

projects.publicSummary:
GAVIGO IRE is the founding-engineer MVP for GAVIGO Inc., a Delaware
startup (founder & CEO Saba Gecgil) building a way past the app-install
step: scroll a phone feed and tap a game or short video that starts
playing instantly, with no download and no loading screen. Behind the
feed, an AI scheduler predicts the next tap and pre-warms that content
on a Kubernetes cluster, so a chosen game resumes in under a
millisecond and, in benchmarks, was already warm 84.6% of the time.
Built as the founding-engineer platform — the activation and
AI-orchestration systems that make instant play work — deployed to
production on Google Cloud after a live migration from DigitalOcean.

narrative.impactHeadline: Founding-engineer build for GAVIGO, a Delaware startup: tap a game or short video in a phone feed and it plays instantly — no app install, no loading wait. An AI scheduler pre-warms content on Kubernetes, so a tapped game resumes in under 1 ms, already warm 84.6% of the time.
narrative.problem:
The native-app paradigm puts a friction tax on every interactive
digital experience: discover an app → app-store landing page → install
→ onboard → first interaction. By the time the user reaches the thing
they actually wanted, the moment has passed. For games, video, and
AI experiences — where the feeling of instantaneity drives engagement
— the install gap is the single largest leak in the funnel. GAVIGO's
thesis, in its own marketing language: we are building the
"Post-App World," eliminating the friction between user intent and
digital experience through proprietary "Instant Reality Switching"
technology.
narrative.audience:
Two surfaces run in production today. **Consumer surface**: end users
on ire.gavigo.com and the mobile build scroll a TikTok-style vertical
feed of 23 self-hosted games across four in-house studios (Arcade
Zone, Puzzle Lab, Reflex Arena, ML Lab) plus a bundled video catalog
— every game served from one always-warm pod, no external
game-portal iframes. **Investor / operator surface**: the React
dashboard at the same domain shows the orchestrator's AI decisions,
the container state machine (COLD → PREPARING → READY → ACTIVE →
REUSABLE), warm-pool budget, activation-quality metrics, and a
side-by-side iframe of the mobile app — purpose-built for live demos
to investors and partner evaluators. A Phase 9 audience-segmentation
feature filters visibility per role (public / investor / operator
presets) at WebSocket broadcast time, and a Phase 11 partner-pilot
layer adds per-session telemetry and an operator-gated proof report
for named-partner trials. The ultimate addressable surface is any
market where the feeling of instantaneity drives engagement — mobile
games, short-form video, AI chat, generative content.
narrative.productLogic:
A user scrolls a vertical feed; the orchestrator watches focus events
stream over a WebSocket hub, scores each candidate piece of content
on three weighted signals (personal attention 0.4, global attention
0.4, trend / virality 0.2), and when the combined score crosses a 0.6
threshold, fires one of seven AI triggers — Swarm Boost, Proactive
Warm, Cross-Domain Inject, Mode Change, Lookahead Warm, Initial Warm,
Checkpoint Restore — each of which patches Kubernetes deployment
replicas through the K8s client-go API to scale a game container to
READY before the user taps.

When the user taps, the experience is already live — either served
instantly from a single always-warm shared game-server pod (the
~12 ms "cold" path here is orchestrator overhead, not a real
container start) or restored from the Reusable pool of
recently-active containers (<1 ms restore path). State survives
across scrolls via a postMessage protocol on web and MMKV storage on
native; both reconcile against Redis as a single source of truth
through a deterministic five-state Activation Spine (COLD → READY →
ACTIVE → REUSABLE → restore). When mobile gaming meets the install
gap, the GAVIGO answer is: there is no install gap.
narrative.businessLogic:
GAVIGO Inc. is a Delaware-incorporated C-Corp at the pre-Series A
stage, founder Saba Gecgil. The product wedge is mobile gaming
(public tagline: "Skip the Store. Start the Play") — but the
underlying activation engine is platform-shaped: any interactive
content category where the install gap or cold-start latency is
the dominant funnel leak. The defensibility is architectural,
not feature-level: a proprietary Activation Spine plus a measured
sub-millisecond restore path is the kind of capability that
compounds rather than decays. GAVIGO frames its architecture
publicly as "Two Pillars": the Technology Platform (CTO-led
foundation) and the Intelligence Layer (the autonomous predictive
engine that makes Instant Reality possible). I own the Intelligence
Layer side of that architecture under the title Founding Principal
Engineer, Activation, Execution & AI Systems.
narrative.technicalApproach:
The orchestrator is a single Go binary (gorilla/websocket hub +
Scorer + Rules Engine + K8s client-go patcher) deployed to a managed
Kubernetes cluster. Companion pods: a React + Vite dashboard, a React
Native / Expo mobile-web build served from an nginx pod, a single
always-warm nginx game-server pod baking the 25-entry catalog (23 web
games across four studios plus 2 ML/compute workloads) for zero
per-game startup latency, an AI-service pod (Node + Express calling
OpenAI `gpt-5.4-mini`, with a simulated-response fallback when no key
is set) that scales 0→1, Redis 7 (allkeys-LRU eviction, 400 MB
budget) for warm-pool state and activation metrics, and optional
PostgreSQL 16 / Supabase for social data.

**Cross-cloud production experience: DigitalOcean and Google Cloud.**
The platform shipped first on **DigitalOcean Kubernetes (DOKS) in
Singapore (sgp1)** — DO Container Registry, REGIONAL LoadBalancer,
Let's Encrypt with manual certbot renewal — chosen for cost-efficient
prototype-to-production iteration. When GAVIGO's Google Cloud
partnership came into effect, I led the full migration to **GKE
Standard Zonal in Belgium (europe-west1-b)** on 2026-03-11: Artifact
Registry replacing DO Container Registry, GCE Ingress + Global
HTTP(S) LB with Google-managed certs (auto-renewal replacing 80-day
certbot toil), a FrontendConfig HTTPS redirect, a BackendConfig
`timeoutSec: 86400` to keep activation WebSocket sessions alive,
service types reshaped from LoadBalancer-per-frontend to
ClusterIP-behind-Ingress, and a GCP global static IP. All five
deployments migrated with `strategy: Recreate` to avoid CPU deadlock
on single-node clusters; total downtime was **~30 minutes**. GCP
remains the only production cloud; a separate AWS account (funded by
NVIDIA Inception AWS Activate credits, with a live GPU **spot** quota)
runs nothing in production and is reserved for short, torn-down
inference/runtime experiments.

Hard problems solved. **Sub-millisecond restore** through a
deterministic five-state Activation Spine with atomic Redis
transitions and phase-by-phase timestamps that prove the decision →
K8s patch → client-observation chain. **Warm-pool LRU without cascade
eviction** via ProtectSlot reservations. **Cross-platform state sync**
between MMKV (native), localStorage / postMessage (web), and Redis
(server) with a typed protocol bridging iframe ↔ WebView. **Async K8s
coordination with real-time telemetry** — EndpointSlice / Pod-status
watches feed `activation_ready` broadcasts with millisecond-precision
timestamps. **Audience-aware broadcast filtering** (Phase 9) so public
/ investor / operator viewers each see a different slice of the
catalog. **Partner-pilot telemetry and CI discipline** (Phase 11):
per-session records joining WebSocket session ↔ user ↔ device ↔
activation count, typed failure classes, time-to-playable p50/p95, an
operator-gated proof report, and the project's first CI pipeline (`go
build/vet/test -race`, `vite build`, `tsc`). Benchmarks run 20
iterations per path on real K8s, validating <1 ms restore p50 against
100 concurrent WebSocket clients.
narrative.outcomes:
Production-deployed at ire.gavigo.com. First shipped on DigitalOcean
DOKS (Singapore), then migrated to Google Cloud GKE (Belgium) on
2026-03-11 in a clean ~30-minute cutover when GAVIGO's Google Cloud
partnership came into effect — production-grade expertise demonstrated
on both clouds. **445 total commits (439 non-merge); I authored 426 of
them (~97%) across my two git identities**, with a second engineer
onboarded in June 2026 and the remainder from the CI bot. Real
measured platform metrics post-migration: restore path <1 ms p50,
~12 ms p50 on the orchestrator "cold" path (a genuine 0→1 container
start measures ~18 s and is a deliberate Phase 6+ boundary, not the
demo path), 84.6% warm-pool hit rate, 0 errors at 100 concurrent
WebSocket clients. Catalog: 23 self-hosted games across four studios
plus 2 ML/compute workloads and a 197-video bundle. Phase 9
audience-segmentation and Phase 11 partner-pilot telemetry are live.
Pre-Series A; the product is the working artifact for upcoming
investor conversations. Role formalized over three contract iterations
(Original Oct 17 2025; Amended Feb 9 2026; Second Amended Apr 16
2026), each escalating recognition of my technical ownership.

**External validation.** GAVIGO holds NVIDIA Inception membership —
evidenced by a $10,000 AWS Activate credit issued through the NVIDIA
Inception path (active 2026-07-01, running to 2028-07-31). GAVIGO also
states acceptance into the **Google for Startups Cloud Program** (the
partnership that unlocked the GCP migration executed on March 11) and
was profiled by tech-business press (feedvoice.com, 2026-02-10) — both
are company/external claims a reviewer should confirm with the team
before citing.
narrative.myRole:
Founding Principal Engineer, Activation, Execution & AI Systems
(prior: Core Engineer), reporting to founder & CEO Saba Gecgil.
Joined Oct 17 2025; current title and scope formalized by the
Second Amended and Restated Consulting Agreement effective
Apr 16 2026.

The contract defines a "Protected Technical Domain" that anchors
my role: activation systems, activation flow and execution
behavior, the activation-economics proof path, AI-assisted
orchestration and execution-control systems, restore and reuse
boundary work, heavier-workload validation and transition work,
and the core activation, execution, and AI-enabled systems
architecture of GAVIGO's interactive workload platform — plus
the natural forward extension of that area as the system evolves.
Recognized in the contract record as "a core long-term technical
builder of the Company's interactive workload platform" with
founder-level equity participation. I authored 426 of the project's
439 commits (~97%); a second engineer joined in mid-2026, so the
codebase is no longer solely mine, but activation, orchestration,
and AI-systems ownership remains my defined domain.

_relatedProjects.publicSummary:
GAVIGO Inc.'s public marketing and investor-readiness website at
gavigo.com — the company's standalone brand surface, deliberately
decoupled from the IRE product runtime (ire.gavigo.com) so marketing
copy and investor messaging can change without redeploying the product.
Chan built and maintains it solo (66 of 66 commits): a Next.js site with
a Markdown/MDX content pipeline (so non-engineers can edit copy),
Motion-driven brand animation, Resend + React Email lead capture backed
by Neon Postgres, and a Google Cloud Run production deploy — including
the investor-readiness polish pass that supported GAVIGO's fundraising
and partner conversations.

narrative.impactHeadline: GAVIGO's company + investor site (gavigo.com) — a standalone brand surface decoupled from the IRE product runtime it links to, with a Markdown content pipeline + Resend capture on Cloud Run. Solo-built (66/66 commits, 100%) with an investor-readiness polish pass for fundraising.
narrative.problem:
A startup's product and its story move at different speeds. GAVIGO's
IRE platform is a fast-changing, latency-critical runtime; its public
company narrative — the pitch to investors, partners, and early
users — needs to change even faster, and by people who aren't
shipping the product. Running the marketing site inside the product
codebase couples the two: a copy tweak risks the runtime, and a
non-engineer can't touch either. GAVIGO needed a company surface that
is polished, investor-ready, editable without engineering, and
completely decoupled from the product it represents.
narrative.audience:
Primarily investors and strategic partners evaluating GAVIGO during
fundraising and business-development conversations — the site is the
first credible impression and the bridge to the live IRE demo. Second,
prospective early users and press arriving at gavigo.com who need to
understand "Skip the Store. Start the Play" and reach the product in
one click. Third, GAVIGO's own non-technical team, who edit copy and
read captured leads without asking an engineer.
narrative.productLogic:
The site is deliberately a **separate surface from the product**. It
presents GAVIGO's positioning and brand, animates key moments with
Motion for a premium feel, and routes every call-to-action to the live
IRE demo (ire.gavigo.com/mobile) so the story hands off directly to
the working product. Content is authored in **Markdown/MDX** through a
remark + rehype + gray-matter pipeline, so marketing copy, announcement
posts, and page sections are editable as plain files without touching
React — the whole point being that the company narrative can move
without an engineer in the loop. A **Resend + React Email** capture
flow collects interested-party leads into Neon Postgres, turning the
site from a brochure into a working top-of-funnel instrument for
investor and partner outreach.
narrative.businessLogic:
This is founding-team company infrastructure, not a revenue product.
Its job is fundraising and credibility: a standalone, polished,
fast-to-update investor-readiness surface that GAVIGO (a Delaware
C-Corp, NVIDIA Inception + Google for Startups member) can point
investors and partners to, that always reflects the current pitch, and
that funnels interest straight to the live IRE demo and into a captured
lead list. Decoupling it from the product runtime is the strategic
choice — the company can iterate its story continuously while the
latency-critical IRE platform ships on its own cadence.
narrative.technicalApproach:
A Next.js + React + TypeScript site deployed as a container on
**Google Cloud Run** (europe-west1) — deliberately separate hosting
from the IRE platform. Copy and content run through a
**Markdown/MDX pipeline** (remark + rehype + gray-matter) so pages are
file-editable; **Motion** drives brand animation; **Tailwind CSS**
carries the design system. Lead capture is a **Resend + React Email**
flow persisting to **Neon Postgres via Drizzle ORM**. The build is
small and focused by design — the engineering value is in a clean,
maintainable, non-engineer-editable company surface with a real
capture backend, shipped and operated solo.
narrative.outcomes:
Live in production at gavigo.com on Google Cloud Run, built and
maintained entirely by Chan — **66 of 66 commits, the repository's
sole contributor** — including the investor-readiness polish pass that
supported GAVIGO's fundraising and partner conversations. It stands as
the company's public front door and the hand-off point into the live
IRE product demo, decoupled from and shipping independently of the
product runtime.
narrative.myRole:
Sole developer and maintainer of the site end-to-end — the Next.js
build, the Markdown/MDX content pipeline, the Motion brand animation,
the Resend + Neon lead-capture backend, and the Google Cloud Run
deployment — as GAVIGO's founding principal engineer (the same role
under which I lead the flagship IRE platform). 66 of 66 commits are
mine.
