projects.publicSummary:
An early Model Context Protocol server — the open standard Anthropic
launched in November 2024 to connect AI assistants to outside tools.
Its first commit landed 35 days after that launch. It lets assistants
like Claude, Cursor, and Cline search live Google News through SerpAPI,
automatically sorted into topics (technology, business, science,
health) and offered in 10 languages. Published on npm as
`@chanmeng666/google-news-server`, it carries a PulseMCP "Top Pick"
badge and ships a one-click install bundle for Claude Desktop.

narrative.impactHeadline: Lets AI assistants like Claude search live Google News — sorted into topics, in 10 languages. One of the first tools built for Anthropic's Model Context Protocol: shipped 35 days after the standard launched, and it's now listed across 15+ MCP directories.
narrative.problem:
Anthropic published the Model Context Protocol in November 2024 — the
open standard that lets AI assistants like Claude reach outside their
training data to real tools and live sources. In the first weeks after
launch the ecosystem had only a handful of reference servers, and none
of them let an assistant search current news. That was a concrete gap:
people were already asking their AI assistant "what's happening with X
today," a question no model can answer from training data alone, and
there was no Google News option in the MCP catalog.

A second friction appeared at the same time: Windows users running Node
Version Manager couldn't get *any* npm-based MCP server to connect to
Claude Desktop, because PATH resolution through NVM's shim layer broke
the desktop client's ability to launch the server process. That blocked
a large share of early Windows adopters from using MCP at all.
narrative.audience:
AI assistants and the developers wiring them up. Direct consumers:
Claude Desktop, Cursor's agent mode, Cline, Continue, and any
MCP-compliant client whose setup includes news search. Downstream: the
people asking those assistants about current events — analysts and
journalists monitoring coverage, developers building research and
content agents. The project is deliberately listed on multiple MCP
registries because that is how AI developers actually find servers —
they browse catalogs, not GitHub search.
narrative.productLogic:
A user asks their AI assistant something like "what's happening with
[topic] in [region] this week." The assistant calls the server's one
tool, `google_news_search`, with a query plus optional `gl` (country)
and `hl` (language) codes. The server passes the request to SerpAPI's
Google News engine, gets structured results back, sorts them into
topics (AI & Technology, Business, Science & Research, Healthcare,
General) with lightweight pattern matching, and returns clean output
with each article's source, date, snippet, and link intact. It also
supports search by news topic, publisher, full-story coverage, and
section via token parameters, and falls back to English for
unsupported language codes.

The server runs over MCP's stdio channel — the default for desktop AI
clients — and installs in one command through Smithery's CLI (`npx -y
@smithery/cli install @chanmeng666/google-news-server --client
claude`), or via a packaged `.mcpb` bundle for one-click setup in
Claude Desktop.
narrative.businessLogic:
Open source, MIT licensed, no direct monetization. The intent is
ecosystem positioning: be a canonical Google News server during the
formative months of the Model Context Protocol, so the project keeps
surfacing wherever a developer looks for an MCP tool. Distribution
across many registries (Smithery, mcp.so, Glama, PulseMCP, Awesome MCP
Servers, Archestra, and others) is the discovery strategy. The README
also carries JSON-LD `SoftwareApplication` metadata and an `llms.txt`
guide so that AI agents themselves can read the server's identity,
parameters, and use cases without parsing marketing prose — the same
discovery-by-machine approach used across the rest of this portfolio.
narrative.technicalApproach:
TypeScript on Node.js, built against `@modelcontextprotocol/sdk` v1.x,
shipped as an ES-module CLI binary (`google-news-server`) declared in
`package.json`. Transport is stdio, so the server runs as a subprocess
the desktop AI client launches. HTTP calls go through axios; SerpAPI is
the underlying data source (2 requests/second, 5 concurrent, 1-hour
cache). On top of the raw API the server adds a thin intelligence
layer: parameter validation, topic categorization, multi-language
formatting, and error handling with clear messages.

The repo also ships production packaging — a Dockerfile, a Smithery
config, a `manifest.json`, a JSON-LD `structured-data.json`, an
`llms.txt`, and a downloadable `.mcpb` install bundle — plus a full
set of community-health docs (CONTRIBUTING, SECURITY, SUPPORT,
GOVERNANCE, CHANGELOG, issue/PR templates) added in 2026.

The third contribution is community, not code: **issue #76** in the
official `modelcontextprotocol/servers` repo (opened Nov 27 2024, two
days after MCP's launch) documented the Windows + NVM PATH failure that
stopped Claude Desktop from launching npm-based MCP servers. The fix —
point the client config at the absolute path of `node.exe` instead of
relying on PATH — is cited as the primary troubleshooting step in this
server's README.
narrative.outcomes:
The server is published on npm as `@chanmeng666/google-news-server`
and listed across multiple MCP discovery surfaces, with third-party
recognition:
- PulseMCP "Top Pick" badge *(external claim — needs registry check)*
- Glama A-grade license rating *(external claim — needs registry check)*
- Smithery one-command install, plus listings on mcp.so, Awesome MCP
  Servers, Archestra, and other catalogs *(exact catalog count is
  external — needs registry check)*
- Independent deep-dive write-ups by third-party reviewers (Skywork
  AI, AgentSkillsHub) *(external claim — needs registry check)*
- A hosted deployment listed on Fronteir AI *(external claim — needs
  registry check)*

Alongside it, the companion `server-google-jobs` (first commit late
December 2024) applies the same pattern to Google Jobs search
*(Jobs-repo specifics not verifiable from the News clone — see
flags)*. Issue #76 remains visible in the official MCP servers
repository as a record of an early ecosystem contribution.
narrative.myRole:
I built and maintain the server solo — the code, the README, the npm
publish, the registry submissions, the packaging, and the
issue-tracker responses. I also filed the early issue #76
troubleshooting contribution. Together these make the
early-MCP-developer story concrete: not a theoretical early adopter,
but someone who shipped, distributed, documented, and supported a
working MCP server within the protocol's first month.

projects.publicSummary:
Show your AI-generated Suno music as animated, always-current
cards on your GitHub profile, portfolio, or docs. Paste one
line for a single song, or add a scheduled GitHub Action that
keeps a whole catalog in sync — new tracks, play counts, and
likes update on their own. 12 built-in card styles, correct
rendering of Chinese/Japanese/Korean titles, and full
English/Chinese/Japanese localization, from an MIT-licensed
pnpm monorepo running on Next.js 15.1 and Vercel.

narrative.impactHeadline: Turns any Suno creator's AI-generated songs into animated cards for their GitHub profile — kept up to date automatically. 12 built-in styles, correct display of Chinese/Japanese/Korean titles, and a one-line setup that syncs a whole catalog.
narrative.problem:
Suno AI creators have no graceful way to surface their music
where developer audiences actually gather: GitHub READMEs,
portfolio sites, technical blog footers, project docs. The
three workarounds all fail. Linking out to suno.com loses the
visual hook and breaks if Suno changes its URL shapes.
Screenshotting the player goes stale the moment you publish a
new song. Hand-editing Markdown is fine for one song and
unmanageable past three. Worse, many Suno creators are
bilingual and release tracks with Chinese, Japanese, or Korean
titles — and plain SVG `<text>` (the usual way to draw text
inside an embeddable image) never inherited HTML's rich
text-shaping rules, so those titles render incorrectly. The
gap: a service that renders animated, visually rich Suno cards
as embeddable images, shapes CJK text correctly, supports both
zero-setup single embeds and CI-driven auto-syncing grids, and
works both as a browse-and-build website and as a fully
programmatic GitHub Action.
narrative.audience:
Three concentric audiences. (1) Developer-musicians using
Suno who want their GitHub profile README, portfolio, or
project docs to show a live, auto-updating catalog of their
music — including CJK-named tracks. (2) Music creators more
broadly who keep a GitHub-shaped public presence
(developer-relations folks, indie-game developers, bedroom
producers with side projects, researchers releasing music
under handles) and want a polished visual surface for their
Suno output. (3) Engineers reading the project as a worked
example of (a) extracting a clean public repo from a larger
internal monorepo, (b) sharing a single validated parser
across two independent products, and (c) the
SVG-`<foreignObject>`-plus-CSS-keyframes technique for
animated, CJK-safe cards that plain SVG can't produce.
narrative.productLogic:
Two complementary paths, plus stats surfaces. **Path 1 —
Direct Embed (zero setup).** Paste one Markdown line pointing
at the hosted card endpoint:
`[![](https://github-readme-suno-cards.vercel.app/api/card?id=YOUR_SONG_ID)](https://suno.com/song/YOUR_SONG_ID)`.
The `id` accepts three formats — a raw UUID, a full
`suno.com/song/<uuid>` URL, or a `suno.com/s/<shortcode>`
short code — which the parser canonicalises to one internal
ID. The hosted `/builder` lets you preview the 12 base
combinations (2 layouts × 2 presets × 3 themes), flip 11
element toggles, override colors with hex codes, and copy the
finished embed URL. **Path 2 — GitHub Action auto-sync.** Add
`<!-- SUNO-CARDS:START -->` / `<!-- SUNO-CARDS:END -->`
markers to your README and a workflow using
`uses: ChanMeng666/github-readme-suno-cards@v0.1` on a
schedule (typically every 6 hours). The Action reads any of
its 36 inputs — a Suno handle (auto-discovery of every public
song), a YAML manifest of song IDs, or an inline CSV; plus
sorting, tag and duration filters, layout/preset/theme/
language, and color overrides — fetches and ranks the matching
songs, and surgically replaces only the content between the
markers. Beyond single songs, a profile summary card
(`/api/profile`) and an auto-discovered card stack
(`/api/cards`) render whole-catalog views. All surfaces share
one parser and one renderer, so cards look identical whichever
path produced them.
narrative.technicalApproach:
A pnpm monorepo with three workspaces: `apps/` (the Next.js
15.1 frontend on Vercel), `packages/` (two reusable kernels),
and `action/` (the GitHub Marketplace Action). The two
packages are the architecturally important part.
**`@suno-cards/parser`** is a Valibot-validated (v0.42.1)
client for Suno's public oEmbed-style API on
`studio-api-prod.suno.com` — anonymous, CORS-enabled,
public-by-design endpoints Suno advertises via the W3C oEmbed
protocol; no scraping, no auth, no headless browsers. The same
parser is shared with Seismophone, a separate Suno analytics
product, so a single schema definition and a single
typed-error hierarchy underwrite both — fix a Suno API drift
once, both benefit. That hierarchy now distinguishes an HTTP
`422` malformed-request (`SunoInvalidRequestError`) from a
`404` unknown-handle (`SunoHandleNotFoundError`), alongside
not-found, private, not-ready, schema, and network error
types. A 2026-07 health audit also quarantined `/api/trending`
— it turned out to be an abandoned September-2024 snapshot,
not a live feed, so `fetchTrending` was removed from the
parser's public exports to stop anyone building on stale data.
**`@suno-cards/render`** is the pure-function SVG engine —
deterministic, cacheable, and callable from both Vercel's
serverless runtime and the Action's Node 20 runner. The
defining rendering technique: SVG `<foreignObject>` carries
inline HTML `<span>`s so CJK text shapes correctly (plain SVG
`<text>` can't), with CSS `@keyframes` animating those spans
into the equalizer bars — a hybrid plain SVG can't produce
cleanly, and one that survives GitHub's Camo image proxy
because CSS keyframes pass its SVG sanitization. Text ships
localized in English, Chinese, and Japanese. Two-level
caching: Vercel's upstream data cache (3600s for single cards)
plus downstream `Cache-Control` headers that let Camo manage
distribution. The Action is built by an esbuild bundle
(`action/build.ts` → committed `action/dist/index.js`, Node
20) so it needs no install step at workflow time. Quality:
Biome for lint/format, Vitest suites across parser, render,
and action.
narrative.outcomes:
Live production service at
github-readme-suno-cards.vercel.app with `/gallery` and
`/builder` routes and three SVG endpoints (`/api/card`,
`/api/profile`, `/api/cards`). The current public repository
is the clean-extract release — first commit `73f4fa8`
"initial commit — github-readme-suno-cards v0.1" on
2026-04-13, immediately followed by `295756e` "clean remaining
internal reference" (the post-extraction sanitization sweep) —
carried forward to its latest commit `86167f4` on 2026-07-06,
which recorded a 2026-07-05 health audit confirming all three
render surfaces against live Suno data. That audit produced
two durable API decisions: the `/api/trending` fossil was
quarantined out of the public parser surface, and HTTP 422 got
its own error type. The shared parser kernel with Seismophone
is the architecturally important outcome: rather than two Suno
integrations drifting apart, both products are pinned to one
Valibot contract and one typed-error hierarchy. Roadmap:
waveform card variant, lyrics-excerpt cards, playlist cards, a
JSON API, and PNG export via `@resvg/resvg`.
narrative.myRole:
Sole author across both the original internal monorepo and
the public v0.1 extraction. Designed the pnpm monorepo split
with the parser and renderer as reusable workspace packages;
wrote the Valibot schemas against Suno's oEmbed contract;
designed the typed Suno error hierarchy (including the
422-vs-404 split); engineered the `<foreignObject>` +
CSS-keyframes technique that handles CJK text and animated
equalizers in a single card; built the GitHub Marketplace
Action with its esbuild-bundled dist and its 36-input surface
with three-format ID normalization; designed the
`<!-- SUNO-CARDS:START/END -->` marker-based README
replacement; built the visual `/builder` with live preview
over all 12 base combinations, 11 toggles, and hex overrides;
ran the 2026-04-13 public-extract release and the 2026-07
health audit that quarantined the trending fossil; and
coordinate parser updates across this project and its
Seismophone sibling.

projects.publicSummary:
An audio-and-status notification system for Claude Code, Cursor
IDE, and Codex CLI. The core idea: you never have to learn the
tool. You talk to your AI agent in plain English — "install
audio-hooks," "switch to chimes," "snooze for 30 minutes," "send
alerts to my Slack" — and the agent runs the commands, reads the
results, and reports back. It ships 37 hook events (3 on by
default), two audio themes, warnings before you hit your 5-hour
and 7-day usage limits, webhooks to Slack/Discord/Teams/ntfy,
text-to-speech that reads the agent's actual reply aloud, and a
29-segment status bar that tracks how much of the context window
is left. Now on v6.3.4 with 248 automated tests running on
Ubuntu, Windows, and macOS across Python 3.9–3.13. Open source
under MIT; runs on the Python standard library with no extra
dependencies. A sister repo, echook-promo-video, ships an
88-second product film built entirely in code.

narrative.impactHeadline: A notification system for AI coding assistants — Claude Code, Cursor, and Codex — that you install and set up just by talking to the AI in plain English. It plays sounds, reads the agent's reply aloud, warns you before you hit usage limits, and shows a live status bar.
narrative.problem:
Developers running long AI coding sessions on Claude Code,
Cursor IDE, and Codex CLI have no easy way to *hear* what their
agent is doing. They alt-tab to check progress, miss
usage-limit warnings, and only notice a stuck session by
looking at it. Add-on notification tools exist, but they all
share the same flaw: the human sits downstream of the command
line. You have to read docs, memorize flags, copy commands, and
re-learn the same surface every time it changes. That breaks
down the moment a serious user rotates between three editors,
each with its own configuration language and its own muscle
memory. The real mismatch isn't audio — it's the working
contract between humans, AI agents, and developer tooling.
narrative.audience:
Software engineers operating Claude Code, Cursor IDE, or Codex
CLI at the "AI-first" level — people comfortable telling their
coding agent to install, theme, snooze, webhook, or reconfigure
things in plain English rather than memorizing subcommands.
Also: power users debugging long-running workflows where a
sound is faster than watching a screen; teams rolling out
shared notification defaults across several developers; and
researchers who want a working reference implementation of an
AI-first plugin contract.
narrative.productLogic:
echook flips the usual relationship: the human is upstream, and
the AI agent learns the tool on the human's behalf. One install
prompt — *"install the audio-hooks plugin from
github.com/ChanMeng666/echook"* — starts the whole flow. The
agent runs `claude plugin marketplace add` and `claude plugin
install`, the user types `/reload-plugins` exactly once (the
only manual step in the tool's entire lifetime, because Claude
Code's reload has no command-line equivalent), and from then on
every interaction is a sentence. *"Switch to chimes." "Snooze
for 8 hours." "Send alerts to my Slack at this webhook and test
it." "Turn everything off except stop and permission
requests."* The `/audio-hooks` skill reads the intent, runs the
right `audio-hooks` command, reads its JSON output, and tells
you what happened. You memorize nothing. Cross-editor parity is
part of the same contract: every prompt works the same way on
Cursor's agent and on Codex CLI, because all three drive the
same Python program against the same JSON interface.
narrative.businessLogic:
Open source under MIT — no paywall, no premium tier, no
telemetry. The value is build-in-public credibility: echook is
the demonstration piece for Chan's AI-first product philosophy,
and it shows up in Engram-cohort interviews, recruiter
conversations, and the README of downstream client projects. A
sustained release cadence (now v6.3.4), a three-OS
continuous-integration matrix, and a 248-case test suite
guarding the cross-editor contract make it visibly production
engineering, not a weekend script. The Remotion promo-video
sister repo extends the same proof — it shows Chan can ship not
only the product but the marketing film for it, end to end and
entirely in code, with no video-production tooling.
narrative.technicalApproach:
AI-first design at every layer. The `audio-hooks` command-line
tool exposes a stable, JSON-only interface with roughly 40
subcommands, no prompts and no spinners, versioned NDJSON event
logging (schema `audio-hooks.v1`, 5 MB rotation × 3 files), and
a fixed set of error codes (`AUDIO_FILE_MISSING`,
`WEBHOOK_TIMEOUT`, `DUAL_INSTALL_DETECTED`, and so on) where
each error carries a `suggested_command` field so an agent can
recover on its own without scraping text. Hook coverage spans
37 canonical events — session start and end, tool execution,
permission requests, file changes, MCP elicitation, and
usage-limit warnings (5-hour and 7-day windows with configurable
80%/95% thresholds, each `(window, threshold, resets_at)` firing
exactly once via debounce markers). Two audio themes ship: an
ElevenLabs "Jessica" voice or modern UI chimes; rebuilds are
declarative via `config/audio_manifest.json` plus the ElevenLabs
generator. Webhooks fan out to Slack, Discord, Teams, and ntfy
through a fire-and-forget subprocess with a versioned
`audio-hooks.webhook.v1` payload, so they never block the hook.
The status bar offers 29 configurable segments, including
color-coded context-window and usage bars with absolute token
counts (e.g. `Context: 83% (166K/200K)`) so users see the
"agent dumb zone" before they reach it. The deepest piece is the
cross-editor architecture: canonical sources live in `/hooks/`,
`/bin/`, `/audio/`, `/cursor-hooks/`, and `/codex-hooks/`, and
`build-plugin.sh` syncs them into the Claude Code plugin layout
— one source of truth, three editor targets — with continuous
integration enforcing no-drift via `build-plugin.sh --check`.
Cursor rides its 3.2.16+ auto-bridge so most users get support
for free; Cursor-only installs use a native
`~/.cursor/hooks.json` registration guarded by a
`DUPLICATE_BRIDGE` error that aborts if both paths would run at
once. The Codex path handles OpenAI's required
`[features]\ncodex_hooks = true` flag by writing a fresh
`~/.codex/config.toml` when none exists and emitting
machine-readable `next_steps` for the agent to edit an existing
one — echook never round-trips a user's TOML, because that would
destroy comments and formatting. Canonical events with no Codex
equivalent no-op cleanly under the Codex invoker with a debug
log line, never blocking the session. The whole contract is
validated by 248 test cases across Ubuntu, Windows, and macOS on
Python 3.9, 3.12, and 3.13 in GitHub Actions.
narrative.outcomes:
~76 GitHub stars (external — needs registry check; live 75),
151 commits across the 5.x → 6.x line (now v6.3.4), and 248
automated test cases. The defining release sequence: v5.0 (Jan
2026) shipped the AI-first redesign — JSON command interface,
`/audio-hooks` skill, NDJSON logging, plugin-native install;
v5.2.0 (May 2026) added native Codex CLI support with +33
contract tests; v5.2.1 (May 2026) executed the echook rebrand —
a "door-only" GitHub rename from `claude-code-audio-hooks` to
`echook` (leading with "Claude Code" had become misleading once
native Cursor and Codex paths shipped) that preserved every
existing install via GitHub's repo redirect and kept the
`audio-hooks` command name, the `chanmeng-audio-hooks`
marketplace name, and all state directories unchanged. The
Engram Architect Cohort Engagement Letter (May 9, 2026)
explicitly names *claude-code-audio-hooks* among Chan's
preserved IP — formal recognition by a commercial
agentic-infrastructure firm that this is portfolio-grade
engineering, not a hobby script. The sister repo
echook-promo-video then closed the loop on the thesis: an
88-second 1080p product film composed entirely in code (React +
Remotion, with Suno-generated background music and ElevenLabs
voice and effects — no video-editing suites and no human voice
actors), fully reproducible from two commands.
narrative.myRole:
Sole author of both repos. On echook proper: designed the
AI-first contract (JSON interface + NDJSON log + fixed error
codes + `suggested_command` recovery), wrote every hook-event
integration, engineered the three-editor synchronization
pipeline (canonical sources synced into the plugin layout via
`build-plugin.sh`, with CI enforcement), authored the Codex
TOML feature-flag bootstrap pattern, and executed the 5.2.1
rebrand without breaking compatibility. On echook-promo-video:
designed the nine-section narrative arc, built the reusable
visual primitives (TypewriterText, Terminal, SoundWave,
GreenGrid, CounterNumber, GlowText), orchestrated the Remotion
transitions, wrote the ElevenLabs audio-generation script, and
tuned the volume-ducking so the background music dips under
voiceover and demo playback. Maintains both repos today.

projects.name: TikTok Shop Go Template
projects.tagline: Production-shape Go + Gin + MySQL + Redis e-commerce backend template — clean architecture, JWT auth, bilingual; distilled from ByteDance Youth Training Camp learnings
projects.publicSummary:
Production-shape Go + Gin + MySQL + Redis e-commerce backend template
with clean architecture, JWT auth, and paired React frontend — built
solo as a teaching artefact from the ByteDance Youth Training Camp
capstone. 52 GitHub stars, 7 forks, < 50 ms average API response time.

narrative.impactHeadline: Production-shape Go + Gin + MySQL + Redis e-commerce backend template — 52 stars / 7 forks (Chan's most-starred primary OSS project), clean architecture with JWT auth and bilingual READMEs, sub-50ms average API response.
narrative.problem:
Developers learning Go web development have access to plenty of
toy hello-world tutorials and plenty of opaque production
codebases, but very little **in between** — no template that
demonstrates a complete e-commerce backend with the directory
layout, build pipeline, and documentation discipline a Chinese
big-tech team would actually ship.
narrative.audience:
Two layered audiences. Primary: Go beginners progressing from
toy projects to "ship-shaped" architecture — students from the
ByteDance Youth Training Camp and similar bootcamps. Secondary:
experienced developers needing a clean starter for an
e-commerce backend who don't want to assemble Gin + MyBatis +
JWT + Redis + Docker themselves.
narrative.technicalApproach:
Full stack: **Go 1.23 + Gin** for the backend, **MySQL 8.0** for
persistence, **React 18** + Vite for the frontend, **JWT
authentication**, **RESTful APIs**, **Redis** caching. Directory
layout follows the Go community's production-correct convention:
`cmd/server/` (entry points), `internal/` (core business logic),
`api/v1/` (handlers and route definitions), `pkg/` (reusable
packages), `configs/` (YAML-based config), `docs/` (API specs +
schema diagrams), `frontend/` (React/Vite). The repo also ships
a **dual frontend implementation**: a React SPA and a vanilla
HTML/JavaScript version side by side, so learners can compare
architectural approaches against the same backend. Deployment
guides cover Docker, Railway, Render, and Fly.io. Bilingual
(EN + 简体中文) README at production quality.
narrative.outcomes:
**53 GitHub stars, 7 forks**, 27 sustained commits — the
strongest external traction of any of Chan's ByteDance Youth
Training Camp artefacts. Archived from the active README lineup
alongside the rest of that training-camp band, but the numbers
stand on their own. **< 50 ms**
average API response time. Apache-2.0 license to permit
commercial reuse while encouraging community contribution.
Doubles as the worked-example artefact accompanying Chan's
ByteDance Youth Training Camp narrative — the same engineering
practices that scored Chan as the #2-most-prolific committer on
the Team-115 capstone, packaged as a public learning resource.
narrative.myRole:
Sole author. Designed the directory layout, wrote both backend
and frontend implementations, authored the bilingual
documentation, and packaged the multi-platform deployment
guides. The repo is its own pedagogical statement: students can
read the instructor's commits to see how patterns they're being
taught compose into a real production-shape service.

projects.publicSummary:
A Model Context Protocol (MCP) server — the open standard that
lets AI assistants call outside tools — that searches Google job
listings through SerpAPI and returns results in English,
Simplified Chinese, Japanese, or Korean. Built on Dec 31 2024,
just 36 days after MCP's public launch, as a companion to the
Google News MCP server from the same author. Installs from npm,
Smithery, Docker, or a one-file `.mcpb` bundle, and is listed
across MCP discovery catalogs including Glama, PulseMCP, mcp.so,
and Smithery (catalog ratings and visitor counts are external
claims — needs registry check).

narrative.impactHeadline: The companion to the Google News server: lets AI assistants search Google job listings in English, Chinese, Japanese, and Korean. Built 36 days after MCP's launch — early enough that the founders of two major MCP catalogs contributed code to it.
narrative.problem:
When it was built at the very end of December 2024, the Model
Context Protocol was only weeks old and there was no ready-made
way for an AI assistant to look up job listings — least of all
in more than one language. A job seeker in Greater China, Japan,
or Korea who wanted Claude Desktop to find relevant openings had
to translate their own query and hand-assemble search-URL
parameters.
narrative.audience:
Three groups: (a) developers wiring job search into an AI
application; (b) multilingual job seekers using Claude Desktop
or another MCP-aware assistant; and (c) other early MCP authors
looking for a worked example of locale-aware integration with a
third-party API. (Skywork AI's write-up describes it as a
reference pattern from the early MCP ecosystem — external claim,
needs registry check.)
narrative.productLogic:
The server exposes Google Jobs (through SerpAPI) as a single MCP
tool, `search_jobs`. A caller passes a `query` plus optional
filters — `location`, `radius`, `employment_type`, `salary`,
`posted_age`, `sort_by`, and `page` — and picks the output
language with `hl` (`en`, `zh-CN`, `ja`, or `ko`). It returns up
to 10 results per page, with every label, status message, and
suggestion translated into the chosen language (24 translated
strings per locale) so the assistant can present results
directly without extra formatting.
narrative.technicalApproach:
TypeScript on Node.js, built against the official
`@modelcontextprotocol/sdk` (kept current at 1.25.1). Rather
than branch on language throughout the code, the four locales
live in one translation table keyed to shared field names, so
the same query logic serves every language and only the output
strings swap. Distribution is deliberately wide for an early MCP
server: npm (`@chanmeng666/google-jobs-server`), one-command
Smithery install, a Dockerfile for containers, and a
self-contained `.mcpb` bundle. `package.json` also carries a
structured `ai:` block (use cases, capabilities, target
audience) so LLM-driven catalogs can index it — the same
discovery-oriented authoring pattern across this portfolio's MCP
work.
narrative.outcomes:
75 commits on the main branch, about 48 by Chan and the rest
split between Renovate dependency updates and outside
contributors — notably the founders of the Smithery (Henry Mao)
and Glama (Frank Fiegel) MCP catalogs, a real early-ecosystem
signal. Carries security-assessment badges from MseeP.ai and
Glama.ai in the README. Listed across MCP discovery catalogs —
Glama, PulseMCP, mcp.so, Smithery, and others (10+ surfaces,
A-rating, PulseMCP visitor count, and the Skywork AI deep-dive
review are external claims — need registry/catalog check).
Shipped one day after its companion Google News MCP server,
making the pair one of the earliest public sets of locale-aware
MCP servers.
narrative.myRole:
Sole author. I designed the single-table localization approach,
wrote every translation, built the multi-channel distribution
(npm, Smithery, Docker, `.mcpb`), and maintain it alongside its
Google News MCP counterpart. External contributions have been
dependency bumps and catalog-integration commits; the tool
design and localization are mine.

projects.publicSummary:
A **formally published Chrome Web Store product** (extension ID
`gdajdfngeonjmcclghkmeoacopnnfpnc`) — not a dev script, not a
GitHub-only release: passed Google's full Chrome Web Store
review, with a vetted `PRIVACY.md`, an Apache-2.0 license that
includes a patent grant, and a **Manifest V3 manifest that
retains the `webRequestBlocking` permission** despite V3's
narrowed permissioning model (the extension's network-intercept
use case justified it during review). The extension auto-detects
Tencent Meeting recording streams on the `*.myqcloud.com` CDN as
the user plays the video, then generates a ready-to-run `curl`
command with the correct headers, problematic ones stripped, and
Windows paths auto-rewritten to Git-Bash-compatible Unix paths.
Listed live in the Chrome Web Store with a live-from-store rating
shield, **first committed Nov 25 2024** (5 days before
Anthropic's MCP launch), **still maintained through April 2026**
across 24 solo commits and 2 tagged releases (v1.0.0 + v1.1.0).
Built with Chrome Manifest V3 APIs (webRequest + webRequestBlocking
+ storage + runtime messaging), JavaScript ES6+, Webpack 5, Babel.

narrative.impactHeadline: Formally Chrome Web Store-published browser extension for downloading Tencent Meeting recordings — Manifest V3 with retained blocking webRequest permission, Apache-2.0 with patent grant, privacy-policy-vetted; maintained continuously since first ship in 2024.
narrative.problem:
Tencent Meeting recordings live on the `myqcloud.com` CDN
behind a combination of authentication headers, referrer
requirements, and URL patterns that make manual download
unusable for non-developers. Educators recording lectures,
business operators capturing client meetings, and content
creators producing interview material all routinely need a
local copy of a Tencent Meeting recording — for accessibility
captioning, archive retention, post-production editing, or
republishing to a different distribution channel — but the
official Tencent Meeting UI provides **no clean download
button**, the underlying CDN URL is buried in the network
tab in a non-obvious format, and ad-hoc `curl` attempts fail
almost universally because the CDN rejects requests missing
the right `referer`, `user-agent`, and `authority` headers.
Worse, Chinese corporate environments standardised on Tencent
Meeting tend to skew Windows-heavy with locked-down IT
policies that block desktop downloaders, leaving Git Bash as
the only practical Unix-shell access path — and Git Bash
consumes Unix-style paths, not Windows ones, which is yet
another conversion the average user gets wrong.
narrative.audience:
The README explicitly names three primary user groups: (1)
**educators** recording lectures who need persistent local
copies for re-use across cohorts; (2) **business professionals**
archiving client meetings for compliance, follow-up review,
or legal record-keeping; (3) **content creators** capturing
interviews, panels, and roundtables for post-production
editing into long-form content. Secondary audience: any
Mandarin-speaking knowledge worker in a corporate environment
standardised on Tencent Meeting whose IT department blocks
installation of desktop downloaders — Chrome extensions are
nearly always installable from the Chrome Web Store even
when local-admin install is restricted. The audience skew
is reflected in the manifest's localized name
(`腾讯会议录屏下载助手`) and the README's bilingual structure.
narrative.productLogic:
The extension is **passive by default**. It sits silently in
the browser, the content script injects into pages under
`*.meeting.tencent.com` and `*.myqcloud.com`, and the
background service worker listens for streaming responses
matching the Tencent Meeting recording URL signature via the
`webRequest` API. When the user plays a recording in the
normal Tencent Meeting UI, the extension captures the CDN
URL, the auth headers, and the referer chain — invisibly. The
user then clicks the extension icon to open the popup, types
(or pastes) a save path like `D:\videos\meeting.mp4`, and
clicks "Generate Command". The popup produces a ready-to-run
`curl` command with: (a) the correct authenticated URL, (b)
only the headers Tencent's CDN actually requires (problematic
ones that fail when re-issued outside the browser stripped),
(c) the Windows path auto-rewritten to `/d/videos/meeting.mp4`
for Git Bash compatibility, (d) the `--compressed` flag for
gzip-encoded streams, and (e) the `--output` flag pre-filled.
The user pastes that one line into Git Bash, hits Enter, and
the file downloads. **No URL extraction, no header guessing,
no path conversion required from the user.**
narrative.technicalApproach:
**Chrome Manifest V3** — the modern security model Google
moved all extensions to in 2023–24 — but with a deliberately
retained `webRequestBlocking` permission, which V3 narrowed
from the V2 baseline for most extensions. The justification
carried through Chrome Web Store review: this is a
download-augmentation tool whose entire purpose is to observe
and capture in-flight CDN responses; without blocking
webRequest the URL signature can't be reliably extracted in
time. **Component separation** is clean and idiomatic for V3:
`background.js` runs as the service worker, registering the
webRequest listeners against `*.myqcloud.com` and writing
matched URLs to `chrome.storage`; `content.js` is the
content-script injection scoped to `*.meeting.tencent.com`
and `*.myqcloud.com`, handling page-level DOM observation;
`popup.js` + `popup.html` + `popup.css` is the user-facing
UI; `utils/download.js` houses the curl-command builder. The
non-obvious engineering is in **two reverse-engineered
details**: (1) which headers Tencent's CDN actually requires
vs. which ones break the request when re-issued from outside
the browser context — established by trial across multiple
recording sessions — and (2) the **automatic Windows-to-Unix
path conversion** for Git Bash (`D:\videos\meeting.mp4` →
`/d/videos/meeting.mp4`), which sounds trivial but is
precisely the conversion average Chinese corporate users get
wrong every time. **Build**: Webpack 5 + Babel transpilation
+ ESLint code-quality. **Distribution**: Chrome Web Store
review-approved with a `PRIVACY.md` (mandatory for V3
approval), an Apache-2.0 LICENSE with patent grant, and a
`CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md`. **Permissions justified at review**:
`webRequest` and `webRequestBlocking` (URL capture), `storage`
(local URL persistence between popup and background), plus
host permissions on `*.qq.com`, `*.tencent.com`, and
`*.myqcloud.com` (all Tencent-controlled CDN origins).
narrative.outcomes:
**Formally published on Chrome Web Store** (the explicit
framing the project leads with) at extension ID
`gdajdfngeonjmcclghkmeoacopnnfpnc` — a credential that
meaningfully exceeds an unpublished GitHub-only release
because it carries Google's review approval for the
Manifest V3 manifest, the privacy posture, and the
permissions justification. The README displays a **live
chrome-web-store rating shield** that pulls the current
store rating dynamically (`shields.io/chrome-web-store/
rating/gdajdfngeonjmcclghkmeoacopnnfpnc`) and a
github-release badge for **two tagged releases (v1.0.0 +
v1.1.0)**. Total **24 solo commits across 18 months** —
from the first commit on **2024-11-25** (which incidentally
predates Anthropic's MCP public launch by 5 days) through
the most recent commit on **2026-04-07**, putting it in the
same productive late-2024 shipping window that produced the
two early MCP servers and the Christmas-greeting CLI that
became SendJoy. Sustained traction for an explicitly niche
utility: the kind of product that exists only because Chan
used Tencent Meeting daily during the China-based work
portion of her career, hit the exact friction the README
describes, and wrote the tool she herself needed —
afterwards committing to the Chrome Web Store distribution
path (rather than a GitHub-only release) so other
Mandarin-speaking knowledge workers could install it from
the same trusted distribution channel as any other Chrome
extension.
narrative.myRole:
Sole author, sole maintainer, sole Chrome Web Store
publisher of record. Reverse-engineered which `*.myqcloud.com`
headers the Tencent CDN actually requires vs. which ones
break the re-issued request; designed the popup UI; wrote
the Manifest V3 manifest with the deliberately retained
`webRequestBlocking` permission and shepherded it through
Chrome Web Store review (including the `PRIVACY.md`
authoring and the permissions justification); engineered
the cross-platform curl generator including the
Windows-to-Git-Bash path conversion; built the Webpack 5 +
Babel build pipeline; published v1.0.0 and v1.1.0 to the
Chrome Web Store; maintains the listing today with
periodic header updates as Tencent's CDN evolves.

projects.publicSummary:
A free tool that turns a single link into a live, animated banner
for GitHub profiles and README files — paste the URL into any
Markdown file and the banner animates on its own, with nothing to
install. It ships 355 ready-made designs across 19 themes (pride
flags, tech, nature, gaming, and more) and a point-and-click
editor for people who don't want to hand-write links. Open-source
and built solo over 18 months; it powers every badge on this
profile, so a broken render shows up here first.

narrative.impactHeadline: Paste one URL into any GitHub README and a live animated banner appears, no install. 355 free, open-source banner designs power every badge in this profile.
narrative.problem:
Giving a GitHub profile, project README, or docs site a
striking animated header usually means one of three bad
options: hand-authoring SVG (a multi-day job per banner),
paying a third-party service that adds a watermark or a narrow
style range, or dropping in a static image that looks dated
within months. None of these scale to the modern norm of
*hundreds* of repos per developer, each wanting its own look.
The specific gap: no zero-install service lets you drop a
single link into Markdown and get a live animated banner the
browser renders on its own — no JavaScript, no video player —
that also survives GitHub's image proxy (which strips most
interactivity). The gap is widest for identity-coded
communities: pride creators wanting accurate flag palettes,
technical writers wanting one consistent capsule shape across
docs, niche aesthetics (cyberpunk, art deco, impressionist)
with no ready-made gallery.
narrative.audience:
Three concentric audiences. (1) Open-source maintainers and
developer-builders who want polished READMEs and profile pages
without pulling in extra dependencies — the primary daily
users. (2) Identity-coded communities with template subsets
authored for them: 20 pride-flag templates (Progress, Trans,
Bi, Pan, Lesbian, Nonbinary, Ace, Genderfluid, Aromantic,
Rainbow, and more), a large GitHub-Profile set oriented around
developer branding, and experimental/creative-technologist
designs. (3) Engineers studying the project as a worked example
of parametric SVG animation built without a heavy graphics
framework, a deliberately documented multi-phase refactor, and
the "truth up marketing numbers" discipline of correcting your
own inflated counts.
narrative.productLogic:
Single-URL contract. The user pastes one Markdown image line:
`![banner](https://gradient-svg-generator.vercel.app/api/svg?text=Hello&template=neural-network&height=120)`
and GitHub (or any Markdown renderer) embeds the live animated
SVG. Ten URL parameters control everything: `text=` (the
display string, `;`-separated for skill-pill grids), `template=`
(any of the 355 template names), `height=`, `duration=` (loop
time), `color0=`…`colorN=` (gradient-stop overrides, hex without
`#`), `gradientType=` (an effect-style override that bypasses
the template default), `stroke=`, `strokeWidth=`, `rotate=`,
`textBg=`. People who don't want to hand-write parameters open
the /create visual editor — a point-and-click composer with live
preview that emits the final URL to copy. People who want to
browse first hit /templates, a virtualized gallery
(`@tanstack/react-virtual`) with fuzzy search across names,
categories, and tags, so all 355 entries scroll smoothly on any
device.
narrative.businessLogic:
Free, ISC-licensed, no paywall, no API key, no watermark. The
whole service runs as a Vercel serverless function that streams
the SVG document straight back to the browser
(`Content-Type: image/svg+xml`), cached at Vercel's edge. The
build-in-public value: the project is shared infrastructure for
Chan's other work — every visual badge across every
Chan-authored README pulls from this one service, so the service
is its own continuous test signal (any rendering regression
breaks visible branding on her own portfolio, forcing a same-day
fix). It's also the project that took the most explicit honesty
audit: commits `fdcb7dd` "truth up marketing numbers",
`db724e0` "drop 50+ Effects claim", and `8fd7886` "catch older
stale counts" walked the codebase correcting every exaggerated
figure to the real template count — engineering discipline that
turns marketing claims into checkable numbers instead of letting
generated copy inflate them.
narrative.technicalApproach:
**The SVG-animation technique is the load-bearing engineering
here.** Every banner is a small live XML document — typically
2–8 KB — that the browser animates on its own with no
JavaScript runtime, using three orthogonal SVG mechanisms in
combination:

(1) **SMIL animation elements** (`<animate>`,
`<animateTransform>`, `<animateMotion>`) animate XML attributes
over time — most often animating a
`<linearGradient>`/`<radialGradient>`'s stop `offset` values so
colors flow (rainbow-wave, hologram-blue, northern-aurora), or
rotating a pattern (zigzag-energy, cyber-matrix). This is the
declarative channel: the whole timeline is XML, no scripting.

(2) **CSS `@keyframes` embedded in a `<style>` block inside the
SVG.** Where SMIL falls short (animating filter strength,
`stroke-dasharray` reveals), CSS keyframes take over —
typewriter-terminal, glitch-cyber, neon-flicker, luminance-glow,
chrome-flow. Same technique used in the sister project
github-readme-suno-cards: CSS keyframes survive GitHub's
image-proxy sanitization pass.

(3) **SVG filter primitives** for organic effects neither SMIL
nor CSS can produce — turbulent-waves, liquid-mercury,
plasma-blob, caustic-underwater all feed `<feTurbulence>`
(fractal noise) into `<feDisplacementMap>` for distortion, plus
`<feGaussianBlur>` and `<feColorMatrix>` for bloom/glow.
Path-text templates layer `<textPath>` on a moving `<path>` so
text follows a curve.

**Template organisation is the architectural bet.** 355
templates split across 19 named categories (Pride, Tech, Nature,
Material, Text-FX, Gaming, Weather, Light & Shadow, Art
Movements, Culinary, Patterns, Metallic, Path-Text, Shapes,
Experimental, GitHub-Profile, and more) plus base gradient
types. A documented refactor saga did the structural work:
Phase 5 extracted shared `svgPrimitives` factories and
`_shared/palettes.js` and moved to feature-folders; Phase 7 was
a JavaScript→TypeScript migration in seven sub-phases plus
Zod-validated /api/svg query params (a request like
`?height=99999999999` returns a clean typed error instead of
crashing the function); Phase 8 virtualized the gallery.
Deployment: Vercel serverless. Testing: Vitest with
`tests/contract/` guarding the URL-parameter contract across
refactors, plus `tests/unit/`. Quality gates: Husky pre-commit
`eslint --fix` + `prettier --write` via `lint-staged`;
`web-vitals` telemetry in production. The July 2026 "Ink Room ×
Living Color" redesign refreshed the whole site UI, added a
gradient footer wordmark, and gave the project its own OG
social-share card.
narrative.outcomes:
283 commits across 18 months (first commit 2025-01-06, latest
2026-07-10), effectively solo — every commit is Chan's except
one automated GitHub Action commit. Production availability on
Vercel's global edge, ISC-licensed for broad reuse. Dogfooded
across every Chan-authored repo: the badges, banners, and
section headers in every public README (including the one that
ships this profile) come from
`gradient-svg-generator.vercel.app/api/svg` URLs, so any
rendering regression breaks visible branding immediately and
forces a same-day fix — a continuous, self-testing loop. The
multi-phase refactor saga is itself an outcome worth surfacing:
Phase 5 (extract primitives, palettes, feature-folders), Phase 6
(truth up the marketing numbers; author `docs/architecture.md`
and an `adding-an-effect` walkthrough), Phase 7 (JS→TS in seven
sub-phases plus Zod validation), Phase 8 (gallery
virtualization), followed by the July 2026 "Ink Room × Living
Color" full-site redesign and a new OG share card. The honesty
discipline is rare enough in solo open source to flag on its
own: Chan went through the codebase and corrected exaggerated
counts rather than letting generated copy inflate them. Roadmap:
continued template additions and broader category coverage.
narrative.myRole:
Sole author. Designed the URL-parameterised streaming-SVG
contract; authored every one of the 355 template generators
across 19 categories; designed the parametric template system so
each template is a self-contained function rather than a
monolithic switch; engineered the SVG-animation toolkit
combining SMIL, CSS keyframes, and SVG filters; built the
`/create` visual editor with live preview and the `/templates`
virtualized gallery; ran the full multi-phase refactor
(primitive extraction, marketing-number truth-up plus docs,
JS→TS migration with Zod validation, gallery virtualization) and
the July 2026 site redesign; set up the Vercel + GitHub Actions +
Vitest + Husky pipeline. I use the service across every personal
repo as a live test signal for the project itself.

projects.name: FemTracker Agent
projects.tagline: 8-agent CopilotKit + LangGraph women's-health companion (merged into CopilotKit's official demos_2025)
projects.publicSummary:
AI-powered women's-health companion built on CopilotKit and Python
LangGraph, with **8 specialized agents** (Main Coordinator + Cycle
Tracker + Fertility Tracker + Symptom Mood + Nutrition Guide + Exercise
Coach + Lifestyle Manager + Health Insights) coordinating through
intelligent routing. WHO-standard health scoring (0-100 metric scales),
Redis caching with reported 90%+ hit rates, Supabase PostgreSQL
persistence. Merged into CopilotKit's official `demos_2025` showcase
via [PR #2068](https://github.com/CopilotKit/CopilotKit/pull/2068)
(publicly thanked by maintainer Tyler Slaton).

narrative.impactHeadline: 8-agent women's-health companion merged into CopilotKit's official demos_2025 — intelligent routing across specialists, WHO-standard scoring, 90%+ Redis cache hit rates, publicly thanked by CopilotKit maintainers as a reference implementation.
narrative.problem:
Existing women's-health apps treat menstrual cycle, fertility,
symptoms, nutrition, exercise, and lifestyle as **disconnected
verticals** — each one a separate dashboard, no shared model of
the user, no agent to reason across them. Users asking
"should I push my workout today given where I am in my cycle?"
get four sub-apps with no answer. Add the privacy concerns of
sensitive health data being aggregated by ad-funded apps and
the category is overdue for an agent-native rebuild.
narrative.audience:
Health-conscious women managing cycle-related symptoms,
planning conception, or pursuing holistic wellness — and the
secondary audience of agent-framework practitioners studying
what production-grade multi-agent coordination looks like
under HIPAA-aligned constraints. CopilotKit's maintainers
themselves are an audience: the project shipped into their
official `demos_2025` showcase.
narrative.productLogic:
Single conversational surface (CopilotKit) routes the user's
intent to one of eight specialised agents: **Main Coordinator**
+ **Cycle Tracker** + **Fertility Tracker** + **Symptom Mood**
+ **Nutrition Guide** + **Exercise Coach** + **Lifestyle
Manager** + **Health Insights**. Each agent has its own
domain logic and prompt scaffolding but shares the same
Supabase-backed user model, so a question that crosses two
domains ("am I dehydrated today?", which touches nutrition +
cycle + exercise) gets routed through the Coordinator and
answered with WHO-standard scoring rather than three
disconnected guesses.
narrative.technicalApproach:
The architectural bet is **CopilotKit on the frontend +
LangGraph on the backend**, communicating through a CopilotKit
runtime adapter. Next.js 15 + React 19 + TypeScript on the
frontend; Python 3.12 + LangGraph for agent orchestration;
Supabase PostgreSQL with **Row Level Security** for
privacy-preserving multi-tenant persistence; Redis caching
achieving 90%+ hit rates on hot read paths. The health-scoring
engine implements WHO-standard algorithms across six dimensions:
exercise (150 min/week benchmark), hydration (2 000 mL daily),
cycle regularity (21–35 day window), mood patterns, lifestyle
factors, fertility indicators — each 0–100, composited into
actionable insights. Codebase is 83.2% TypeScript for type
safety in a medical-domain product.
narrative.outcomes:
**Merged into CopilotKit's official `demos_2025` showcase**
via [PR #2068](https://github.com/CopilotKit/CopilotKit/pull/2068),
publicly thanked by maintainer Tyler Slaton — external
recognition of production-grade quality from the framework's
own maintainers, not just a self-published demo. The demo
deployment measured a 95+ Lighthouse score, sub-second TTFB,
and 99.9% uptime while it was live. Demo video at the linked
YouTube URL. 90 sustained commits.
narrative.myRole:
Sole author of the agent architecture, the WHO-scoring engine,
the Supabase schema with Row Level Security, and the CopilotKit
runtime adapter. Drove the project into CopilotKit's official
demo set — a path that required not just shipping but
demonstrating reference-implementation-grade quality to a
third-party maintainer team.

projects.name: LibraryOS — Multi-Tenant Library SaaS
projects.tagline: 18-month / 80-commit production multi-tenant SaaS on its own libraryos.live domain — 4-tier access model with deliberate regional accessibility design, 4-role RBAC, Supabase row-level security + keep-alive cron, survived two architectural pivots (single-tenant->multi-tenant Dec 2025, Vercel->Cloudflare Workers Feb 2026) and a 12-commit Stripe-SDK-on-Workers debugging saga that ended by switching the Stripe client to fetch-based HTTP.

Supabase PostgreSQL (auth + RLS policies + stored procedures + migrations)
Stripe 20 (Checkout + Customer Portal + webhooks + signature verification)
Stripe with fetch-based HTTP client (Cloudflare Workers compatibility)
Stripe price IDs read from database (per-tier/per-region dynamic pricing)
React Hook Form + Zod 4 (typed form validation)
React Context (AuthContext, OrganizationContext for tenant switching)
libraryos.live custom domain (purchased, not default subdomain)

projects.publicSummary:
Production **multi-tenant library management SaaS** with its own
purchased custom domain ([libraryos.live](https://libraryos.live)),
built over **18 months across 80 solo commits** spanning **two
major architectural pivots and one infamous debugging saga**. The
product offers a 4-tier access model with Stripe-integrated
flows (Free / Basic / Pro / Enterprise), 4-role RBAC
(Owner/Admin/Librarian/Member), per-tenant data isolation via
Supabase row-level security policies, team invitations via Resend
transactional emails, multi-organization membership (users can
belong to multiple libraries and switch between them), and an
**Africa-friendly accessibility tier** explicitly designed for
lower-income markets. **Defining engineering moments**: (1) the
single-tenant -> multi-tenant SaaS rebuild on 2025-12-30 (commit
`5e7e8c5`); (2) the same-week rebrand from
`library-management-system` -> `LibraryOS` (commit `4bd58b5`);
(3) the **Supabase keep-alive cron** to prevent the free-tier
database from auto-pausing after 7 days of inactivity (commits
`17344d6` + `64b03d0`); (4) the **Vercel -> Cloudflare Workers
migration** (commit `14c42fc`, 2026-02-03) with the custom
`libraryos.live` domain switch (`ecf6c14`); (5) the
**12-commit Stripe-SDK-on-Cloudflare-Workers debugging saga**
ending with the architectural fix in commit `e1ab824`
*"use fetch HTTP client for Stripe SDK on Cloudflare Workers"*
and the companion `85b6244` *"read Stripe price IDs from database
instead of env vars"*. Stack: Next.js 16.1 + React 19.2 +
Tailwind 4 + Supabase + Stripe 20 + Resend 6 + shadcn/ui +
Zod 4 + @opennextjs/cloudflare. Apache-2.0.

narrative.impactHeadline: Multi-tenant library-management SaaS with deliberate regional accessibility design — survived two architectural pivots (single→multi-tenant, Vercel→Cloudflare Workers), 4-tier access model, ready for community libraries, small schools, and makerspaces.
narrative.problem:
Small libraries — community libraries, school libraries,
startup-office book collections, niche-genre clubs, university
residence hall libraries — are stuck between two bad options
for managing their book collections. Option A: enterprise-grade
Integrated Library Systems (Koha, Evergreen, ExLibris Alma)
that demand heavy operational overhead, require dedicated IT
staff to operate, and ship with UX patterns from 2005.
Option B: spreadsheets and shared Google Sheets, which silently
break the moment two librarians edit at once, give no role-based
permissions, and can't handle email invitations or membership
scoping. Both options also assume **one library per installation** —
none of them handle the case where a single platform should serve
hundreds of independent organisations, each with its own books,
members, and loans. That's the gap LibraryOS fills: a
**multi-tenant library SaaS** with per-organisation data
isolation, a tiered access model including an Africa-friendly
accessibility tier for lower-income markets, and a modern
Next.js 16 + React 19 frontend that any community librarian
can navigate without training.
narrative.audience:
Three primary audiences. (1) **Community library operators**:
churches, residence halls, makerspaces, co-working spaces,
small private schools, niche-genre clubs — anyone running a
book collection serving 50–5,000 members who outgrew
spreadsheets but can't justify Koha's TCO. (2) **Multi-branch
organisations**: parent organisations operating multiple
libraries (school districts, library networks, NGOs running
multi-site book-donation programmes) who need
per-organisation isolation but unified administration. (3)
**Engineers studying the project as a worked example** of
(a) Supabase row-level security policies for multi-tenant
data isolation, (b) Stripe Checkout + Customer Portal +
webhooks with database-stored access IDs for per-tier and
per-region routing, (c) the @opennextjs/cloudflare
adapter pattern with non-trivial third-party SDK integration
(the Stripe-on-Workers fetch-HTTP-client workaround is the
kind of production issue a project ships through, not
around).
narrative.productLogic:
The product surface is structured around three concentric
nouns: **users** (one person, one auth identity), **organisations**
(one library entity with its own books, members, settings),
and **memberships** (the join between user and organisation
carrying a role). A user signs up once and can belong to
many organisations — switching between them via the
`OrganizationContext` provider in the React tree, which
mirrors the same pattern Slack and Linear use. Within an
organisation, four roles compose a granular permission
matrix: **Owners** can delete the organisation and manage
access; **Admins** can manage members but not delete the
org; **Librarians** can add/edit books and manage loans;
**Members** can browse, borrow, return, and review books.
Per-tenant data isolation is enforced **at the database
layer** via Supabase row-level security (RLS) policies on
every table — `books`, `loans`, `members`, `reviews`,
`categories` — so even a SQL injection in application code
can't leak across tenants. **Entitlement tiers**: the entry
level covers 100 books / 5 users / 3 loans-per-user, and higher
levels scale those quotas progressively to unlimited — enforced
server-side against the tenant record, not hidden in the UI.
Stripe Checkout handles initial activation, Customer Portal
handles upgrades / downgrades / cancellations, and the
`/api/stripe/webhook` endpoint atomically updates the
`organizations.subscription_status` field based on signed
Stripe events. **Team invitations**: an Admin invites a new
member by email; Resend sends a transactional invite with a
scoped token; the recipient clicks through, authenticates,
and joins the organisation with the pre-assigned role.
**The Africa-friendly accessibility tier** is a deliberate
regional design decision (commit `16e81c1`) — lower-income
markets need a region-aware entry point for the product to
be reachable at all.
narrative.businessLogic:
The infrastructure choices are structurally favourable: Supabase
covers the database for typical small-library volume (the
Supabase keep-alive cron — commit `17344d6` — prevents the
database from auto-pausing after extended idle periods, which is
the single load-bearing infrastructure decision); Cloudflare
Workers handle edge compute. The Africa-friendly accessibility
tier is the non-obvious product decision: rather than ignoring
lower-income markets, LibraryOS deliberately ships a region-aware
entry point so the product reaches global communities that the
Koha / Alma incumbents have priced out entirely.
**Apache-2.0** with patent grant licensing makes the codebase
explicitly fork-and-deploy-able as a worked example for engineers
wanting to ship their own multi-tenant SaaS — particularly useful
given the Stripe-on-Workers compatibility solution baked in.
narrative.technicalApproach:
**Multi-tenant SaaS architecture.** Single Supabase
Postgres instance with strict row-level security policies
on every domain table — a policy like `organization_id =
auth.jwt() ->> 'org_id'` enforces tenant isolation
regardless of application bugs. Stored procedures for the
non-trivial loan-lifecycle logic (loan -> due-date
calculation -> return -> overdue triggers). The schema is
documented as an ERD in the README covering
`organizations`, `organization_members`, `books`, `loans`,
`reviews`, `categories`, and the subscription-state
columns on `organizations`. **The Stripe-on-Cloudflare-
Workers fight (March 2026).** The Stripe Node SDK uses the
Node `https` module by default — which Workers don't have.
First symptom: checkout endpoint times out silently.
Initial debug attempts (commits `fdd1103` "add visible
debug panel", `2b54c48` "add /api/stripe/debug endpoint",
`991af90` "step-by-step server logging") progressively
isolated the failure to the Stripe SDK's HTTP client
layer. Final fix (commit `e1ab824`): configure the Stripe
constructor with `httpClient: Stripe.createFetchHttpClient()`
so it uses the standard `fetch` API instead of `https`,
making it Workers-compatible. The companion fix (commit
`85b6244`): move Stripe price IDs from environment
variables to a database lookup, so the same deployment can
serve different prices for different tenants or regions
without env-var sprawl. The whole episode is a real
production-debugging artefact, documented in the
Stripe + Cloudflare Workers integration guide (commit
`12ddaef`). **Supabase keep-alive system** (commit
`17344d6`, optimised in `64b03d0`). Supabase's free tier
auto-pauses any database with 7 consecutive days of zero
queries. For a low-traffic SaaS this would silently break
the production deployment. The keep-alive system is a
scheduled cron that pings the database with a no-op query
every few days, keeping it active without consuming
meaningful free-tier quota. **Custom domain
infrastructure**: registered `libraryos.live` (a purchased
domain, not a default Workers subdomain) on 2026-02-03
(commit `ecf6c14`), wired DNS through Cloudflare, and
co-located the Stripe live-mode account on the same
domain to keep the production billing surface
single-origin. **Frontend stack**: Next.js 16.1 App Router
with React 19.2 + TypeScript 5.9, Tailwind 4 with custom
brand-gradient colours (#02FF73 / #09ADAA), shadcn/ui
component library on top of 10 Radix UI primitives,
React Hook Form with Zod 4 schema validation for typed
forms end-to-end. **State**: two React Context providers
— `AuthContext` for user/auth and `OrganizationContext`
for the currently-selected tenant — so component code
reads the current tenant without prop drilling.
**Security responsiveness**: Vercel's automated CVE
patching system flagged two React Server Components +
React Flight RCE vulnerabilities in late 2025
(`vercel/react-server-components-cve-vu-64tvuo` and
`vercel/react-flight-and-nextjs-rce-ad-i38an0`); both PRs
merged within hours of discovery
(commits `85fa6aa` + `101568a`).
narrative.outcomes:
Live and operational at
[libraryos.live](https://libraryos.live) — a **purchased
custom domain**, not the default `*.workers.dev`
subdomain, signaling a commitment beyond a hobby
deployment. **80 solo commits across 18 months** from the
first commit on 2024-10-30 through the most recent on
2026-04-07 (commit `5c8d8e7`). The defining timeline is
the **Dec 2025 multi-tenant rebuild**: commit `2ad43de`
(2025-12-30) upgrades the entire dependency stack to
latest tech (React 19, Next.js 16, Tailwind 4), commit
`5e7e8c5` (same day) ships *"add multi-tenant support
with Stripe billing and Resend emails"* — the rebuild
that transformed a single-tenant library tool into a
SaaS, followed by 10 more commits across Dec 30–31 wiring
up Stripe live mode + auth + UI brand redesign + the
rename to LibraryOS (`4bd58b5`) — a full multi-tenant
SaaS shipped in ~36 hours. The **Feb 2026 Vercel ->
Cloudflare Workers migration** (commit `14c42fc`)
consolidated the production stack on Cloudflare's edge
platform. The **March 2026 Stripe-SDK-on-Workers debugging
saga** is documented across 12 commits and resolved with
`e1ab824` — the kind of production issue that
demonstrates engineering ability to **debug across
platform boundaries**, not just ship features. Continuing
maintenance through April 2026 with README polish
(`5c8d8e7`). The project serves as Chan's worked example
of full-stack solo SaaS execution — multi-tenant
architecture, payment integration, security
responsiveness, persistence infrastructure (Supabase
keep-alive), and globalisation (Africa-friendly
accessibility tier) — all on a purchased custom domain
that signals "this is a real product".
narrative.myRole:
Sole product designer, sole engineer, sole maintainer
across all 80 commits and 18 months. Designed the
multi-tenant schema with row-level security; designed the
4-tier access model including the Africa-friendly regional
accessibility decision; designed the 4-role RBAC
permission matrix; built every API route under
`/api/auth`, `/api/email`, `/api/stripe`, and `/api/health`;
engineered the Supabase keep-alive cron preventing
free-tier auto-pause; executed the December 2025 single-
tenant -> multi-tenant rebuild as a ~36-hour sprint;
executed the February 2026 Vercel -> Cloudflare Workers
migration with the libraryos.live custom-domain switch;
debugged and fixed the March 2026 Stripe-SDK-on-Workers
compatibility issue (5 debug commits + 1 architectural
fix moving price IDs from env to DB + 1 final fix
switching to fetch HTTP client + integration-guide docs);
applied two React Server Components CVE patches within
hours of Vercel-automated PRs; purchased and configured
the `libraryos.live` custom domain. Maintains the live
deployment today.

projects.name: LinkedIn Jobs Search — Dual-Audience Job Search Platform (Same Operations via SPA, HTTP API, MCP, or CLI)
projects.tagline: One Cloudflare Worker serving four surfaces over a single backend — a React SPA for humans who click-and-type, plus three identical-power agent surfaces (HTTP API at `/api/*` with OpenAPI 3.1 + Scalar docs at `/docs`, MCP Streamable HTTP at `/mcp` with 11 locked tools mirroring the HTTP routes 1:1, and an `lja` CLI on npm mirroring the MCP names 1:1). Every operation a human can do by clicking, an AI agent can do by HTTP call, MCP tool, or CLI invocation, with full feature parity and a unified `lja_*` API key.
techStack: @modelcontextprotocol/sdk 1.29 — Streamable HTTP MCP server at `/mcp` with 11 locked tool names
cac-based `lja` CLI published as `@chanmeng666/linkedin-jobs-search-cli` on npm (8 commands mirroring MCP names 1:1)
React 18 SPA built by Vite 6 + react-router-dom 6 (served as static assets by the Worker)
Once UI design system (`@once-ui-system/core` 1.5) — composable layout primitives
@scalar/api-reference-react — `/docs` route renders the OpenAPI spec interactively
Neon Postgres via `@neondatabase/serverless` HTTP driver + Drizzle ORM 0.38
Drizzle Kit 0.30 — schema push + studio (3 hand-written SQL migrations: subscriptions, api_keys, usage_events)
Stack Auth 2.8 (cookie/JWT for humans) + custom `lja_<keyId>_<secret>` Bearer scheme (agents) with strict precedence
Stripe 22 metered subscriptions with dual `STRIPE_MODE=test|live` mode switching + canonical-JSON + sha256 idempotency
Cloudflare R2 — export artifact bucket with 1-day lifecycle, HMAC-signed presigned download URLs (1h TTL)
Cloudflare KV — SWR cache with `{HIT, REVALIDATING, MISS}` headers + KV-backed cost-class-aware per-minute rate limiter (5 points for `POST /api/jobs`/`/api/export`, 1 elsewhere)
ULID 3.0 — `req_<26-char>` IDs threaded through `X-Request-Id` header, every JSON-line log, and problem+json `trace_id`

RFC 7807 `application/problem+json` errors with stable machine codes (`unauthorized`, `forbidden`, `not_found`, `validation_error`, `quota_exceeded`, `rate_limited`, `internal_error`, `invalid_token`, `expired_token`, `export_expired`)
Pulumi TypeScript (`infra/`) — replaces wrangler.jsonc as the primary deploy path; `deploy:wrangler` kept as fallback
node --test --experimental-transform-types — unit tests colocated next to source + cross-cutting `worker/contract.test.ts` using in-process `app.fetch` dispatch
jose 5 (JWT verify) · ulid 3 · zod 3.25 (validation everywhere) · json2csv (export)
TypeScript strict mode everywhere; ESLint 8 + react-hooks 7.1
keywords: Same operations via UI / HTTP API / MCP / CLI
MCP Streamable HTTP server with 11 locked tools
OpenAPI 3.1 from Hono Zod schemas (single source of truth)
Stripe-integrated metered usage events with idempotency
Pulumi + CrossGuard IaC with no-destroy-prod policy
RFC 7807 problem+json with stable machine codes
SWR cache + cost-class-aware KV rate limiter
Contract tests via in-process app.fetch dispatch
