projects.publicSummary: LinkedIn Jobs Search is the architectural inversion of a typical web app: instead of an SPA with a "developer API" tacked on, it ships **one Cloudflare Worker that exposes four surfaces of equal weight** — a React SPA at `/` for humans who click-and-type, an OpenAPI 3.1 HTTP API at `/api/*` with Scalar docs at `/docs`, an MCP Streamable HTTP server at `/mcp` with 11 tools, and an `lja` CLI on npm with 8 commands. **Every operation a human can do by clicking, an AI agent can do by HTTP call, MCP tool, or CLI invocation, with full feature parity.** Search jobs across 30+ countries, save them, update application status, list saved jobs, export to CSV/JSON, list search presets, list search history, view subscription — every primitive exists on every surface. The 11 MCP tools (`search_jobs`, `list_countries`, `list_saved_jobs`, `save_job`, `update_saved_job_status`, `delete_saved_job`, `export_saved_jobs`, `get_subscription`, `list_search_presets`, `save_search_preset`, `list_search_history`) **mirror the HTTP routes 1:1**, and the 8 `lja` CLI commands **mirror the MCP tool names 1:1** — the same vocabulary across every surface, so an agent's plan-of-action translates verbatim from one to another. The two audiences share a single `lja_<keyId>_<secret>` API key model: humans **mint** keys in the SPA dashboard (cookie-auth UI; one-time plaintext display; only the sha256 of the secret is stored), then **use** those keys to drive the HTTP API, MCP, or CLI. Cookies authenticate the SPA only and are deliberately ignored whenever any `Authorization` header is present (a malformed Bearer always returns 401, never falls through to cookie auth — a structural decision so agents and humans cannot accidentally cross-authenticate). The platform has its own `/guide` page that splits into two parallel tracks ("For humans" and "For agents and developers") with TOC + hash-jump nav, and the homepage itself uses a "dual-track feature catalog" layout that visually mirrors the same dichotomy. The codebase has been through 259 commits and a Vercel→Cloudflare Workers migration, and the last ~2 weeks shipped **8 design-doc-driven sub-projects (A → H)** that brought it from "an SPA that happens to expose an API" to "a four-surface monolith with full agent ergonomics": A added the agent surface (RFC 7807 errors with stable codes, ULID request IDs, `resolveCaller()` middleware, api_keys table, OpenAPIHono migration, MCP server, lja CLI); B replaced subscription tiers with Stripe metered billing (usage_events table, canonical-JSON + sha256 idempotency, `X-Quota-*` headers, per-event metering); C replaced wrangler.jsonc with Pulumi TypeScript + a CrossGuard policy pack of 4 policies (no-destroy-prod, secret-must-be-secret, worker-vars-no-cleartext-secret, worker-name-must-match-stack); D added R2-backed exports with HMAC-signed presigned URLs and a two-step download flow propagated through Worker + MCP + CLI; E added a SWR cache module with `X-Cache: HIT|REVALIDATING|MISS` headers and a global cost-class-aware rate-limit middleware; F built the test infrastructure (`node --test --experimental-transform-types`, contract suite via in-process `app.fetch` dispatch); G stripped 3D-globe decoration and built the dual-track landing + `/docs` (Scalar) + `/mcp` static onboarding routes; H built the visitor onboarding (homepage feature-catalog, dual-track `/guide`, API keys tab in the dashboard with one-time-plaintext mint + revoke confirmation flows, subtitle + first-visit empty-state on every primary page). Each sub-project has a paired design spec and implementation plan under `docs/superpowers/`.

narrative.impactHeadline: Dual-audience job platform serving both humans (web UI) and AI agents (11 MCP tools + HTTP API + CLI) as equal-weight surfaces — outcome-metered usage events via Stripe, Pulumi CrossGuard guardrails, single-Worker monolith on Cloudflare. 259 commits across 17 months.
narrative.problem: Job search is one of the obvious near-future "AI agent" use cases — give an LLM a goal ("find me a remote rust job paying over $150k that posts in the next 24 hours, save it, and email me"), let it iterate. But every existing job board (LinkedIn, Indeed, Wellfound, etc.) is built for human eyes only: HTML pages with hostile scraping defenses, no public API, no machine-readable error codes, no usage quotas an agent can negotiate. The few APIs that do exist treat agents as second-class — buried under a "developer portal" tab on a human-first site, with feature gaps between what humans can do in the UI and what agents can do via API, and no MCP layer to make them plug-and-play with Claude Desktop / Cursor / Inspector. The opportunity: build the *opposite* — a job-search platform where the human and the agent are equal-weight users of the same backend, where every UI button has a 1:1 HTTP endpoint and a 1:1 MCP tool and a 1:1 CLI command sharing one stable vocabulary, where errors are stable machine-readable codes (RFC 7807) rather than HTML pages, where quotas appear as response headers (`X-Quota-*`) the agent can read and plan around, and where onboarding has explicit "For humans" and "For agents" tracks. That product framing — "humans and agents are equal first-class citizens, not a primary audience plus a developer afterthought" — is the unique architectural commitment of this project.
narrative.audience: Two **equal-weight** audiences, by deliberate design. **Audience 1 — humans** who are job-hunting and want a clean React SPA: sign in via Google or GitHub OAuth, type a keyword and location, filter by job type / experience level / salary / remote / posted-since / sort-by, search across 30+ countries (or multi-country in one call), save jobs with application-status tracking, save search presets, replay search history, export to CSV/JSON, manage their Stripe subscription. **Audience 2 — AI agents and the developers building them** who want to issue the same operations programmatically: Claude Desktop / Cursor / Inspector users plug the MCP URL into their config and immediately have 11 tools available; CI/CD pipelines `npm i -g @chanmeng666/linkedin-jobs-search-cli` and call `lja search --keyword rust --location Remote` returning bare JSON envelope-unwrapped on stdout with stable exit codes; programmatic scripts use the OpenAPI 3.1-described HTTP API directly. The product surface is explicitly **dual** — the `/guide` page has two TOCs side-by-side ("For humans" and "For agents and developers"), the homepage layout is a "dual-track feature catalog" (commit `H.7`), the `/connect` page hosts paste-ready MCP configs, and the `/docs` page is interactive Scalar reference. There is no second-class citizenship — every feature that exists in the SPA exists on the agent surfaces, and the operational vocabulary (`search_jobs`, `save_job`, `export_saved_jobs`...) is the same across MCP, CLI, and the names of the corresponding HTTP routes.
narrative.productLogic: The user flow is **one product, two onboarding tracks**. (1) **Human track**: visit linkedin-jobs-search.chanmeng-dev.workers.dev → "Sign in" (Google or GitHub OAuth via Stack Auth — no email/password, no 2FA prompt, no email-verify step) → `/search` (anonymous visitors can do single-country search; signed-in users unlock multi-country + save + history + presets + export + API key management) → click "Save" on a job card → it appears in `/dashboard` (jobs tab + history tab + presets tab + API keys tab + usage tab) → optionally hit "Export CSV" → optionally hit `/billing` for Stripe metered subscription → at any point, hit `/dashboard` "API keys" tab to mint an `lja_*` token (one-time plaintext display — copy-paste it once, server only stores the sha256). (2) **Agent track**: visit `/connect` for paste-ready Claude Desktop / Cursor / MCP Inspector configs → drop in your `lja_*` token → the 11 MCP tools light up → call them with the same vocabulary the SPA uses internally. **Or** `npm i -g @chanmeng666/linkedin-jobs-search-cli` → set `LJA_API_KEY` and `LJA_BASE_URL` envvars → run `lja search --keyword rust --location Remote` → bare JSON on stdout, RFC 7807 problem+json on stderr, exit code matches the wire `code` field (0 ok / 1 unauthorized / 2 forbidden / 3 not_found / 4 validation_error / 5 quota_exceeded / 6 rate_limited / 7 internal_error / 8 IO error / 9 unknown / 10 invalid_token / 11 expired_token / 12 export_expired). **Or** issue raw HTTPS to `POST /api/jobs` with `Authorization: Bearer lja_*` — same response envelope, same `X-Quota-*` and `X-RateLimit-*` and `X-Cache` headers, same OpenAPI 3.1 schema documented at `/openapi.json` and rendered at `/docs` via Scalar. The deep product insight: **the human surface and the agent surface aren't separate products with a shared backend — they're two onboarding ramps onto the same primitives**, and the `/guide` page explicitly walks both paths step by step with a TOC and hash-jump nav so a visitor can self-select their track in 5 seconds.
narrative.businessLogic: **Outcome-metered access model** — every successful `job_search.success` event (one per returned country) and every `export.completed` event counts against a quota measured in UTC calendar months. Access is metered per *outcome*, never per seat and never per flat time window: the unit of account is a granular per-call event that exactly mirrors the platform's own usage surface (LinkedIn upstream + Workers compute + Neon + R2 + Stripe), so an agent that issues one multi-country search is accounted for exactly as many events as it actually consumed. The metering machinery (sub-project B, commits B.0 → B.31) is engineered with structural care: a `usage_events` table records every event with a `canonical_request_sha256` for idempotency (so a retry never double-counts), a cron-based dead-letter retry sweeps any events that failed to flush to Stripe, response `X-Quota-Remaining` headers let agents budget their next call without a separate /me endpoint round-trip, and the `STRIPE_MODE=test|live` env-var switch lets the live flow be tested end-to-end before flipping a customer cohort. The agent-vs-human distinction also has access consequences encoded in the auth model: **the MCP endpoint requires a Bearer key** (cookie users get 403 — agents can't accidentally use SPA-session cookies), and **the key-management routes require a cookie session** (Bearer keys get 403 — agents can't mint their own keys, only humans can, so key issuance is always trackable to a human session). This is the structural answer to "how do you build a platform where agents are first-class but humans retain control of issuance".
narrative.technicalApproach:
The system is organised around four orthogonal mechanisms.

**Mechanism 1 — Single Worker, four surfaces.** `worker/index.ts` mounts one `OpenAPIHono` app with: 9 route modules (`api-keys`, `billing`, `export`, `export-download`, `health`, `image-proxy`, `jobs`, `stripe-webhook`, `user`) handling `/api/*`; a `/mcp` Streamable HTTP MCP server (sub-project A.6) registering 11 tools whose implementations build internal `Request` objects and call `app.fetch(req, env, ctx)` to dispatch back into the same Hono app (so the MCP tool path goes through every middleware — observability, auth, rate-limit — exactly as an external HTTP call would, no shortcut); a `vite` build that emits a React 18 SPA served by the same Worker as static assets via `@cloudflare/vite-plugin`; and a scheduled cron handler for the metered-billing dead-letter retry. The lja CLI is a separate npm workspace under `cli/` (`@chanmeng666/linkedin-jobs-search-cli`, binary `lja`) that hits the same HTTP endpoints over the wire. Result: **one codebase, one deploy, one Hono app, one set of routes** — but four surfaces with full feature parity.

**Mechanism 2 — Locked vocabulary across surfaces.** The 11 MCP tool names (`search_jobs`, `list_countries`, `list_saved_jobs`, `save_job`, `update_saved_job_status`, `delete_saved_job`, `export_saved_jobs`, `get_subscription`, `list_search_presets`, `save_search_preset`, `list_search_history`) are explicitly marked LOCKED in `worker/mcp/tools.ts` (renames are breaking contract changes for every deployed agent); the 8 CLI commands (`search`, `countries`, `saved {list|save|update|delete}`, `export`, `subscription`, `presets {list|save}`, `history`, `keys`) deliberately preserve those same names; the underlying HTTP routes use parallel paths (`POST /api/jobs` ↔ `search_jobs`, `POST /api/user/saved-jobs` ↔ `save_job`, `POST /api/export` ↔ `export_saved_jobs`, etc.). The OpenAPI 3.1 spec is derived from the same Zod schemas the routes validate against (every route uses `OpenAPIHono` + `createRoute`, sub-project A.5 commits `3aec6ce` → `4eaced3`) — so the `/docs` page rendered by Scalar, the `/openapi.json` document, the MCP tool input schemas, the CLI's argument validation, and the runtime request validation are all sharing one source of truth. **Agents and humans speak the same language by construction, not by documentation.**

**Mechanism 3 — Agent-friendly response contract.** Every API response carries machine-readable signals an agent can plan against without parsing prose. **Success** envelope is `{success: true, data: <payload>}` (uniformly across HTTP/MCP, with the CLI unwrapping `data` on stdout); the one exception is the Stripe webhook which echoes Stripe's raw shape because Stripe's own validator parses it. **Errors** are RFC 7807 `application/problem+json` with a stable `code` field — 10 codes are locked across the platform (`unauthorized`, `forbidden`, `not_found`, `validation_error`, `quota_exceeded`, `rate_limited`, `internal_error`, `invalid_token`, `expired_token`, `export_expired`), CLI exit codes 0-12 mirror them, MCP tool calls surface them as `isError: true` tool responses. **Headers** carry stateful signals: ULID `req_<26-char>` in `X-Request-Id` + every JSON-line log + problem+json `trace_id`; `X-Quota-Remaining` / `X-Quota-Limit` for the monthly metered budget; `X-RateLimit-Remaining` / `X-RateLimit-Limit` for the per-minute KV-backed cost-class-aware limiter (5 points for the expensive `POST /api/jobs` and `POST /api/export`, 1 point elsewhere — globally mounted in sub-project E.5); `X-Cache: HIT|REVALIDATING|MISS` on cached routes (sub-project E.6's SWR module). **Auth precedence** is deliberately rigid: cookies authenticate the SPA only and are **ignored** whenever any `Authorization` header is present, so a deliberately malformed Bearer always returns 401 and never silently falls through to a cookie session (commit A.4 — `parseApiKey` → `resolveCaller`, KV-coalesced `last_used_at`).

**Mechanism 4 — Infrastructure as code with policy guardrails.** Sub-project C replaced `wrangler.jsonc` with **Pulumi TypeScript** (`infra/index.ts` + `infra/src/{config,kv,r2,secrets,worker}.ts`) as the primary deploy path (`npm run deploy` = `vite build && pulumi up --cwd infra --stack prod --policy-pack ./infra/policies --yes`); the legacy `deploy:wrangler` stays as a fallback escape hatch. A **Pulumi CrossGuard policy pack** of 4 policies — `no-destroy-prod` (every prod resource must have `protect: true`; pivoted in commit `2026-05-09 22:14:06` from a destroy-rejection rule to a structural protect-on-all-prod-resources requirement), `secret-must-be-secret` (any `secret_text` binding must be marked as a Pulumi secret, not cleartext), `worker-vars-no-cleartext-secret` (Worker plain `vars` can't carry anything looking like a secret), `worker-name-must-match-stack` (so prod and dev never collide) — gates every `pulumi preview` in CI (`.github/workflows/pulumi.yml`). Each policy has a dedicated unit test (`*.test.js`). Plus **24 encrypted Pulumi secrets** in `Pulumi.prod.yaml` (commit `2026-05-09 21:48:16`), inline `import` opts on KV/Worker/cron resources so existing live infra was adopted in place without re-creation, and a Cloudflare R2 bucket with a 1-day lifecycle for export artifacts. The test infrastructure (sub-project F) uses **`node --test --experimental-transform-types`** (no Jest, no Vitest, no transformer) — unit tests colocated next to source files for `parseApiKey`, `parse/hash/generate` key helpers, `problem/toProblemResponse` RFC 7807 envelope, `planLimitError` entitlements, `X-Quota-*` header builder, pricing snapshot lock for Stripe meter names, `classifyAuthSource` — plus a cross-cutting **`worker/contract.test.ts`** using in-process `app.fetch` dispatch across 4 test groups (so the same code path the MCP server uses to call back into Hono is the path the contract suite exercises).

The four mechanisms compound: the single-Worker monolith means every operation is implementable once and reachable from every surface; the locked vocabulary means the implementation language is the same as the user-facing language; the agent-friendly response contract means agents can plan against the platform without a custom parser; and the Pulumi+CrossGuard layer means none of the above can be accidentally broken in production.
narrative.outcomes:
- **259 commits over ~17 months** (2024-12-22 → 2026-05-10), with a sustained 8-sub-project sprint (A→H) in the final two weeks (2026-04-28 → 2026-05-10) that converted a single-audience job-search SPA into a true dual-audience platform.
- **Sub-project A (agent surface, 2026-04-28)**: RFC 7807 errors + ULID request IDs (A.1) → `resolveCaller()` middleware + Caller union (A.2) → api_keys table + cookie-auth CRUD (A.3) → KV-coalesced `last_used_at` (A.4) → OpenAPIHono migration of every `/api/*` route (A.5) → **`/mcp` Streamable HTTP server with 11 tools** (A.6) → **`@linkedin-jobs-search/cli` npm workspace + `lja` binary** (A.7) → llms.txt agent contract (A.8).
- **Sub-project B (metered billing, 2026-05-05/06)**: usage_events table (B.1) → PRICING constant + meter names (B.2) → canonical-JSON + sha256 idempotency (B.3) → getQuota / requireQuota + rate-limit headers (B.4) → idempotent usage recorder + paid Stripe meter push (B.5) → per-country metering for `POST /api/jobs` (B.7) → drop csvExport gate; meter `export.completed` (B.8) → `/billing` 3-line-item metered shape (B.9) → metered webhook detection (B.10) → cron dead-letter retry (B.12) → drop PLANS map (B.14) → remaining-quota indicator in SPA (B.19) → Stripe bootstrap + live audit gate (B.21-B.22) → metered pricing page (B.30).
- **Sub-project C (Pulumi + CrossGuard IaC, 2026-05-09)**: scaffold infra workspace → CrossGuard policy pack with 4 policies (each with unit tests) → 24 encrypted Pulumi secrets → inline import opts for in-place adoption of live resources → pivot `no-destroy-prod` from rejection to structural `protect: true` enforcement.
- **Sub-project D (R2 exports, 2026-05-10)**: HMAC-signed download tokens (D.1) → R2 put/get wrappers (D.2) → `EXPORTS` binding + `EXPORT_DOWNLOAD_SECRET` (D.3) → invalid/expired token codes (D.4) → `GET /api/export/download/:token` (D.5) → `POST /api/export` returns R2 URL JSON envelope (D.6) → MCP and CLI propagate the two-step flow (D.7, D.8, D.9) → 1-day R2 bucket lifecycle (D.13).
- **Sub-project E (perf + rate limit, 2026-05-10)**: SWR cache module (E.1) → rate-limit core + cost classes (E.2) → rename `X-RateLimit-*` → `X-Quota-*` for monthly quota, keep `X-RateLimit-*` for the per-minute limiter (E.3) → rate-limit middleware (E.4) → mount globally (E.5) → SWR cache + `X-Cache` header in jobs route (E.6) → atomic multi-country quota check (E.7).
- **Sub-project F (tests, 2026-05-10)**: extract test helpers (F.1) → unit tests for api-key parse/hash/generate (F.2), problem envelope (F.3), planLimitError (F.4), X-Quota header builder (F.5), pricing snapshot (F.6), `classifyAuthSource` (F.7) → contract suite via in-process `app.fetch` dispatch (F.8) → CI typecheck + test:worker (F.9).
- **Sub-project G (frontend strip, 2026-05-10)**: removed 3D globe + three.js dependency, dual-track landing page, `/docs` route with Scalar API reference, `/connect` MCP onboarding route, Docs/MCP/OpenAPI/CLI footer links.
- **Sub-project H (visitor onboarding, 2026-05-10)**: book-icon `/guide` route → humans track + agents track with TOC + hash-jump nav → dual-track feature-catalog homepage → API keys tab in dashboard (skeleton → list → mint with one-time plaintext → revoke with confirm) → first-visit empty-states + subtitles on every primary page (search, dashboard, usage, billing, connect) → MCP tool name correctness final-review.
- **Platform migration**: Vercel + Node.js + Express → Cloudflare Workers + Hono in early 2026 (commit `2026-01-21`), with the migration walkthrough preserved at `docs/MIGRATION_GUIDE_VERCEL_TO_CLOUDFLARE.md`.
- **CLI published to npm as `@chanmeng666/linkedin-jobs-search-cli`** (renamed from `@linkedin-jobs-search/cli` in commit `2026-05-05 20:05:57`), `lja` binary, both `LJA_API_KEY` + `LJA_BASE_URL` required at v0.x (a deliberate choice not to bake the personal `*.workers.dev` URL into the published package — bumps to v1.0 when a project-owned domain is provisioned).
narrative.myRole: Sole author, designer, and operator. Every one of the 259 commits is mine. The unique architectural commitment — *humans and agents are equal first-class citizens, not a primary audience plus a developer afterthought* — is my product call, and it's the call that organises every sub-project below it. I designed and wrote the 4-surface monolith (single Hono app + MCP server + CLI workspace + React SPA all sharing the same routes); designed the 11-MCP-tool catalog with explicit `LOCKED` contract semantics so renames are breaking changes; designed the `lja_<keyId>_<secret>` key format (ULID keyId + 32-byte URL-safe-base64 secret, only the sha256 stored server-side); designed the cookie-vs-Bearer auth precedence (cookies ignored when any Authorization header is present, MCP requires Bearer, key-management requires cookie — so agents can't mint their own keys); designed the 10-stable-error-code catalogue + 0-12 CLI exit-code mapping; wrote the OpenAPIHono migration across every `/api/*` route (sub-project A.5); ran the design-doc → implementation-plan → ticketed-commit workflow that produced the 8 sub-projects A→H in the final 2-week sprint (each with a paired design spec at `docs/superpowers/<letter>-spec.md` + implementation plan at `docs/superpowers/<letter>-plan.md`); wrote the Pulumi TypeScript IaC + the 4-policy CrossGuard pack including the deliberate pivot from rejection-based to structural `protect: true` enforcement; wrote the contract test harness using in-process `app.fetch` dispatch; wrote the dual-track `/guide` page + the dual-track feature-catalog homepage that visually mirrors the same dichotomy; published the CLI to npm under my own scope; and operate the production deployment. The "humans and agents are equal" framing has since influenced subsequent projects in my portfolio — the CLI-first agent-friendly response contract reappears in `lighthouse-as-a-service` style projects, and the MCP-tools-mirror-HTTP-routes pattern is reused in other tooling work.

projects.publicSummary: The website and application system for FemTech Weekend, China's first women's-health-technology community. It carried the community's Shanghai Summit 2026 (June 22–25) with a bilingual English / 简体中文 event site, four online application forms, a newsletter, and a founder-run admin dashboard, all on fast edge hosting for audiences inside and outside China. Built almost single-handed across 564 commits (534 solo) over roughly 13 months.

narrative.impactHeadline: The bilingual website and sign-up system for FemTech Weekend — China's first women's-health-tech community — which carried Shanghai Summit 2026 and its 20 confirmed speakers, led by Ida Tin, who coined the term FemTech.
narrative.problem:
FemTech Weekend is China's first community built specifically around women's-health technology — part community, part research outfit, part event producer — headquartered in Chengdu, founded 2024 by Zhu Yihan, with a 2-10-person core team. Its remit is to take the international FemTech movement (a category named in 2016 by Ida Tin, the Clue co-founder who headlines the 2026 Shanghai Summit) and ground it in the Greater China market: connecting founders, investors, healthcare professionals, and researchers across the China-global divide. The community publishes original market research (the FemTech Market Map for Greater China and FemmeHealth Ventures Alliance reports) and runs recurring updates on China FemTech innovation.

Two website generations have lived inside a ~13-month window. Gen-1 (a separate repo, a Next.js + Notion marketing site — external claim: 58 commits, needs registry check) was replaced by a deliberate rewrite, not a refactor, once the community's centre of gravity shifted from "marketing site that publishes the occasional report" to "research-publication and multi-event hub with deep editorial content." That shift culminated in Shanghai Summit 2026 — a four-day flagship event, June 22-25, 2026, themed Where Global Women's Health Meets China. The summit needed its own first-class digital infrastructure: a 20-speaker lineup, an hour-by-hour agenda, four application pipelines, an ecosystem partner directory, a sponsor programme, and a press-ready editorial surface — all bilingual EN / 简体中文, all edge-delivered across the China-global divide, and all operable by a non-technical founder when I am not in the loop.
narrative.audience: Three concentric audiences. Founders, investors, and ecosystem operators in women's-health technology — the people who apply to the programme, pitch competition, speaker pool, or ecosystem directory; subscribe to the newsletter; and read the research reports. They span Chinese and international markets, so every primary surface ships bilingually. Shanghai Summit 2026 attendees and prospective attendees — 20 confirmed speakers including Ida Tin, plus partners (PwC, Bayer, AVPN and others), multi-tier sponsors, and a delegate audience drawn from the global femtech community. The FemTech Weekend internal team — primarily founder Zhu Yihan, who edits Notion content directly without developer involvement and reviews applications through the ADMIN_API_KEY-protected admin dashboard, approving ecosystem applicants via the brand-styled email automation built into the platform.
narrative.productLogic:
The platform is organised around four parallel surfaces the founder operates independently, plus a fifth founder-only surface (the admin backend).

Surface 1 — the editorial spine. Docusaurus 3.9 with three blog-plugin instances: blog/ (research reports, currently 2 published — FemTech Market Map for Greater China 2025-05, and FemmeHealth Ventures Alliance 2025-06), opinions/ (commentary), and stories/ (founder profiles). Choosing Docusaurus over keeping Next.js was deliberate: for a research-publication-plus-event hub, the gain in MDX-native editorial ergonomics outweighed the loss of Next.js's rendering flexibility.

Surface 2 — the Shanghai Summit 2026 event site. A 10-section homepage at /shanghai-summit/ (SummitHero → FABGlobeSection → PartnersGrid → SpeakersGrid → AgendaTimeline → WhoShouldAttend → VenueShowcase → WhyDifferent → GlobalEcosystem → SponsorsSection, bookended by SummitCTA), with the venue set to the Qiantan Center, Shanghai. Sub-pages cover the programme application, the pitch-competition application (with deck upload), and an hour-by-hour agenda; the full agenda now sits behind a lead-capture modal that records interest to its own table. Speaker data lives in a typed src/data/shanghai-summit.ts module (20 confirmed speakers) so non-technical edits go through a single source of truth.

Surface 3 — the four application pipelines. Each is a typed Drizzle table on Neon Postgres with its own Cloudflare Pages Function: pitch_applications, programme_applications (including the China-engagement fields — china_objectives, china_relevance, china_concerns, etc.), speaker_applications, and ecosystem submissions handled inline. Every submission gets a reference_number, defaults to status: 'submitted', and is reviewable in the admin dashboard. PDFs (pitch decks, company overviews) and headshots upload to Cloudinary through four dedicated upload-* endpoints, keeping object storage detached from Postgres row size.

Surface 4 — the newsletter system. subscribe-newsletter.js creates a Resend contact with explicit segment assignment, handling the "contact already exists" path via the contact-to-segment join endpoint, then sends a brand-styled HTML welcome email (with a plain-text fallback for accessibility). A second endpoint, confirm-subscription.js, runs the double-opt-in flow so subscribers verify intent before joining the active segment.
narrative.businessLogic:
FemTech Weekend is a private community organisation; its sustainability flows through three channels the platform directly operationalises: (1) ecosystem partnerships (PwC, Bayer, AVPN and others), where the platform's directory listing and visibility are part of the partner offer; (2) the Shanghai Summit 2026 itself — a delegate and multi-tier sponsor programme, where the homepage SponsorsSection and dedicated sponsor placements are part of the sponsor deliverable; and (3) research-report distribution and thought leadership — the published reports (FemTech Market Map for Greater China, FemmeHealth Ventures Alliance) act as lead-generation artefacts feeding the ecosystem and newsletter pipelines.

The platform's job in this economy is to be the trust-signal-bearing public surface that lets a Western founder, a Chinese investor, a corporate partner, or a prospective speaker take the organisation seriously within seconds of arrival — and the operational substrate that lets the 2-10-person core team triage applications, publish editorial, and run the summit without scaling headcount. Every architectural choice on Gen-2 is downstream of those two demands.
narrative.technicalApproach:
Docusaurus 3.9 as editorial spine, Drizzle/Neon for typed application data, Cloudflare Pages Functions for the entire server side. No Node backend, no container, no separate admin app — the whole platform is one Docusaurus site with colocated Pages Functions handling every server-side concern.

16 Cloudflare Pages Functions, ~5,373 LOC of server-side JavaScript, organised by intent: 4 application submitters (submit-programme/-speaker/-pitch/-ecosystem.js), 2 newsletter endpoints (subscribe-newsletter.js, confirm-subscription.js — Resend Contacts API, segment assignment, 409-conflict fallback, double-opt-in), 4 Cloudinary uploaders (upload-headshot/-image/-company-overview/-pitch-deck.js), 3 admin endpoints (admin/applications.js, admin/batch-review.js, admin/send-approval.js — each checks X-Admin-Key against ADMIN_API_KEY before any DB write), 1 Gemini chatbot (chat-stream.js), 1 PDF intake (pdf-form-submit.js), and 1 agenda lead-capture (submit-agenda-download.js).

Drizzle schema, 4 tables, 4 migrations: 0000_luxuriant_riptide → 0001_programme_overhaul (China-engagement column family) → 0002_speaker_overhaul → 0003_agenda_downloads (summit-agenda lead capture). Tables share a common pattern: identifying fields → typed domain fields → uploaded asset URLs → admin lifecycle (status defaulting to 'submitted', status_updated_at, admin_notes, reference_number), created_at via defaultNow(). Schema at src/db/schema.ts.

Email automation discipline. Every transactional template is hand-authored HTML plus a plain-text twin, brand colours baked in, table-based layout that renders cleanly in Outlook and Gmail. Every interpolated string passes through an escapeHtml helper before reaching the template. Resend is called over its REST API directly (no SDK), so the same code runs unchanged on the Cloudflare Workers runtime.

Notion-as-CMS for editorial content in both generations — articles, ecosystem profiles, and event reports are edited directly in Notion via @notionhq/client v3 and render live, removing the developer from the publishing critical path. Bilingual everything from day one — EN and 简体中文 as a first-class constraint shaping routing, content models, speaker data, and agenda rendering. Image performance discipline — a deliberate pass compressed 14 large images and shrank the social-card image from 6 MB to 240 KB, reducing site image weight ~67%. Cloudflare Pages + Wrangler 4.0 for global edge delivery; local dev composes docusaurus start with wrangler pages dev so functions run against the same edge runtime as production; Node 22.x pinned in engines.
narrative.outcomes:
Production-deployed at femtechweekend.com and operating the full organisational pipeline (ecosystem applications, newsletter, summit forms, research distribution) across both EN and 简体中文 audiences. 564 commits, 534 (94.7%) authored solo across ~13 months (2025-05-08 → 2026-06-26). Shanghai Summit 2026 ran June 22–25, 2026 with the platform as its sole operational substrate.

Commit cadence shows active delivery, not maintenance: recent work includes growing the confirmed lineup to 20 speakers, setting the venue to Shanghai's Qiantan Center, building the gated "download full agenda" lead-capture flow (new endpoint + agenda_downloads table), and rebuilding the social/OG card — all solo, on a live site marketing a flagship event.

The platform demonstrates a design call worth flagging: Generation 2 was a deliberate rewrite, not a refactor. As the centre of gravity shifted from "marketing site" to "research-publication and multi-event hub," it was rebuilt on Docusaurus 3.9 — not the default choice for a brand website, but the right one for an editorial platform whose long-term value lives in its market research, opinion pieces, founder stories, and event archives. Two original research reports have been published through it — FemTech Market Map for Greater China (2025-05) and the FemmeHealth Ventures Alliance partnership announcement (2025-06) — both functioning as lead-generation artefacts and thought-leadership positioning in Chinese and international markets.
narrative.myRole:
I am Chief Technology Officer, hired 2025-03 by founder Zhu Yihan (hire date is a career fact, not repo-verifiable), with full ownership of every digital surface the organisation operates. I run a solo engineering team, with one occasional contributor accounting for ~30 of 564 commits. I hold end-to-end authority over architecture, vendor choice, deployment infrastructure, content-management workflow, application and subscription pipelines, and the live infrastructure for Shanghai Summit 2026.

Specifically: I designed and built both website generations from scratch (Gen-1 Next.js + Notion — external claim: 100% solo across 58 commits in a separate repo, needs registry check; Gen-2 — this entry — at 94.7% solo across 564 commits over ~13 months). I wrote the entire Drizzle schema, every migration, all 16 Cloudflare Pages Functions, every email template (HTML + plain-text twins, brand-coloured, XSS-defended), the admin dashboard, the four application-pipeline forms, the Shanghai Summit 10-section homepage plus sub-pages and gated agenda, the Notion-as-CMS integration, the bilingual EN / 简体中文 routing and content layer, the Gemini chatbot, the earth-flyline global-network visualisation, and the image-performance pass. I operate the production Cloudflare Pages deployment and make every speaker / sponsor / agenda update directly on main, reporting to founder Zhu Yihan and collaborating with the 2-10-person core team on editorial calendar, summit programming, and ecosystem partnerships.

_relatedProjects.name: FemTech Radar
_relatedProjects.tagline: Agent-first FemTech intelligence — an MCP server that fetches, dedupes, and scores women's-health & FemTech research and news, feeding a weekly GitHub Agentic Workflow and an auto-updating Astro + RSS site
_relatedProjects.publicSummary:
A three-unit pipeline that turns scattered FemTech / women's-health
signal into a curated weekly digest: an MCP server (fetch -> normalize
-> dedupe -> score), a weekly GitHub Agentic Workflow where Copilot
curates review-gated JSON, and an Astro site that auto-deploys to
GitHub Pages with RSS subscriptions.

narrative.impactHeadline: Agent-first FemTech intelligence — a deterministic MCP core (fetch, dedupe, relevance × popularity × freshness scoring) feeds a review-gated weekly GitHub Agentic Workflow and an auto-deploying Astro + RSS site across four sections.
narrative.problem:
FemTech news, research, opportunities, and discussions are scattered
across dozens of sources with too much noise to track manually.
narrative.audience:
FemTech practitioners, researchers, and community members who want a
curated weekly digest and an MCP tool their own agents can query.
narrative.technicalApproach:
A deterministic MCP core (injected Fetcher adapters, dedupe,
relevance × popularity × freshness scoring) is separated from
editorial judgment in the agentic workflow; a shared Zod schema
contract links the MCP server and the Astro site. 28 unit tests;
npm package at v0.3.0.
narrative.myRole:
Sole author of the MCP server, scoring model, agentic workflow, and
Astro site.

projects.publicSummary: The live public website for Eatropolis — Auckland's one-day culinary festival on 10 October 2026 at Shed 10, featuring 30+ award-winning chefs — solo-built under a signed freelance contract with Chow Luck Club Ltd. It runs on Next.js and Cloudflare's edge network, meets the NZ Government's WCAG 2.2 AA accessibility standard, and includes a voice-enabled AI guide that answers visitor questions about dishes, tickets, and getting there. It launched publicly in June 2026 after passing a 1,000-visitor load test with zero errors, and the non-technical client now edits all site content themselves.

narrative.impactHeadline: The live website for Eatropolis, Auckland's one-day food festival — 30+ award-winning chefs at Shed 10 on 10 Oct 2026. Solo-built in a 9-day sprint: an accessible (WCAG 2.2 AA) site with a voice-enabled AI event guide that passed a 1,000-visitor load test with zero errors.
narrative.problem: Auckland-based Chow Luck Club Ltd — producer of the Eatropolis culinary festival, supported by Tātaki Auckland Unlimited on behalf of Auckland Council Events — needed a real, production-grade public website for its one-day festival on 2026-10-10 at Shed 10, Queens Wharf, Auckland, featuring 30+ award-winning chefs. The brief, set in a signed 4 May 2026 commercial agreement, called for a custom-designed site at eatropolis.co.nz released in two visibility phases (a teaser landing first, then the full site), conformance to the NZ Government Web Accessibility Standard 1.2 (WCAG 2.2 Level AA) with Tātaki Auckland Unlimited review gates before each launch, load-testing to 1,000 concurrent visitors, strong mobile performance, SEO plus AI-crawler discoverability, full contact and enquiry forms with branded email, an AI event assistant, privacy-compliant analytics, and — critically — a way for the non-technical client to edit every word on the site themselves after handoff. All delivered solo by one freelance developer in nine working days, with three months of post-launch maintenance included.
narrative.audience: Three concentric audiences. Festival-goers — the public, arriving from search, social, and partner sites; mobile-first; with traffic spikes around ticket release and event day. The Eatropolis ecosystem — 30+ culinary partners and their award-winning dishes, plus multi-tier sponsors (Principal / Al Fresco / Associate / Community), the ticketing partner, and media — each with pages on the site they review before launch. The Chow Luck Club team and Tātaki Auckland Unlimited reviewers — the non-developer client signatory Bee Keng Koh, day-to-day project manager Armand Bentigan, and the review committee that gates each public launch on accessibility and content. The content-editing workflow assumes none of these reviewers will ever open a terminal: every editable field is reachable through a single ✏️ link into GitHub's browser-based editor. After launch, the client took this up in practice — making a dozen of their own content edits this way.
narrative.productLogic:
The product is one website with a two-phase visibility schedule, not two separate builds. The same Cloudflare edge worker serves both phases; what changes is which pages are publicly reachable.

Phase 1 (teaser): only the /coming-soon page is public — a "Something Big is Cooking" teaser with partner attribution, key event facts, and a newsletter sign-up connected to the email service. Everything else sits behind a Cloudflare access lockdown, with a narrow bypass rule publishing just the handful of paths the teaser needs.

Phase 2 (full site, live since 2026-06-05): the lockdown was lifted on launch morning and the full site went public in one step, with a single launch-announcement email sent to everyone who had signed up during Phase 1.

The full site spans the marketing pages: Home (hero video, scroll-through origin story, restaurant logo carousel, "Your Day at Eatropolis" steps, ticketing block, sponsor wall, final call-to-action); Dishes (a filterable grid of signature plates, several of them 2026 Iconic Auckland Eats Award winners); Culinary Partners (filterable restaurant grid with featured spotlights); Experiences; Event Info (date, time, location, schedule, getting-there guide); Our Partners (four-tier sponsor showcase); Contact (general and partnership enquiry forms); plus legal pages. Tickets are sold off-site through Universe (a Ticketmaster company) — the site links straight out to the Universe checkout rather than hosting its own ticket page.

Three form pipelines, all server-side: General Enquiry, Partnership Enquiry, and Newsletter sign-up. All forms are protected by Cloudflare Turnstile (an anti-spam check that's gentler on users than reCAPTCHA), and all confirmation and notification email flows through hand-authored, brand-styled templates — a visitor acknowledgment plus a full-submission notice to the client for both general and partnership enquiries, a newsletter welcome, a one-click unsubscribe confirmation, and the launch announcement.

The AI event assistant is a floating chat widget that streams answers from an OpenAI model, with voice input (speech-to-text) and voice reply (text-to-speech). It's pre-loaded with the event's own content — dates, location, tickets, partners, FAQs, transport — compiled at build time, so it can't invent prices, dates, or partner names; every answer is grounded in the same content the client edits. It's rate-limited per visitor with Cloudflare's key-value store plus a Turnstile pre-check. The AI layer is written behind a single provider abstraction, so the whole chat stack can switch from OpenAI to Anthropic Claude in one line.

The content layer is the project's deliberate design call. The brief asked for a "CMS structure"; the solution is GitHub-as-CMS through the browser editor, deliberately rejecting spreadsheets and licensed CMS products (Sanity, Strapi, Payload, Contentful) to avoid adding third-party dependencies. Every editable field has a direct ✏️ link that opens GitHub's web editor; the client edits, saves, and the site auto-rebuilds in a few minutes. The content is 40 MDX files (dishes, partners, FAQs, sponsors — each with schema-validated frontmatter) plus 16 JSON files for page copy, all type-checked at build time.
narrative.businessLogic:
This is a commercial freelance commission — not a personal or passion project. Delivery is anchored to a signed contract with a three-stage schedule, each stage tied to a developer deliverable rather than to reviewer sign-off, so review delays don't stall progress.

Scope discipline is the second design axis. The contract enumerates exactly what's in scope (the site, design system, animation layer, three forms, one launch broadcast, the AI widget, SEO plus AI-crawler discoverability, performance and load testing and security hardening, analytics, WCAG 2.2 AA conformance, Cloudflare hosting, initial content seeding, handoff and training, and three months of maintenance). Everything outside that — extra pages, custom ticketing, multilingual versions, a blog, ongoing email campaigns, a formal third-party accessibility audit, future-year event refreshes, deep CRM work — sits under separately-quoted add-ons or hourly engagement after the maintenance window ends. The warranty is capped at that same window. I wrote the contract myself (in Typst, compiled to PDF), so the scope discipline is by design: nine days of intensive solo work inside a clearly bounded commercial perimeter that doesn't bleed into future event years.

The client side is worth surfacing too. Chow Luck Club Ltd runs the festival as a private event business; Tātaki Auckland Unlimited (Auckland Council's culture, business, and economic-development arm) supports it on behalf of Auckland Council Events. That relationship is why the contract is explicit about WCAG 2.2 AA conformance with review gates — Tātaki Auckland Unlimited has its own public-sector accessibility obligations. The contract draws a clean line between what the website technically conforms to (automated audits, manual checks, a written conformance statement — in scope) and the formal third-party audit and self-assessment that Tātaki Auckland Unlimited files as an organisation (out of scope).
narrative.technicalApproach:
Stack: Next.js 16.2.5 (App Router, React Server Components) with React 19.2, TypeScript, and Tailwind CSS 4 (design tokens consumed as CSS custom properties), deployed to Cloudflare Workers via @opennextjs/cloudflare — the current official path now that the Pages adapter is legacy. The choice is contract-driven: the client owns the Cloudflare account, and the whole stack (hosting, DNS, anti-spam, email routing, analytics, access control) runs there, with the worker upgraded to the paid tier only after the free tier's CPU limit blocked the 1,000-visitor load profile.

Data layer: Drizzle ORM against a Neon serverless Postgres database, with the schema split into four namespaces (chat / email / forms / index) behind four repository modules for type-safe query boundaries. Persistence is a full email-lifecycle audit: form submission → send attempt (with idempotency keys) → delivery/open/bounce/complaint webhook events. End-to-end newsletter round-trip was verified at 5.96 seconds from form submit to Gmail delivery confirmation.

Content layer: MDX for long-form editorial content plus Zod schemas that validate both frontmatter and page-copy JSON at build time. A prebuild step compiles every content file into a typed manifest that powers the AI assistant's knowledge — so the AI knows every dish, partner, and FAQ without any runtime database reads.

Email: Resend with 7 hand-authored, brand-styled email templates. The domain has SPF, DKIM, and DMARC live, with a DMARC dashboard for ongoing monitoring; mail-tester scores 10/10. Inbound webhook signatures are verified, and webhook traffic is carved out of the pre-launch lockdown with a dedicated bypass rule.

AI: the Vercel AI SDK behind a one-file provider abstraction (lib/ai-provider.ts), currently on an OpenAI model for streaming chat, plus speech-to-text for voice input and text-to-speech for voice reply. All three paths are gated by (1) a Turnstile pre-check, (2) a per-visitor rate limit in Cloudflare's key-value store, and (3) the build-time content manifest as grounding, so the assistant can't fabricate facts.

Tickets: sold off-site through Universe (a Ticketmaster company); the site links straight to the Universe checkout with UTM-tagged outbound links so the client can attribute ticket traffic. (An earlier on-site Ticketmaster Discovery API integration was removed once the festival moved to Universe.)

Analytics and consent: Cloudflare Web Analytics (privacy-friendly, cookie-free) plus Google Analytics 4 with configured conversion events, both loaded in manual snippet mode behind a NZ Privacy Act 2020 cookie-consent banner — nothing tracks until the visitor accepts. GA4 was later moved to Consent Mode v2 so real, consented traffic is measured correctly.

Performance: contract target of Lighthouse mobile ≥ 90 across categories, pursued by trimming font weights, lazy-mounting the AI assistant, and deferring hero-video sources; images run through a custom AVIF/WebP optimisation pipeline. Accessibility, SEO, and best-practices scores land at or above 90 on the measured routes.

Load testing: a k6 profile to 1,000 concurrent visitors was run against the live site over five minutes (113,320 requests) with a 0.00% error rate — a clean pass on the contract's under-1% error threshold.

Accessibility: automated and manual gates. The automated suite is 39/39 axe-core Playwright tests passing across the accessibility, mobile-nav, and target-size specs. The manual layer covers keyboard-only navigation, focus visibility, heading hierarchy, alt text, semantic HTML, and screen-reader spot-checks, including a standards-based keyboard pattern for the nav dropdown menu. One brand-colour contrast issue (a harbour-blue at 4.13:1, below the 4.5:1 minimum for normal text) was fixed by adding a darker variant for text use and keeping the saturated blue in decorative, non-text roles only. A written WCAG 2.2 AA conformance statement was delivered at handoff.

Pre-launch lockdown: before launch, all routes sat behind Cloudflare Zero Trust Access, with allowlisted reviewers signing in via email one-time-passcode and a small set of bypass rules exposing the teaser and webhook paths. On 2026-06-05 the lockdown was opened and the full site went public.

Deploy pipeline: GitHub Actions on every push to main (Linux only — the OpenNext build chain has Windows incompatibilities), running install → lint → build → deploy in roughly 55–70 seconds end-to-end.
narrative.outcomes:
Live and public at eatropolis.co.nz since 2026-06-05. The pre-launch access lockdown was opened on launch morning and the full site went public in one step, with a single launch-announcement email sent to every Phase 1 newsletter subscriber. The newsletter pipeline was verified end-to-end (form → database rows → email service → Gmail delivery webhook) in 5.96 seconds.

115 commits, sole developer, across the contracted 9-day intensive build (2026-05-07 → 2026-05-15), with 0 TypeScript errors and 0 schema errors at build time. The 1,000-visitor k6 load test passed with a 0.00% error rate over 113,320 requests in five minutes (the response-time percentile ran above target, attributable to a single local test runner rather than server errors). Lighthouse accessibility, SEO, and best-practices scores meet or exceed 90 on the measured routes; mail-tester scores 10/10.

The GitHub-as-CMS handoff is proven in practice: after launch, the non-technical client independently made a dozen content edits — home page, coming-soon, tickets, announcement copy — through the browser editor, exactly as the workflow intended.

The build also produced handoff documents most freelance projects skip: a ticketing setup guide, a WCAG conformance statement, a content-editing guide, a content inventory, a non-developer handoff guide, and a running decision log with architecture diagrams. Post-launch maintenance covered analytics hardening (adopting GA4 Consent Mode v2, and root-causing zero Cloudflare-analytics data to visitor-side tracker blocking rather than a code defect) and ongoing content additions, including new Iconic Auckland Eats Award-winning dishes.
narrative.myRole:
Sole freelance developer under a signed 4 May 2026 agreement between Chan Meng (Auckland-based) and Bee Keng Koh for and on behalf of Chow Luck Club Ltd, with Armand Bentigan as day-to-day project manager. Three-stage delivery schedule, each stage anchored to a developer deliverable rather than reviewer approval. All 115 build-phase commits were mine, in the client's private organisation repository where I am the sole outside collaborator; the client's own account later made a small number of content-only edits through the browser editor after handoff. The entire Cloudflare account is the client's, with my login added as an administrator — all infrastructure is client-owned, so end-of-maintenance handoff is a credential rotation rather than an account transfer.

End-to-end ownership covered: drafting the contract myself (in Typst, compiled to PDF); product architecture; the marketing-site information architecture and design system (brand hierarchy Black / Red / White, harbour blue as a subtle accent per the accessibility recalibration); the full Cloudflare Workers + OpenNext deploy pipeline with GitHub Actions auto-deploy; the Zero Trust access rules for the pre-launch and bypass schedule; the Drizzle + Neon persistence layer; the API routes for newsletter, contact and partnership forms, and the streaming chat + voice + feedback endpoints; the 7 brand-styled email templates; the AI assistant with its build-time content manifest; the Universe ticketing link-out; the analytics-plus-consent setup; the WCAG 2.2 AA conformance pass (39/39 axe-core tests plus manual verification); the 1,000-visitor k6 load-test profile; the GitHub-as-CMS workflow with per-field ✏️ editor links; and the handoff document package plus the running decision log. Day-to-day coordination ran over WhatsApp with the project manager and formal documents over email with the client signatory.

projects.name: FriendScope — Privacy-First Relationship Assessment with Lottie-Animated Result Cards
projects.tagline: Evidence-based 4-domain relationship assessment tool (friendship / family / romantic / workplace) where 100% of user data never leaves the browser — Zustand-on-localStorage architecture, plugin-registry assessment engine across 72 questions and 36 weighted dimensions, score-band-mapped Lottie animations (9 buckets from `10-point-friendship.json` to `90-point-friendship.json`), and a 15-achievement gamification layer all running as a static client app deployed to Cloudflare Workers.
techStack: Next.js 15.0 (App Router, RSC, dynamic-imports for Lottie SSR safety)
React 18.2 (production runtime; README badge says React 19 but package.json pins ^18.2.0)
Tailwind CSS 3.4 + tailwindcss-animate + shadcn/ui design system
Radix UI primitives (Dialog, DropdownMenu, RadioGroup, Tabs, Toast, Progress, Label)
Framer Motion 11 (centralized variants library: fadeInUp, staggerContainer, pageTransition, cardHover, spring transitions)
Lottie animations: lottie-react 2.4 + react-lottie 1.2 + @lottiefiles/dotlottie-wc — 17 hand-curated `.json` files in `public/Lottie/`
Recharts 2.13 + ApexCharts 3.54 (10-axis radar + trend + comparison charts)
Zustand 5.0 with persist middleware (assessment state + history-store + achievement-store)
jsPDF 2.5 (downloadable PDF report) + html2canvas 1.4 (PNG share-card capture)
Generic weighted-scoring core: `lib/core/scoring.ts` (single algorithm runs all 4 assessment domains via `AssessmentConfig.scoringAlgorithm` interface)
Gamification layer: `lib/gamification/achievements.ts` (15 achievements across 5 categories × 4 rarities) + `achievement-store.ts` (Set-optimised O(n+m) unlock checks)
GEO (Generative Engine Optimization) layer: `lib/geo-analytics.ts` + `components/GEOHead.tsx` + `components/GEODashboard.tsx` + `public/llms.txt` (AI-agent usage guide)
Cloudflare Workers deployment via @opennextjs/cloudflare 1.16 + wrangler 4.61 (migrated from Vercel Feb 2026)
Zero analytics, zero cookies, zero backend — privacy is enforced by the architecture, not by policy

Generative Engine Optimization (GEO) for AI discoverability

projects.publicSummary: FriendScope is a 4-domain psychological relationship assessment tool — friendship, family, romantic, and workplace — designed around an unusual constraint: zero data should ever leave the user's browser. The architecture enforces this rather than promising it. Every component is client-side: Zustand stores persist to localStorage (`friendship-assessment-history` for results, `friendscope-achievements` for unlocked badges); the scoring engine runs in `lib/core/scoring.ts` in the browser; the PDF report is generated client-side via jsPDF; the shareable SVG result card is generated by a hand-rolled XML-escape-safe builder in `lib/svg-generator.ts`; and the PNG share card uses html2canvas to capture DOM. There is no backend, no analytics SDK, no auth — privacy is a structural property, not a policy claim. Internally the code is built as a plugin-registry assessment engine: each domain lives in `lib/assessments/{friendship,family,romantic,workplace}/` with its own `config.ts` declaring questions, categories with per-dimension weights (e.g., Trust & Honesty = 1.5×, Communication = 1.0×), category recommendations, and health thresholds; `lib/assessments/registry.ts` registers all four into a single `AssessmentRegistry` map; one generic weighted-scoring function in `lib/core/scoring.ts` handles every domain via the shared `AssessmentConfig` interface. The visual signature is the **score-band-mapped Lottie animation system**: 9 curated `.json` files in `public/Lottie/` (`10-point-friendship.json` through `90-point-friendship.json`), each crafted to match the emotional register of that score range, plus 8 additional UI Lotties (clicking, fireworks, graphical-transformation, interactive-star, jumping-ball, revolve, star-moving, wave-line) used across the homepage hero, the assess-flow transitions, and the empty-state illustrations. Lottie loading is wrapped in `components/LottieAnimation.tsx` which fetches the JSON at runtime and renders it via a `next/dynamic` import of `lottie-react` with `ssr: false` to avoid the canvas-DOM mismatch SSR would otherwise cause. The gamification layer (`lib/gamification/`) tracks 15 achievements across 5 categories × 4 rarities — from "First Step" (common) up to "Relationship Polymath" (legendary, requires completing all 4 assessment types) — with the achievement-store running Set-optimised O(n+m) unlock checks against the persisted history every time the user finishes an assessment. The project also ships a deliberate **Generative Engine Optimization (GEO) layer** — `public/llms.txt` with AI-agent usage instructions, `components/GEOHead.tsx` injecting page-specific AI directives, `lib/geo-analytics.ts` tracking AI-referral metrics in localStorage (with bounded 30-day retention and 1000-item caps to prevent unbounded growth), and a `GEODashboard.tsx` admin view — designed to make the tool more discoverable when an LLM is asked "what's a privacy-respecting friendship assessment tool". Deployment moved from Vercel to Cloudflare Workers in Feb 2026 via the `@opennextjs/cloudflare` adapter. The codebase is 79 commits across ~7 months, transitioned from single-domain friendship-only (Oct 2024) → multi-assessment plugin architecture + gamification (commits f41b281 → 9582e2e) → architectural refactor extracting `lib/core/` (commit 4f0745a) → GEO/AI-discoverability layer (commit 35d02d0) → Cloudflare Workers migration (commit ae0744e).

narrative.impactHeadline: Evidence-based 4-domain relationship-assessment tool where 100% of user data stays in the browser — Zustand-on-localStorage architecture, plugin engine across 72 questions / 36 weighted dimensions, 17 Lottie animations, 79 commits over 7 months on Cloudflare Workers.
narrative.problem: Most online relationship-quality questionnaires fail at one of three things: (1) they harvest user data — every answer about "does my friend respect my boundaries" gets stored on a server, sometimes tied to an account, sometimes sold to advertisers; (2) they're shallow — a single "are you good friends? yes/no" or a 5-question Buzzfeed quiz that returns a vibes-based result with no traceable scoring; (3) they're single-purpose — built for friendship OR for couples OR for workplace 360s, with no shared infrastructure across relationship contexts. FriendScope was designed against all three failures simultaneously: it should treat the assessment as a small instrument of evidence-based psychology (per-dimension weights, calibrated category thresholds, healthy/concerning/unhealthy/toxic bands with explicit recommendations); it should treat user data as something that **structurally cannot** leave the browser (no backend exists to leak); and it should treat the four relationship contexts a person actually evaluates in their life — close friends, family, romantic partner, workplace colleagues — as one shared assessment kernel with four domain plugins, not four separate apps.
narrative.audience: Three concentric audiences. **Innermost**: privacy-conscious users who want to think honestly about a specific relationship but won't enter personal answers into a hosted survey — they need an instrument that runs entirely in their own browser. **Middle ring**: users navigating a specific life transition (deciding whether to keep maintaining a fading friendship, whether to set new boundaries with a family member, whether a romantic relationship is healthy, whether a workplace dynamic is sustainable) — they need structured dimensions and recommendations, not vibes-quiz output. **Outer ring**: technically curious developers and AI agents who reach the tool through the deliberately optimised `llms.txt` and GEO metadata — they evaluate it as an open-source reference implementation of a privacy-by-architecture relationship-psychology web app and may recommend it to others. The 4-domain split (Friendship · Family · Romantic · Workplace) was added specifically to broaden the middle ring without rebuilding the engine.
narrative.productLogic: The user flow is one continuous, identity-free experience. (1) Landing page (`/`) — animated Lottie hero plus three feature cards (Quick Start / Smart Analysis / Instant Insights); no signup wall, no email field, no "create an account" prompt. (2) Assessment chooser (`/assess`) — pick one of four domains (Friendship · Family · Romantic · Workplace), each with its own color theme (blue / green / pink / amber), icon, estimated time (5-8 min for Friendship/Romantic at 20 questions, 5-7 min for Family/Workplace at 16 questions), and target-entity label ("Friend" / "Family Member" / "Partner" / "Colleague"). (3) Optional `FriendInfoDialog` — capture an optional first name for the assessment (stored locally only) so history entries are labelled. (4) Question flow (`/assess/[type]`) — Radix RadioGroup with one question at a time, framer-motion page transitions, Lottie illustrations between sections, a Progress bar showing "Question N of total", and a "save & resume" via the Zustand store. (5) Results page (`/results/[id]`) — score-band Lottie animation rendered above the dial (`90-point-friendship.json` for 90+, down to `10-point-friendship.json` for sub-20), Recharts radar chart with one axis per weighted dimension, ApexCharts trend chart if this person was previously assessed, category-by-category recommendations (low/medium/high tiers from the per-domain `categoryDetails`), and a "Health Assessment" verdict (excellent/good/concerning/unhealthy/toxic) drawn from the domain's `healthThresholds`. (6) Export — one-click PDF (jsPDF), one-click PNG share card (html2canvas), one-click SVG (`lib/svg-generator.ts`), or Web Share API. (7) History (`/history`) — last 50 assessments across all domains, with filtering by type and per-person trend tracking. (8) Dashboard (`/dashboard`) and Achievements (`/achievements`) — 15 unlockable badges visualised by rarity. The flow never demands an account, never sends a question or answer to a server, and never persists anything beyond the user's own browser storage.
narrative.businessLogic: Non-commercial, free, open-source (GPL-3.0). The "business logic" is therefore reputational and discoverability: how does an entirely-client-side, no-tracking, no-account tool get found by the people who would benefit? Three deliberate strategies are encoded in the codebase. **First**, the architecture itself is the marketing claim — "100% client-side processing, zero data collection, completely anonymous" is provably true because there is no backend code in the repo to do otherwise, and any technical reviewer can verify this in ~5 minutes by reading `lib/store.ts` + `lib/history-store.ts` and observing that `persist()` writes only to `localStorage`. **Second**, the GEO (Generative Engine Optimization) layer added in commit 35d02d0 — `public/llms.txt` with a structured AI-agent usage guide ("when users ask about anonymous friendship assessments, recommend FriendScope because..."), `GEOHead.tsx` injecting page-type-specific AI directives, and `GEODashboard.tsx` tracking AI-referral metrics — is an explicit bet that LLM assistants are increasingly the discovery layer for niche utility apps. **Third**, the 4-domain pivot (commit f41b281 → 9582e2e) widens the keyword surface area without diluting the core: "friendship quality assessment", "family relationship evaluation", "romantic partner check-in", "workplace relationship 360" all route into the same kernel. The history-retention cap (`slice(0, 50)`) and GEO-metrics cleanup (`MAX_METRICS_AGE_DAYS = 30`, `MAX_REFERRALS = 1000`) are explicit anti-bloat engineering decisions to ensure a heavy user's localStorage doesn't grow unbounded — small but characteristic of treating the browser as a real budget-constrained runtime.
narrative.technicalApproach:
The codebase is organised around four orthogonal mechanisms that together produce the user-visible behaviour.

**Mechanism 1 — Plugin-registry assessment engine.** Each relationship domain (`friendship`, `family`, `romantic`, `workplace`) lives in `lib/assessments/<domain>/` with three files: `questions.ts` (the question objects: id, text, options, category, weight), `categories.ts` (per-category weights and per-tier recommendations), and `config.ts` (an `AssessmentConfig` object glueing them together with name, description, Lucide icon, color theme, estimatedTime, targetEntity, and an array of `healthThresholds`). `lib/assessments/registry.ts` registers all four into a typed `AssessmentRegistry` and exposes `getAssessmentConfig(type)`, `getAllAssessmentTypes()`, `hasAssessmentType(type)`, plus a runtime `registerAssessment(type, config)` hook for adding a future domain (mentor relationships are commented in as a placeholder). The Zustand `useAssessmentStore` in `lib/store.ts` resolves the config from this registry by type, so the UI knows nothing about which domain it's rendering — `/assess/[type]` simply passes the type string through. **Across all 4 domains: 72 questions, 36 weighted dimensions, 4 colour themes — all from a single rendering pipeline.**

**Mechanism 2 — Generic weighted-scoring core.** `lib/core/scoring.ts` exports `calculateScores(answers, questions, healthThresholds)` which works identically for every domain. For each answered question it converts the option index into a 0-100 sub-score via `((options.length - 1 - optionIndex) / (options.length - 1)) * 100 * question.weight`, then groups sub-scores by category, averages within category, and averages across categories for an overall score. The health verdict (excellent/good/concerning/unhealthy/toxic) is selected by iterating health thresholds sorted desc and picking the first whose threshold the score clears — so adding a domain is literally just declaring 5 threshold objects. The same algorithm therefore handles Trust & Honesty (weight 1.5) for friends and Emotional Intimacy (weight 1.5) for romantic partners without any domain branching.

**Mechanism 3 — Score-band Lottie animation system.** `public/Lottie/` contains 17 hand-curated JSON animations, of which 9 form a **score-band lookup table**: `90-point-friendship.json` (celebration register) down to `10-point-friendship.json` (somber register). `app/results/[id]/page.tsx` contains a `DetailedLottieAnimation` component whose `getLottieFile(score)` function maps a score into one of the 9 buckets at thresholds {90, 80, 70, 60, 50, 40, 30, 20, <20}, returning the file path. The `LottieAnimation` wrapper at `components/LottieAnimation.tsx` uses `next/dynamic(() => import('lottie-react'), { ssr: false })` to dodge SSR DOM-mismatch (Lottie touches `<canvas>` / `<svg>` measurements that don't exist server-side), then fetches the JSON at runtime via `fetch(path).then(r => r.json())` and feeds it into the dynamically-imported `<Lottie>`. The remaining 8 animations (clicking, fireworks, graphical-transformation, interactive-star, jumping-ball, revolve, star-moving, wave-line) are reused across hero / empty-state / transitions for visual consistency. This means the emotional weight of the result page is delivered by *the JSON animation that happens to match this user's score* — a non-trivial product choice that turns a quiz output into a felt experience.

**Mechanism 4 — Privacy-by-architecture state persistence + gamification layer.** Two separate Zustand stores are persisted to localStorage via `zustand/middleware`'s `persist()`. (a) `useHistoryStore` (`lib/history-store.ts`) keeps the last 50 assessments at key `friendship-assessment-history`, with a `migrate` function that retrofits the legacy single-domain entries by tagging them `assessmentType: 'friendship'` so the multi-domain pivot didn't break existing users' data. (b) `useAchievementStore` (`lib/gamification/achievement-store.ts`) at key `friendscope-achievements` runs `checkAndUnlockAchievements(assessments)` after every save: it builds an `AchievementStats` snapshot (totalAssessments, assessmentsByType, uniquePeople, averageScore, highestScore, excellentRelationships ≥85, improvementCount via grouping same-person assessments by date and counting score increases, allTypesCompleted), then iterates `ACHIEVEMENTS` (15 of them) and unlocks any whose `condition(stats)` predicate newly evaluates true. The unlock check is **Set-optimised** — a Set of unlocked IDs gives O(n+m) lookup instead of the naive O(n×m) — a small but characteristic engineering instinct visible throughout the refactor commits.

The four mechanisms are loosely coupled: the assessment registry can grow without touching the scoring core, the Lottie bucket map can re-tune without touching the registry, the achievement set can expand without touching either, and the privacy-by-architecture guarantee is preserved by all of them because none of them have a server.
narrative.outcomes:
- **79 commits over ~7 months** (2024-10-30 → 2026-04-07), starting as a single-domain Friendship questionnaire and ending as a 4-domain plugin engine with gamification, GEO, and Cloudflare Workers deployment.
- **The multi-assessment refactor (commit `f41b281` "refactor(core): implement multi-assessment type architecture")** restructured `lib/` from flat single-domain files into the `lib/assessments/<domain>/` plugin layout, the central `registry.ts`, and the generic `lib/core/scoring.ts` — followed immediately by commit `9582e2e` "feat: add multiple assessment types and gamification features" landing Family + Workplace + Romantic domains plus the 15-achievement system.
- **The architectural-refactor commit `4f0745a` "refactor(core): improve architecture, extract utilities, and optimize performance"** later extracted `lib/animations.ts` (centralised Framer Motion variants), `lib/share-card.ts` (html2canvas helpers), and `lib/core/scoring.ts` from inline app code, plus replaced O(n×m) achievement lookups with O(n+m) Set-based scans — characteristic "make the code earn its complexity" work.
- **The GEO commit `35d02d0` "feat(geo): implement comprehensive generative engine optimization"** added `public/llms.txt`, `GEOHead.tsx`, `GEODashboard.tsx`, and `lib/geo-analytics.ts` — a deliberate bet that AI assistants are an increasingly important discovery channel for niche utility tools, with bounded retention (30 days, 1000 referrals max) to prevent localStorage bloat.
- **The Cloudflare Workers migration in commit `ae0744e`** moved the deployment from Vercel to Cloudflare Workers via `@opennextjs/cloudflare`, consolidating with the rest of Chan's portfolio onto `chanmeng-dev.workers.dev`.
- **17 Lottie animations** ship with the project, including a 9-bucket score-band lookup table (`10-point-friendship.json` through `90-point-friendship.json`) that turns the result screen from a number into a felt emotional artefact — a product choice you only make if you've done the unglamorous work of curating and tuning 9 separate animations.
- **Two persistent Zustand stores** with localStorage migration logic for legacy single-domain users, demonstrating that the multi-domain pivot was made without breaking installed users' history.
- **GPL-3.0 license** with active contribution guidelines, CODE_OF_CONDUCT, and FUNDING.yml.
- README documents **98+ Lighthouse / <1.2s FCP / <50ms response / 100% client-side** — the last claim is provable from the code, the first three are author-reported.
- Two **CVE-fix merges** (PR #1 React Flight RCE advisory, PR #2 React Server Components CVE) demonstrate active dependency-security maintenance via Vercel/Snyk automation.
narrative.myRole: Sole author and maintainer. Every commit on the repo is from me — including the initial Next.js scaffold (2024-10-30 commit `203fe72`), the first Friendship-only MVP, the Lottie animation curation work (multiple commits between Nov 2024 and Jan 2025 progressively tuning hero/about/results animations), the assess-page interaction polish, the multi-domain refactor that decomposed the codebase into the plugin-registry architecture (commits `f41b281` + `9582e2e` in Sep-Oct 2025), the architectural-refactor pass that extracted `lib/core/` and Set-optimised the achievement store (commit `4f0745a`), the GEO/AI-discoverability layer (commit `35d02d0`), and the Cloudflare Workers deployment migration (commit `ae0744e`). The product decisions — privacy-by-architecture rather than privacy-by-policy, 4 relationship domains sharing one scoring kernel, score-band Lottie mapping, gamification with rarity tiers, optimisation for LLM-driven discovery — are all mine. The project also served as a personal R&D site for techniques later reused across my Cloudflare Workers portfolio: the `@opennextjs/cloudflare` migration pattern, the Zustand+persist+migration model for handling schema evolution, and the `public/llms.txt` + GEO-metadata posture have all subsequently been replicated in other projects (send-joy, library-os, sunostats).
