projects.publicSummary:
A **no-code visual abstraction layer over the Resend API**, built
so that **non-technical users never have to read Resend's docs,
open Resend's dashboard, or learn merge-field syntax** to send
beautiful personalised email campaigns. Started life on Christmas
Eve 2025 as a one-shot CLI for sending Christmas greetings; four
days later, on Dec 27 2025, the codebase pivoted in a single
commit (`fe16fdd`: *"transform CLI tool into visual web
application platform"*) into a full Next.js 15 web app with a
drag-and-drop block-based template editor (7 block types,
`@dnd-kit`), a 5-step sending wizard, a Resend Audiences sync,
and a deliberately authored **onboarding + context-aware help
system "for non-technical users"** (commit `782731c`). Wraps four
Resend API surfaces — `/emails`, `/templates`, `/audiences`, and
`/audiences/.../contacts` — behind a unified UI so the user
never sees a request body. Ships **6 Neobrutalism preset
templates**, real-time desktop/mobile preview, undo/redo with
50-state history, and CSV-import contact management.
Privacy-first: all user data (templates, contacts, settings)
lives in browser localStorage, and the Resend API key is passed
per-request rather than persisted on any server. Live at
[sendjoy.chanmeng-dev.workers.dev](https://sendjoy.chanmeng-dev.workers.dev/),
migrated from Vercel to Cloudflare Workers in Feb 2026 with
GitHub Actions auto-deploy.

narrative.impactHeadline: No-code visual abstraction layer over the Resend API — drag-and-drop block editor, 5-step send wizard, Audiences sync, onboarding designed for non-technical users. Born as a Christmas-greeting CLI, pivoted to a full visual platform in 4 days across 43 commits.
narrative.problem:
Resend is the modern transactional-email API of choice for
developers — clean SDK, fast onboarding, MX-record sanity — but
the gap it leaves wide open is **non-technical users**.
Marketers, community managers, school teachers, holiday-card
senders, small-business owners, and event organisers want
exactly what Resend provides (high deliverability, real
sender domains, real bounce handling) but **cannot get
through the API on-ramp**. To send one campaign through
vanilla Resend, a non-technical user has to (1) read SDK docs,
(2) write Node code with the `Resend` client, (3) hand-author
HTML or React Email JSX, (4) understand merge-field syntax
differences between Resend's native templates and their own
templating, (5) manage `Audiences` and `Contacts` through the
REST API directly, and (6) deploy a serverless function or
run scripts locally. Every one of these steps is a wall.
Tools that **do** cater to non-technical users — Mailchimp,
ConvertKit, Substack — are either expensive at small list
sizes, lock the user into their templating, or don't expose
the underlying mail infrastructure (custom domains, API key
rotation, deliverability metrics) that Resend gives away
cheaply.
narrative.audience:
Explicitly **non-technical or semi-technical Resend users**.
The persona stack: (a) marketers and community managers who
want to send a polished campaign through Resend's
deliverability stack but don't write Next.js; (b) holiday
senders who want to send Christmas / Lunar New Year / Birthday
greetings to dozens of friends with personalised names baked
in; (c) small-business owners running newsletters out of
their own domain who don't want Mailchimp branding or pricing;
(d) developers who *can* code but want a visual editor for
speed because they're authoring one-off campaigns rather than
building a templated system. Secondary audience: other
developers who want to study a worked example of wrapping a
commercial API into a no-code platform — the codebase ships
MIT-licensed as a study artefact.
narrative.productLogic:
The product replaces six pieces of Resend's developer
workflow with visual surfaces. **(1) HTML authoring** becomes
a drag-and-drop block editor with 7 block types — Header,
Text, Image, Button, Wishes List, Divider, Footer — each with
a focused property panel; React Email renders to real
email-client-safe HTML on the server. **(2) Templating** is
the visual canvas plus a theme panel with 9+ colour pickers
(`react-colorful`), 600px/375px device preview toggle, and
50-state undo/redo. **(3) Recipient management** becomes a
Contacts page with three import modes — manual entry, CSV
bulk import (Email/FirstName/LastName columns), and a
one-click sync from a Resend Audience (`audienceId` selected
from a dropdown). **(4) Merge fields** become four named
variables — `{{recipientName}}`, `{{senderName}}`,
`{{currentYear}}`, `{{nextYear}}` — with the server-side
renderer normalising between local camelCase syntax and
Resend native UPPER_SNAKE_CASE so a template can use either.
**(5) Sending** becomes a 5-step wizard: pick template -> pick
recipients -> customise variables -> preview the actual
rendered HTML (not a mockup) -> send-and-track with a real
per-recipient success/failure result table; the server hits
Resend per recipient with progress streamed back. **(6) API
infrastructure** is replaced by a Settings page where the
user pastes their Resend API key and verified sender email
once; the key never leaves the browser except as a
per-request header. A welcome-modal **onboarding flow**
(commit `782731c`, *"comprehensive onboarding and help
system for non-technical users"*) walks first-time visitors
through these six surfaces step by step, and every screen
has a context-aware help modal pinned to it.
narrative.businessLogic:
Open source under MIT. The product is **free to self-host**
(deploy to Cloudflare Workers in under a minute via the
`npm run deploy` script and `@opennextjs/cloudflare`) and
free to use on the public instance at
sendjoy.chanmeng-dev.workers.dev (the user supplies their own
Resend API key, so Chan bears no email-delivery cost). The
commercial premise is asymmetric: Resend already monetises
through their own free tier (3,000 emails/month) plus paid
plans for higher volume; SendJoy makes their funnel reachable
to a customer segment Resend can't service directly, which
makes it a complementary product rather than a competing
one. The build-in-public value for Chan: a portfolio-grade
worked example of **"abstract a developer API into a no-code
product"** — a pattern the agentic-AI economy will need
repeatedly as more APIs ship without UX layers. The codebase
is deliberately reusable as a template for wrapping any
other Resend-shaped API (transactional SMS, push
notifications, file delivery).
narrative.technicalApproach:
Next.js 15.1.2 App Router with React 19 and TypeScript 5.7,
deployed on **Cloudflare Workers via
`@opennextjs/cloudflare`** (migrated from Vercel on
2026-02-04, commit `e9ace8a`, with a follow-up GitHub
Actions auto-deploy workflow in commit `a970c1a`). Four
Resend-wrapping API routes form the entire backend surface:
**`POST /api/send`** takes `{apiKey, from, to, subject,
html}` and forwards to the Resend SDK, returning
`{success, data: {id}, error?}`; **`POST /api/preview`**
takes `{blocks, theme, variables}` and runs
`@react-email/render` to produce email-client-safe HTML
with merge fields substituted; **`GET/POST/DELETE
/api/contacts`** wraps the Resend Audiences contact CRUD;
**`GET /api/resend-templates`** plus its `[id]/duplicate`
and `[id]/publish` sub-routes wrap Resend's cloud
templates. **State management** uses Zustand 5.0 with a
50-state undo/redo history stack — every block move, edit,
delete, or theme change is reversible. **Templates and
contacts persist to browser localStorage** under six
namespaced keys (`email-platform-templates`,
`email-platform-contacts`, `email-platform-settings`,
`email-platform-onboarding`, `email-platform-emails-sent`,
`email-platform-sidebar-collapsed`) — there is no
server-side database, no auth layer, and no telemetry. The
editor itself uses **`@dnd-kit/core` + `@dnd-kit/sortable`**
for accessible drag-and-drop (commit `1dadb76`, *"implement
drag-and-drop block reordering"*). The visual language is a
custom **Neobrutalism design system** — 4px solid black
borders, 8px hard-offset shadows (no blur), three brand
colours (`#DC2626` red, `#16A34A` green, `#F59E0B` gold),
custom Tailwind `shadow-neo-*` utilities — that signals
"approachable / playful" while still feeling polished.
Performance: 95+ Lighthouse, <1s TTFB, <100ms API. **GEO
optimisation** (commit `04fc131`, *"add Generative Engine
Optimization for AI discoverability"*) ships
machine-readable metadata for LLM-driven discovery, same
pattern as Chan's other public projects.
narrative.outcomes:
Live and functional at
[sendjoy.chanmeng-dev.workers.dev](https://sendjoy.chanmeng-dev.workers.dev/)
since end of December 2025; 43 solo commits across roughly
6 weeks of active development with subsequent maintenance
through April 2026. The defining engineering moment is the
**4-day arc from CLI to visual platform**: Dec 23 ships
commit `16c7026` *"initial release of Christmas greeting
email sender"* (a CLI script using `tsx` and `dotenv` to
send one templated greeting via Resend), Dec 27 ships
commit `fe16fdd` *"transform CLI tool into visual web
application platform"*, and Dec 27–28 layers the visual
editor, the 5-step wizard, the Resend cloud-templates
integration, the Resend Audiences sync, and the onboarding
flow for non-technical users on top — all before the
product gets its final name `send-joy` (commit `dba12bc`,
Dec 29) and brand identity (`0798540`). The Feb 2026
Cloudflare Workers migration cuts hosting cost to zero and
adds GitHub Actions auto-deploy. The codebase serves as
Chan's worked example of the "wrap a developer API into a
no-code product for non-technical users" pattern — directly
cited in conversations with founders and recruiters as a
demonstration of full-stack product judgement (not just
engineering): identifying the non-technical-user gap in a
developer-first API, then closing it in a week.
narrative.myRole:
Sole product designer, sole developer, sole maintainer. Made
the call to pivot from a one-off Christmas CLI into a
general-purpose visual platform after the CLI's 6th commit;
designed the Neobrutalism visual language; designed the
7-block schema; designed the 5-step wizard; designed the
onboarding flow specifically for non-technical users (the
explicit framing in commit `782731c`); implemented every
Resend API wrapper route; engineered the localStorage
persistence model with zero server-side state; executed the
Vercel -> Cloudflare Workers migration with GitHub Actions
auto-deploy; authored the README, TUTORIAL.md, and GEO
metadata. Maintains the live deployment today.

projects.publicSummary: Seismophone (codebase: sunostats) is an independent, third-party observatory for AI-generated music, instrumented deepest on Suno. It captures Suno's public data daily and publishes free readings the platform itself hides — forensic proof that Suno's "trending" chart froze in 2024, a daily pulse on Suno's live curated Explore shelves, and a rare map of who remixes whom, recovered from a since-closed gap in Suno's own data. Point the same instruments at your own catalog and they produce a free public portfolio at /@handle plus seven single-purchase insight reports; solo-built on Next.js 16 + Neon Postgres, trilingual (EN / 简中 / 日本語), self-hosted on a Docker + Traefik server with a read-only MCP server for AI agents.

narrative.impactHeadline: Seismophone (repo: sunostats) is an independent observatory for AI-generated music. It measures Suno from the outside and publishes free readings the platform hides: proof its "trending" chart froze in 2024, and a rare map of who remixes whom from a since-closed data gap.
narrative.problem: AI-music platforms like Suno expose almost nothing about their own ecosystems. A creator gets a follower count and a play count; the structural data that would let them understand their catalog, trace a song's lineage, or read the platform's editorial behaviour is simply not published. Nobody outside Suno can answer basic ecosystem questions: is the "trending" feed actually live, where did real discovery move, which model eras dominate, who remixes whom? Seismophone exists to measure that ecosystem from the outside and publish the readings openly — its stated stance is "we publish what the platforms won't."
narrative.audience: Two audiences on one set of instruments. First, anyone — no account — can read the free observatory: the fossil-watch "pulse" of Suno's dead trending feed, a daily pulse on Suno's live curated Explore shelves (Staff Picks, Best of v5.5), the model-era timeline, and the cross-author "who remixes whom" lineage graph. Second, signed-in Suno creators point the same instruments at their own catalog: they claim a handle, get a free public portfolio at /@handle, watch up to ten playlists with daily pulse diffs, and buy any of seven deep-dive insight reports on their own music. A third, machine audience is served deliberately: a public read-only MCP server lets external AI agents query the observatory without Seismophone bearing their inference cost.
narrative.productLogic:
The product is one measurement apparatus pointed in two directions.

**Outward (the observatory, free).** A daily pipeline captures Suno's public surfaces and turns them into published readings. `/pulse` has two halves. The top is a "fossil-watch": it treats Suno's `/api/trending` feed forensically, having proven it is a frozen September-2024 playlist object served fresh but never updated, and renders a cadence "seismograph," a model-version timeline, the longest-reigning #1, and tag-trend curves. Below it is a live block that diffs Suno's real curated Explore shelves (Staff Picks, Best of v5.5) day to day — new entries, risers, fallers, drop-outs — the forward-looking instrument that replaced the dead feed. `/trending` preserves the 2024 feed as a historical exhibit. `/observatory/who-remixes-whom` and the per-song `/lineage/[clipId]` explorer render Suno's real remix/derivation graph as a clickable SVG, walked server-side with recursive Postgres CTEs (bounded to a few hops each way); private clips appear only as anonymized nodes.

**Inward (creator lenses).** A creator signs in with Google, claims their Suno handle, and instantly gets a free public portfolio at `/@handle` (themable, with configurable stats and tag filters). They then spend credits on seven single-purchase reports — Tag Optimization, Performance Snapshot, Method Mix, Song DNA, Persona Mix, Remix Readiness, and Model Era — each a deterministic analytics engine over their catalog, optionally topped with a short LLM "analyst's note." Each report is a discrete, single-purchase artifact — a creator asks one specific question of their own catalog and gets one answer back, rather than committing to an ongoing relationship with the tool.
narrative.businessLogic: The shape is open-observatory-plus-discrete-reports. Everything ecosystem-level and every public creator portfolio is open to all; the only gated surface is the seven per-catalog insight reports, each a self-contained analysis of a creator's own catalog. The defensible asset is the lineage archive: because it was recovered from a redaction gap Suno has since closed, it is a sealed, un-reproducible dataset — a genuine data moat rather than a feature a competitor can re-derive. Distribution is deliberately agent-native: the read-only MCP server turns the observatory into a tool other people's AI agents can call, extending reach without ad spend, while the trilingual surface (EN / 简中 / 日本語) reaches Suno's large East-Asian creator base.
narrative.technicalApproach:
A Next.js 16 (App Router, pinned to Webpack + `output: standalone`) app in React 19 and TypeScript 5, over a single 17-table Drizzle schema on Neon serverless Postgres. Auth is NextAuth v5 + Neon Auth (Google OAuth only); payments run through Stripe with a webhook-verified credit ledger; Resend delivers report emails; an optional OpenAI `gpt-5.4-nano` pass narrates otherwise numbers-only reports.

**The lineage harvest is the crux.** Suno's serializer redacts clip parent-UUIDs to the all-zeros UUID on its main endpoints, but missed that pass on exactly two response shapes — the slim clips of `/api/trending` and the `personas[].clip` sub-trees of a public profile. A dedicated parser path (the *only* sanctioned one) extracts the real parent-pointer types from those shapes, filters out zero-UUIDs, and deduplicates them into a directed-edge table with an idempotent unique constraint; the public graph is then walked with `WITH RECURSIVE` Postgres CTEs. Ingestion is strictly compliant — only W3C oEmbed-discovered public endpoints, anonymous and CORS-enabled, at a once-daily cadence, with no scraping, credentials, or headless browser, and listing-level privacy always respected.

**Money-path and reverse-engineered-API correctness are hardened deliberately.** Concurrent double-charges are blocked by a partial unique index over in-flight report claims; balances debit via optimistic `UPDATE … RETURNING` with a sub-zero rollback; Stripe idempotency is enforced two ways (a unique ledger index plus a processed-events dedupe log); generator failures auto-refund. Because the whole product depends on Suno's unofficial response shapes, a weekly schema-drift canary alerts when they change, and a daily editorial-shelf staleness sentinel flags when a tracked shelf freezes (its UUID rotated away); a durable cron-runs table provides leasing and observability that Neon's HTTP driver can't. The app self-hosts as a multi-stage Docker image (Node 20 slim, non-root) behind Traefik + Let's Encrypt, shipped via GHCR and GitHub Actions, with five daily/weekly cron pipelines; the trilingual dictionaries are TypeScript-checked so a missing translation key fails the build.
narrative.outcomes: Live at seismophone.chanmeng.org, solo-built across 99 commits over roughly three months (2026-04-12 → 2026-07-10). The apparatus runs 19 pages, 28 API route handlers, 57 components, and five cron pipelines over a 17-table schema, plus a 7-tool read-only MCP server. Its signature result is the sealed remix-lineage archive — ~102 real parent→child edges recovered in a single 2026-05-09 backfill before Suno closed the redaction gap — which makes the public lineage explorer a dataset that, by construction, cannot be collected again. The forensic teardown of Suno's frozen `/api/trending` fossil is itself a published finding; when that feed proved dead, the observatory added a live pulse on Suno's real curated Explore shelves as the forward-looking replacement. The build also went through structured hardening waves (Codex-reviewed) covering credits, privacy, query performance, observability, and the schema-drift and editorial-shelf sentinels.
narrative.myRole: I built the entire system solo — product concept and "observatory" positioning, the lineage-harvest discovery and parser, the 17-table schema and daily ingestion pipeline, the fossil-watch teardown and the live editorial-shelf pulse, all seven insight engines, the Stripe credit ledger and its concurrency hardening, the recursive-CTE lineage graph and SVG explorer, the read-only MCP server, the trilingual i18n system, and the self-hosted Docker + Traefik deployment with its cron and observability tooling. 99 of 99 commits are mine.

projects.publicSummary: AI career agent live at **vitex.org.nz**. Paste a job ad and describe your background in plain text, and an 8-step AI pipeline writes a resume and cover letter tailored to that specific job, then compiles them into a polished, ATS-ready PDF in about 30 seconds across 7 auto-selected templates — no document is rendered by an outside service. Vitex is **agent-ready**: everything the website does is also reachable over a public API, a command-line tool, and an MCP server, so a terminal or an AI assistant (Claude, Cursor, ChatGPT) can drive the whole product without a browser.

narrative.impactHeadline: Paste a job ad and describe your background — it writes a resume and cover letter tailored to that job and builds them into a polished PDF in about 30 seconds. Works from a browser, the command line, or inside an AI assistant.
narrative.problem: Most AI resume tools fail in one of two ways: (a) a thin chatbot wrapper that hands you a paragraph and calls it a resume — no PDF, no ATS-aware structure, no template intelligence; or (b) a form-filling site that spends a minute-plus rendering the same generic template every other user gets. Both miss the real job: the resume has to be tailored to the *specific* job ad, the document has to survive automated applicant-tracking (ATS) parsing, the visual template has to fit the target industry, and the whole round-trip has to feel instant. None of that is true of "paste your CV into ChatGPT."
narrative.audience: Three groups. **Job seekers in live applications** — especially in the NZ / Australia / Asia-Pacific markets where Chan personally needed the tool and where vitex.org.nz deliberately lives. **Career-changers** who have to re-tailor every application — the people for whom rewriting a resume per job ad is the actual bottleneck. And **AI-engineering reviewers** assessing Chan's ability to ship a production multi-step AI agent with real users, real billing, and real operations — not a demo.
narrative.productLogic: The flow is deliberately short. (1) **Homepage** — paste a job ad and describe your background in free text; no CV upload, no field-by-field form. (2) **Generate** — click run and the 8-step pipeline streams its progress live (reading the job ad → reading your background → matching → tailoring → ATS scoring → cover letter → building the PDF), so you watch each stage finish instead of staring at a spinner, then land in the **Editor** with a PDF preview, an ATS score with concrete feedback, and the matching cover letter. (3) **Refine & export** — a plain-English box ("make the leadership section stronger", "shorten the intro") re-does just that slice rather than regenerating everything; then download the PDF or publish a share link. A dashboard keeps a searchable history for signed-in users.
narrative.businessLogic: Each run is a multi-call AI pipeline plus a document compile, so capacity is metered per successful result rather than left open-ended: the ledger records a run **exactly once, and only after a PDF actually compiles**, which makes a failed or retried attempt a non-event. Public **share links** give the product organic reach — a candidate who lands a job can credit Vitex, and the shared resume is itself a live demo of what the product produces. The full stack (auth, database, cache, payments, AI, PDF rendering) is shaped for a real user base, not a showcase.
narrative.technicalApproach:
**AI agent layer.** The pipeline runs on the **Vercel AI SDK** with the OpenAI provider across **eight sequential stages**, on two-tier model routing — a cheaper **extract** model (default `gpt-5.4-mini`) for parsing and a stronger **reasoning** model (default `gpt-5.5`) for tailoring, both overridable per deployment, with per-tier reasoning-effort control. Each stage emits a **Zod-validated** structured output:
1. **JD parsing** — role, seniority, must-have and nice-to-have skills, company signals
2. **Background parsing** — free-text description → structured skills/experience
3. **Match analysis** — candidate↔job fit profile
4. **Tailoring** — reorder, rewrite, and emphasise history toward the parsed job ad
5. **ATS scoring** — a **deterministic, no-LLM** check of parse-ability, with actionable feedback
6. **Cover letter** — generated from the same parsed pair
7. **Render** — auto-select a template + seeded design tokens, emit Typst source
8. **Compile** — Typst → PDF server-side; the PDF is the deliverable, so the run is committed only here

Right after tailoring, a deterministic **faithfulness gate** re-checks for fabricated skills or employers and triggers one bounded corrective re-tailor — an anti-hallucination guard with no model in the check itself. Every stage streams progress over **Server-Sent Events**. Inputs are read from `sessionStorage` rather than URL params so deep links can't leak a user's data.

**Document engine — Typst, not LaTeX.** PDFs are compiled by a **local Typst binary** inside the Docker image, with no call to any outside service; the project migrated from LaTeX to Typst on 2026-04-06 (same day as the Cloudflare Workers → DigitalOcean VPS move) because Typst compiles in milliseconds where LaTeX needs seconds and Workers couldn't run the binary at all. The image pre-warms the Typst package cache (FontAwesome) so cold starts don't pay a fetch cost. **7 templates** (academic, banking, compact, creative, executive, modern-cv, two-column) are auto-selected from parsed industry/role signals.

**Persistence + product stack.** Neon serverless Postgres + Drizzle ORM (resumes, history, credit ledger); Upstash Redis for short-lived cache; Stack Auth for sign-in and agent API keys; Stripe for the credit ledger; Cloudinary for uploads and rendered PDFs; nanoid for share tokens.

**UI — Phantom.** Next.js 15 App Router + React 19 + TypeScript. The **Phantom** design system — a soft, flat, single-hue aubergine-and-lavender look with light-weight type and generous whitespace — is the deliberate visual identity, and replaced an earlier Neobrutalism (thick borders, hard shadows) treatment in a 2026 UI pass. shadcn/ui + Tailwind for primitives, Framer Motion for transitions, react-hook-form + Zod for validation.

**Deployment + hardening.** Production runs as a single **Docker container** on a **DigitalOcean VPS** behind **Traefik** (reverse proxy + automatic TLS), shipped by **GitHub Actions** with **health-gated rolling deploys** and automatic database migrations. Recent hardening added site-wide security headers (Content-Security-Policy + HSTS in production), request rate-limiting on the API-key/credit/billing routes, and auth + rate-limiting + sandboxing on the PDF-compile endpoint. Two earlier platform migrations (Railway → CF Workers → VPS) and the LaTeX → Typst swap all shipped without an outage.
narrative.outcomes: Live and operational at **vitex.org.nz**, serving real users end-to-end from a pasted job ad to a compiled PDF. **379 commits over ~18 months** (2025-01-11 → 2026-07-14), essentially sole-authored. Two platform migrations and a LaTeX → Typst engine swap shipped without a service outage, each step captured in the commit log (the `xz-utils`/`curl` Dockerfile fixes, FontAwesome package pre-cache, the OpenAI strict-JSON-schema regression, the `sessionStorage` move for sensitive input). A 2026 UI pass replaced Neobrutalism with the Phantom design system; a subsequent security/ops wave added production CSP + HSTS, rate limits on the billing and API-key routes, a sandboxed compile endpoint, health-gated rolling deploys, and a production runbook. Backed by **49 test files / 437 tests**.
narrative.myRole: Sole engineer, sole designer, sole product owner. Designed the 8-step agent pipeline with Zod-validated stages (migrating the editor from CopilotKit to the Vercel AI SDK agent chat along the way), wrote every API route, built the Stripe credit system, set up Stack Auth, designed the Drizzle schema for resumes / credits / share tokens, ran both platform migrations (Railway → CF Workers → DigitalOcean VPS), executed the LaTeX → Typst engine swap (including the Dockerfile fixes and the FontAwesome package pre-cache), designed the Phantom UI that replaced the earlier Neobrutalism treatment, built the plain-English refinement loop in the Editor, upgraded the model routing to gpt-5.5 / gpt-5.4-mini with per-tier reasoning-effort control, and hardened production (CSP + HSTS, rate limits, sandboxed compile, health-gated rolling deploys). I continue to operate the live service.

projects.name: FanFic Lab — HSR DreamWriter, an Adaptive LangGraph Agent Powering a Gaming Fan-Creation Community
projects.tagline: A full-stack AI co-creation platform for the *Honkai: Star Rail* (崩坏：星穹铁道) fandom — a 7-node LangGraph adaptive agent (intent parser → architect → writer → quality guard → revision loop → summarizer → delivery) with a self-revising quality loop, hand-curated 5-character HSR canon knowledge pack + RAG over a `KnowledgeChunk` pgvector index, and a UGC community layer with follows / nested-reply comments / notifications / pgvector related-story recommendations — split into two Docker services (`web` + `agent`) self-hosted on a DigitalOcean VPS via Coolify behind Cloudflare.

Motion (formerly Framer Motion) 12 — parallax hero, transitions

pg_trgm GIN indexes (`Story_title_trgm_idx`, `Story_summary_trgm_idx`) — fast ILIKE substring search

Cloudflare proxy mode — SSL termination + CDN + DDoS protection

projects.publicSummary: FanFic Lab (live at fanfic-lab.tech) is a full-stack AI co-creation community built specifically for the *Honkai: Star Rail* (崩坏：星穹铁道) fandom — the most popular HoYoverse game in China during the project window. The 182-commit codebase compresses three usually-separate things into one product: (1) **an adaptive LangGraph.js agent named "DreamWriter"** that takes a Chinese natural-language prompt like "想看砂金和藿藿的现代AU虐转甜" and runs it through a 7-node graph (intent parser → story architect → writer → quality guard → conditional revision loop → summarizer → delivery), where the quality-guard node uses GPT-4o-mini to score the draft 1-10 against an HSR canon knowledge prompt and either passes the story through or sends it back to the writer for up to MAX_REVISIONS=2 self-corrections; (2) **a hand-curated fandom knowledge pack** at `src/knowledge/hsr/` containing 5 deeply-modeled HSR characters (砂金 / 藿藿 / 丹恒 / 景元 / 花火) with personality / speech-pattern / emotional-trigger / relationship maps + 4 relationship dynamics + 5 world-rule categories (命途体系, etc.) + 5 trope templates (现代AU, ABO, Hurt/Comfort, 豆花/甜饼, 前世今生/命运轮回), composed into a system prompt by a `toSystemPrompt()` method and combined at runtime with **RAG over a `KnowledgeChunk` pgvector table** (1536-dim `text-embedding-3-small` vectors, retrieved by the writer node before drafting); and (3) **a UGC community layer** with Stack-Auth identities, public follows, public author pages, nested-reply comments + comment likes + spam protection, in-app notifications with a bell + page, per-reader font/line-height/scroll-position preferences, dark mode, per-story view tracking, pgvector-cosine related-story recommendations, pg_trgm GIN-indexed search, and SEO/OG tags + sitemap. The whole thing is split into **two Docker services** — `web` (Next.js 16 frontend + API routes) and `agent` (LangGraph CLI runtime on port 8123) — wired together via Docker Compose internal networking, self-hosted on a **DigitalOcean VPS via Coolify** behind Cloudflare proxy-mode SSL, with GitHub Actions building both images to GHCR and SSH-deploying on every `git push master`. The migration from Railway to DigitalOcean+Coolify (commit `734e69c`, 2026-04-03) is documented in a 600+-line `docs/MIGRATION_RAILWAY_TO_DIGITALOCEAN.md` playbook that doubles as a reusable Claude-Code automation reference. Before the HSR DreamWriter pivot in commit `a288e25` (2026-03-23), the project shipped as a more generic CopilotKit + LangGraph fandom-agnostic editor with HITL approval cards, Tavily fandom research, and a creative wizard — that earlier work (since removed from the codebase in the pivot) informed the current agent design. The pivot commit `b5baf34` the same day deliberately removed the old editor/wizard/generator code, demonstrating willingness to delete working code when a sharper product direction emerges.

narrative.impactHeadline: Adaptive LangGraph agent for Chinese fanfiction creators — 7-node quality-loop graph over a hand-curated Honkai: Star Rail canon + pgvector RAG, plus a follower/comment/recommendation community shipped as a lean two-service Docker stack self-hosted via DigitalOcean + Coolify.
narrative.problem: The Chinese gaming fanfiction (同人文) scene around HoYoverse titles — and *Honkai: Star Rail* / 崩坏：星穹铁道 in particular — has two structural problems that generic AI writing tools don't solve. **First**, generic LLM creative writing assistants (CopilotKit-style autosuggest, ChatGPT, Claude) have no deep grounding in HSR canon: they default to surface-level character clichés ("cold prince" / "shy boy"), get the speech patterns and emotional triggers wrong (e.g., they don't know that 砂金 stops speaking softly when 黑天鹅 is threatened, or that 藿藿's stutter is canon-specific), and routinely produce OOC (out-of-character) drafts that any serious fanwriter rejects. **Second**, existing fanfiction platforms (LOFTER / AO3 / 长佩) are *reading* communities, not *creation* communities — they host finished work, but they don't lower the activation energy for the much larger group of fans who want to consume CP (couple/ship) content but find a blank page intimidating. The opportunity: build a platform where any fan can type "I want to read 砂金 × 藿藿 现代AU 虐转甜 HE" in Chinese, get back a canon-respecting 3000-word draft in 60 seconds, edit it, publish it, and have other fans like/comment/follow them. That requires solving the OOC problem at the agent level (canon knowledge baked in, plus a self-revising quality loop) and the activation-energy problem at the product level (one-shot prompt → finished story → save → publish). FanFic Lab is the answer.
narrative.audience: Three concentric circles. **Innermost**: the 同人圈 (fandom writers' circle) for *Honkai: Star Rail* — typically Chinese-speaking female fans of HoYoverse games who lurk on LOFTER / Weibo / Bilibili, who already know who 砂金 / 藿藿 / 丹恒 are, who have CP preferences and trope preferences (现代AU / ABO / Hurt-Comfort), and who want a co-writer that won't break their characters. **Middle ring**: consumers who like reading HSR fic but never wrote any — they get the one-prompt-to-finished-story path and become first-time creators, then liking/commenting on others' work pulls them into the community. **Outer ring**: the broader gaming-fanfic and CN-creative-writing community who can adopt the same architecture for other fandoms (the `src/knowledge/` directory is deliberately structured as `base/types.ts` + per-fandom packs, with HSR as the only currently-shipped pack but mentor / 原神 / 鸣潮 packs as natural future expansions — `FandomKnowledge` is a typed interface, not an HSR-hardcode). The Chinese-first product surface is a deliberate choice: prompts, system messages, character data, world rules, even the agent's internal log messages ("正在根据反馈修改第 X 版...") are all in Chinese, because the audience is.
narrative.productLogic: The user flow collapses what would normally be 4-5 separate apps (writing assistant + character database + fanfic editor + social platform + recommendation engine) into one screen. (1) **Land on `fanfic-lab.tech`** — cinematic full-bleed hero with Honkai: Star Rail splash arts and KV (key-visual) imagery, plus a Honkai: Star Rail fan content attribution credit (the project takes the IP-attribution discipline seriously even though it's a non-commercial fan tool). (2) **Sign in via Stack Auth** — branded sign-in/up shell with explicit error classification, mobile + perf polish. (3) **Hit the "create" page** — type a Chinese prompt describing the CP you want to read about ("想看砂金和藿藿的现代AU虐转甜"). (4) **Backend kicks off a DreamWriter agent run**: the Next.js API route POSTs to `LANGGRAPH_URL=http://agent:8123` (Docker Compose internal hostname) which runs the 7-node graph; the frontend polls for `logs` updates and shows them in Chinese ("正在解析您的需求..." → "正在策划故事..." → "正在创作..." → "正在质量检查 (8/10)..." → "正在根据反馈修改第 1 版..." → "故事完成"). (5) **Story auto-publishes to the Fandom Feed** (a deliberate product decision in commit `b090c04` "auto-publish stories, remove shelf page and drafts tab" — friction kills first-time creators). (6) **Other users discover it via**: the feed (filterable by fandom / ships / tags / rating / status, sortable by recent / popular / comments / word count, with infinite scroll); SEO/OG tags + sitemap for organic search; **pgvector related-story recommendations** on each story page (commit `48428e6` — cosine similarity over title+summary+ships+tags embeddings, deliberately *not* over chapter content because we want thematic matching, not prose-style matching); **per-author public pages** at `/users/[username]` with their stories + followers + following. (7) **Engagement primitives**: like a story, follow an author, leave a nested-reply comment, like comments, get notified in the bell + notifications page when someone interacts with your work. (8) **Story reader page**: per-reader font / line-height preferences (commit `6551966`) + scroll position memory so the reader can pause + resume, plus dark mode toggle in the header (commit `b922cf8`). (9) **Author tools**: edit published stories, continue any story with a new chapter (with auto-generated chapter titles, commit `30df8fe`), avatar crop preview via react-easy-crop. The cumulative effect: **a fan-creation community where the friction from "I want to read this" to "this story exists and other fans are commenting on it" is one Chinese sentence**.
narrative.businessLogic: This is an MIT-licensed open-source fan-creation tool with a non-commercial usage stance toward the HSR IP — it ships an explicit Honkai: Star Rail fan content attribution on the homepage and treats the canon knowledge pack as a *fan-curated* reference, not commercial use of HoYoverse assets. The infrastructure choices are however explicit and deliberate. **Deployment model**: the production stack runs on a self-hosted DigitalOcean droplet behind Coolify (the migration playbook records the rationale — moving off Railway gave full control over runtime + the ability to host more services on the same box). **Model tiering**: GPT-4o-mini handles the intent parser + quality guard nodes; GPT-4o handles the writer node where prose quality matters; the quality-guard revision budget is capped at MAX_REVISIONS=2 so the agent loop is bounded; embeddings use `text-embedding-3-small`; the `SourceResearchCache` Prisma model + Redis tier persist Tavily results for 30 days to avoid duplicate fandom research. **Credits scaffolding**: the Prisma schema includes `UserCredits` and `Generation` models with `creditsCharged: Int` — a June 2026 Claude×Codex adversarial-review pass (PRs #2-#3) moved credits from data-layer scaffolding to enforced billing by making story generation server-authoritative and fail-closed on QA, and calibrating story length to the paid tier — so generation cost and capacity are now controlled server-side rather than trusted from the client. **Build-time + runtime layout**: two services (web + agent) in one Docker Compose file means health checks, internal networking, restart policies, and rolling deploys are all handled by one Coolify project rather than fragmented across providers. **Discovery loop**: per-story SEO/OG tags + sitemap (commit `897a43d`) + the related-stories pgvector recommender compound organic discoverability over time.
narrative.technicalApproach:
The system is organised around four orthogonal layers that together produce the user-visible behaviour.

**Layer 1 — Adaptive LangGraph agent with self-revising quality loop.** `src/agent/dreamwriter/graph.ts` defines a `StateGraph<DreamWriterStateAnnotation>` with 7 nodes wired into a deliberate topology: **intent_parser** (GPT-4o-mini, temp 0.3, extracts `{cp, setting, tone, constraints, language}` from Chinese natural-language input) → **story_architect** (GPT-4o, plans `{title, cp, setting, tone, wordTarget, emotionalArc, scenes[]}`) → **writer** (GPT-4o, temp 0.9, generates prose using the HSR knowledge prompt + RAG context) → **quality_guard** (GPT-4o-mini, temp 0.3, scores the draft against the same knowledge prompt and returns `{overallScore, oocIssues[], consistencyIssues[], proseNotes[], passesThreshold}`) → **conditional edge** `routeAfterQualityCheck`: if `!passesThreshold && revisionCount < MAX_REVISIONS(=2)`, route back through **revision_counter** → **writer** with the quality feedback appended to the prompt; otherwise → **summarize** (writes the public-facing one-paragraph blurb) → **delivery** (END). The state annotation includes a `logs[]` reducer so the frontend can show Chinese progress text in near-real-time. `MemorySaver` checkpointing is used so runs survive process restarts during long agent calls.

**Layer 2 — Hand-curated fandom knowledge pack + pgvector RAG.** Two complementary mechanisms feed the writer node: **(a) the deterministic knowledge prompt** in `src/knowledge/hsr/` — `characters.ts` defines 5 HSR characters (砂金 / 藿藿 / 丹恒 / 景元 / 花火) as `CharacterProfile` objects with `name`, `aliases[]`, `personality[]`, `speechPatterns`, `emotionalTriggers[]`, `relationships: Record<string, string>`, and `timelineStates`; `relationships.ts` has 4 `RelationshipDynamic` objects with `keyMoments[]`; `world.ts` has 5 `WorldRule` categories (命途体系 etc.); `tropes.ts` has 5 `TropeTemplate` objects (现代AU / ABO / Hurt-Comfort / 豆花/甜饼 / 前世今生) with per-character `characterAdaptations`; `index.ts` composes everything via a `toSystemPrompt()` method that produces a structured Chinese prompt with `▎character` headers + `性格特征` / `说话方式` / `情感触发点` / `人际关系` / `时间线状态` fields. **(b) The probabilistic RAG layer** in `src/knowledge/base/rag.ts` — `retrieveRelevantChunks(query, fandom, limit)` embeds the query via `text-embedding-3-small`, runs a raw SQL `embedding <=> vector` cosine-distance query against the `KnowledgeChunk` table (with a pgvector ivfflat index), returns the top-k chunks. The writer node calls both, concatenating the deterministic knowledge prompt with the RAG context before invoking GPT-4o. This split lets the *fixed* canon (character personalities) be edited as code while the *long-tail* canon (specific scenes, story snippets) lives as embeddings and grows over time.

**Layer 3 — Two-Docker-service production deployment via DigitalOcean + Coolify.** The codebase ships **two Dockerfiles** (`Dockerfile.web` for Next.js 16 + Prisma 7, `Dockerfile.agent` for LangGraph CLI) and one `docker-compose.coolify.yml` that wires them into a single Coolify project. Internal networking uses Docker Compose service names (`web` calls `http://agent:8123` via the Compose-managed bridge network — no Railway-internal-DNS, no nginx, no manual sidecar config). **Health checks** are explicit: `web` polls `/api/health` (which itself checks Redis + Postgres latency), `agent` polls LangGraph's `/info` endpoint, both with `start_period: 30-40s` to tolerate cold starts. **External proxy chain**: Cloudflare (proxy mode, SSL termination, CDN) → DigitalOcean droplet 159.223.173.17 → Traefik (built into Coolify, handles `:80`/`:443` and routes by host) → Docker container. **CI/CD**: `.github/workflows/deploy.yml` on every `git push master` runs a 2-image parallel Docker Buildx build, pushes both to GHCR (`ghcr.io/chanmeng666/fanfic-lab/{web,agent}` tagged per-SHA + `latest`, with separate `cache-from` scopes for each image), then SSH-deploys to the VPS with a 20-minute timeout. The 600+ line `docs/MIGRATION_RAILWAY_TO_DIGITALOCEAN.md` playbook documents the entire transition including DNS cutover, Cloudflare config, Coolify project setup, lessons learned (Docker registry case-sensitivity bug fixed in commit `839e9c1`), and a "Claude Code Automation Playbook" section — turning the migration into a reusable artifact.

**Layer 4 — Full UGC community substrate with pgvector recommendations + trigram search.** The Prisma schema models 21 tables centred on `User` ↔ `Story` ↔ `Chapter` with full social graph (`Follow`), engagement primitives (`Like`, `Comment` with `parentId` self-reference for nested replies, `CommentLike`), discovery primitives (`Notification` with polymorphic `payload: Json`), preferences (`UserPreferences` includes `preferredCPs[]`, `preferredTropes[]`, `avoidTropes[]`, `tonePreference`, `lengthPreference` — feeding into the personalised feed *and* into future agent-prompt personalisation), and the AI-generation audit trail (`Generation` model with `request: Json`, `plan: Json`, `deliverable: Json`, `tokensUsed`, `creditsCharged`, `durationMs`). **Two specialised PostgreSQL extensions** are deliberately exploited: **(i) pg_trgm** with GIN indexes on `Story.title` and `Story.summary` (migration `20260421091500_enable_pgtrgm_search`) — turns Prisma's `contains:` queries from sequential scans into trigram-index lookups, scaling search past a few hundred stories without code change; **(ii) pgvector** with an ivfflat cosine index on `Story.embedding` (migration `20260421093000_add_story_embedding`, `lists=100`) — every story gets a 1536-dim embedding generated from `title + fandom + ships + tags + summary` (deliberately *not* chapter content, because we want thematic match, not prose-style match), and the related-stories endpoint runs cosine-distance queries to surface similar work. `setStoryEmbedding()` in `lib/story-embedding.ts` uses raw SQL because Prisma 7 doesn't yet support direct writes to the `vector` type. A `scripts/backfill-embeddings.ts` script handles legacy stories created before the embedding column existed.

The four layers are loosely coupled: the agent can be re-shaped without touching the DB schema, the knowledge pack can grow without touching the agent topology, the deployment substrate can move providers (the Railway → DigitalOcean migration is the proof) without touching the application, and the community layer's pgvector + pg_trgm indexes are content-agnostic.
narrative.outcomes:
- **182 commits over ~16 weeks** (2026-01-01 → 2026-04-21), spanning a complete product redesign mid-project: the first iteration was a generic CopilotKit + LangGraph fanfic editor with HITL approval cards; the HSR DreamWriter pivot in commit `a288e25` ("HSR DreamWriter - complete product redesign", 2026-03-23) plus commit `b5baf34` ("remove old pipeline, editor, wizard, and generator code", same day) deliberately deleted the old codebase to focus on a single fandom with deep canon support.
- **Adaptive quality-loop agent shipped** in commit `a06d96f` "wire up adaptive DreamWriter agent graph with quality loop" — the conditional revision edge with MAX_REVISIONS=2 turns a one-shot generator into a self-correcting agent, dramatically reducing OOC errors per Chan's internal observations.
- **Hand-curated HSR canon knowledge pack** built across commits `e5e82ac` (initial pack) → `1a0fa53` (HSR_TROPES extraction) → `611d0e0` (KnowledgeChunk model + DreamWriter preference fields in `UserPreferences`) → `f9b1fcf` (RAG utilities). 5 deeply-modeled characters + 4 relationships + 5 world rules + 5 trope templates.
- **Railway → DigitalOcean + Coolify migration** completed 2026-04-03 (commit `734e69c`), documented in a 600+-line reusable playbook at `docs/MIGRATION_RAILWAY_TO_DIGITALOCEAN.md` that includes lessons-learned (Docker registry case-sensitivity, SSH timeout tuning) and a "Claude Code Automation Playbook" section.
- **GitHub Actions zero-downtime CI/CD** added in commit `985e0b4` "ci: add GitHub Actions CI/CD for zero-downtime deployment" — parallel image builds with per-image cache scopes, GHCR registry, SSH-deployment-by-push.
- **Full social graph + discovery substrate** built in the last 4 days of active development (2026-04-21): nested-reply comments + likes + spam protection (`d234471`), in-app notifications (`f171ce3`), public followers/following pages (`d96fc68`), per-author public pages (`e465240`), pgvector related-stories (`48428e6`), pg_trgm search indexes (`9bd86a9`), per-story view counts (`f598394`), per-story SEO/OG + sitemap (`897a43d`), dark mode (`b922cf8`), reader font/line-height + scroll memory (`6551966`), avatar crop preview (`9554785`), branded sign-in/up shell (`a8fccd3`).
- **Production stack survives a multi-service migration**: the same codebase ran on Vercel + Railway, then Vercel + Railway-LangGraph-only, then DigitalOcean + Coolify (current), without breaking the agent contract.
- **Operational discipline**: 2 services run on a self-hosted DO droplet via Coolify; deliberate model tiering (mini for parsing/checking, full for prose) + cache-first Tavily research + capped revision budget keep the agent loop predictable.
- **Production URL live**: fanfic-lab.tech/ (with www → root redirect), Cloudflare-proxied, MIT-licensed.
- **June 2026 billing-integrity + reliability hardening** (Claude×Codex adversarial review, PRs #2-#3, 2026-06-28→29): server-authoritative generation with fail-closed QA, per-scene progress + degraded-quality surfacing + cost disclosure, Zod route validation + a Vitest suite + a CI gate, extra DB indexes, series-resume, and story-length calibration to the paid tier — turning the free MVP into a billing-safe, tested product.
narrative.myRole: Sole author, designer, and operator. Every one of the 182 commits is mine. I wrote both the initial CopilotKit + LangGraph fanfic editor (commits `0a3d1f5` → `36c5469` Teal+Amber "Literary Atelier" design system → `cdc3586` Railway deployment), made the call to scrap that direction and pivot to HSR-specific DreamWriter (commits `cbd31cc` "pivot from MDZS to Honkai: Star Rail based on 2025-2026 fandom trends" → `4790851` MDZS implementation plan as comparison reference → `a288e25` "HSR DreamWriter - complete product redesign" + `b5baf34` the same-day old-code deletion), hand-curated the entire HSR canon knowledge pack (5 characters with their specific 性格特征 / 说话方式 / 情感触发点 — this is bespoke fandom-domain knowledge, not scraped), wrote the 7-node adaptive LangGraph topology including the conditional revision edge, designed the Prisma schema (21 models including the polymorphic `Notification.payload: Json`, the self-referential `Comment.parentId` for nested replies, the deliberate split between `KnowledgeChunk` for RAG and `SourceResearchCache` for Tavily caching, the `UserCredits` + `Generation` audit foundation), wrote both Dockerfiles + the Coolify compose file + the GitHub Actions deploy workflow, executed the Railway → DigitalOcean + Coolify migration (and *documented* it as a reusable playbook), wrote the pgvector + pg_trgm migrations and the `lib/story-embedding.ts` raw-SQL escape hatch, and built the entire community-feature sprint (comments, notifications, follows, related stories, search indexing, view counts, SEO, dark mode, reader preferences, avatar cropping) in the final ~96-hour push 2026-04-18 → 2026-04-21. The project doubles as a personal R&D lab for the rest of the portfolio: the Coolify-on-DO migration playbook informed similar deployments in archcanvas (Workers → DO VPS), the GitHub-Actions-to-GHCR-to-SSH pattern reappeared in subsequent multi-service projects, and the pgvector + ivfflat + cosine-similarity recipe became the template I reused for related-content recommendations elsewhere.

projects.name: ArchCanvas
projects.tagline: 'Your AI architect — dimensioned floor plans you can actually build from.' An AI design agent that turns a natural-language brief into ONE strong floor plan — written as parametric ArchLang (Chan's own DSL), compiled to real dimensioned SVG, plus a GPT-Image rendering grounded on that exact plan — then refined conversationally on an infinite zoomable canvas where edits morph in place. Select-to-act editing, a review mode that overlays circulation and pins critique, parametric sliders, and export of the whole design history as a real git repo. Refinement mutates the plan in place — it never regenerates it.
techStack: Vercel AI SDK (ai 4.3, @ai-sdk/openai) + OpenAI 6.45 — orchestrator migrated off GPT-4o to a newer reasoning model for tool use; GPT-4o retained for vision
GPT Image grounded renderings (view-aware model chain: aerial vs eye-level)
@chanmeng666/archlang ^1.18.0 (Chan's own declarative floor-plan compiler, consumed as a package)
Neon Postgres + Drizzle ORM (9 tables, schema-push)
isomorphic-git + memfs + fflate (export design history as a real git repo, in-memory)
Zod validation · shadcn/ui + Tailwind 4 + Framer Motion + GSAP
Docker canary-then-swap deploy over SSH (DigitalOcean VPS, Traefik + Let's Encrypt, Cloudflare Full-Strict)
keywords: Parametric ArchLang DSL under the hood (not freehand SVG)
Infinite zoomable design canvas with in-place morphing edits
Select-to-act room editing + parametric sliders (no-LLM recompile)
Review mode — circulation overlay + anchored critique + one-click have-AI-fix
Design history exported as a real git repository

projects.publicSummary: ArchCanvas is an AI design agent for architects and self-builders. Describe a building in plain language and it asks a few questions, then designs one dimensioned floor plan you can actually build from — with room labels and measurements — alongside a realistic AI rendering of it. From there it stays conversational: on an infinite zoomable canvas you refine the plan by talking to it, scrub through every past version, and export the whole design as files or as a full change history.

narrative.impactHeadline: An AI design agent for architects and self-builders: describe a building in plain words and it designs a dimensioned floor plan you can actually build from, plus a realistic rendering — then you refine it by talking, on an infinite zoomable canvas.
narrative.problem: AI floor-plan tools produce pretty pictures you cannot build from and cannot precisely edit. An image-generated plan has no real dimensions, no guarantee a wall is straight, and no handle to grab when you want the kitchen two metres wider — you re-prompt and hope. Meanwhile professional CAD is powerful but slow and expert-only, and neither world lets you simply *talk* to your design. ArchCanvas closes that gap: a plan you can converse your way to, that is nonetheless a real, dimensioned, buildable artifact you can keep editing precisely.
narrative.audience: Architects and designers who want to move fast from brief to a credible dimensioned plan, and self-builders / renovators who have a vision but not CAD skills and need something they (or their builder) can actually work from. Because every plan is a real floor-plan program underneath, the same product serves a careful professional nudging a wall thickness with a slider and a first-timer who just wants to describe their dream house and see it — and refine it — in plain language.
narrative.productLogic:
The studio runs in two acts. **Act I** is a full-screen briefing conversation: the user picks a typology (residential, accessible clinic, open-plan office, passive house) and describes the build; the agent asks two-to-five clarifying questions inline, then generates ONE plan — deliberately not a grid of options — as parametric ArchLang, compiles it to a dimensioned SVG with `data-arch-id` anchors, renders a grounded AI visualization, and critiques it. That single turn is the only point at which the agent is asked for a brand-new plan.

**Act II** is a canvas-first studio, and nothing in it regenerates. Edits are conversational and **morph the plan in place** — the parent geometry fades to a dashed ghost, a semantic-diff chip shows what changed, and a version-lineage timeline lets you scrub the history. **Select-to-act**: click a room, door, or window and a floating action bar offers verb chips (Enlarge / Shrink / Widen / Move) plus agent-suggested scoped edits. A **Parametric Tweaks** panel turns the plan's own variables (room sizes, counts, wall thickness) into live sliders that recompile instantly with no AI call at all. **Review mode** toggles a walking-circulation overlay (routes, pinch points) and pins anchored critique findings, each with a one-click "have AI fix" — display-only, never auto-applied. The finished design exports as SVG/PNG/DXF/PDF, or as a full versioned change history whose log *is* the design conversation.
narrative.businessLogic: The unit of work is one generation turn — plan + grounded rendering + critique, produced as a single atomic set — and everything downstream of it is refinement rather than regeneration. That split is deliberate: a design task's value is lumpy (you want a few strong plans, then iterate on them for a long time), so every edit, parametric tweak, re-render, and review action mutates the existing frame in place, and the model is re-invoked only when the user genuinely asks for new work. The advantage is the ArchLang pairing: because plans are a deterministic program rather than pixels, ArchCanvas can offer editing, tweaking, diffing, and change-history export that an image-generation competitor structurally cannot.
narrative.technicalApproach: A single Next.js 15 monolith (React 19, TypeScript 5.8) over a 9-table Drizzle schema on Neon Postgres, with Stripe credits, Cloudinary rendering storage, and jose-based auth. The AI layer is an orchestrated tool-agent on the Vercel AI SDK: the orchestrator was deliberately migrated off GPT-4o (which under-used tools and wrote non-parametric DSL) to **gpt-5.5**, while GPT-4o is retained for vision (reference-image analysis). Its tools center on `generateFloorPlanDSL` — write parametric ArchLang, compile it through the `@chanmeng666/archlang` package (now 1.18), pass a ship-gate, save a frame — with `editFloorPlanDSL` as the minimal-diff follow-up. Renderings are a **grounded, view-aware chain**: the compiled plan is rasterized and sent as the grounding image to GPT Image 2, with model order chosen per view (aerial vs eye-level) so the render matches the real geometry instead of hallucinating a layout. `designFrames` stores each version's ArchLang source, compiled SVG, rendering URLs, lint warnings, review findings, and a self-referential `parentFrameId` for lineage — which is exactly what the in-memory change-history export (isomorphic-git + memfs, zipped with fflate) walks root-to-tip. Deployment is a canary-then-swap over SSH: build a Docker image, push to GHCR, boot a health-checked canary, and swap traffic only if `/api/health` passes — otherwise abort untouched.
narrative.outcomes: Live in production at archcanvas.uk, solo-built across roughly 295 commits over three months (2026-04-08 → 2026-07-16): 13 pages, 28 API route handlers, 71 components, and 205 source files over a 9-table schema. The product is unusually rigorous about testing its own thesis — a `generationAttempts` telemetry table tracks first-pass ArchLang validity per model, and a `bench` harness runs briefs through both the DSL pipeline and a legacy freehand-pixel pipeline as a control group (now with timing and token axes), so the "DSL beats pixels" claim is measured, not asserted. It is also the proving ground that hardened ArchLang: real in-browser use of the compiler drove concrete upstream fixes, most recently the 1.18 topology work.
narrative.myRole: Sole author of the entire product — the two-act studio and canvas UX, the AI orchestrator and its tools, the grounded view-aware rendering chain, the ArchLang integration, the 9-table schema and credit/billing system, the change-history export, the falsifiability instruments, and the canary-then-swap deployment. And of ArchLang itself, the floor-plan language it is built on. Every commit is mine.

projects.publicSummary:
Production **full-stack text-to-image SaaS** — image generation
powered by **Cloudflare Workers AI's FLUX.1 Schnell** 12B-parameter
model accessed through the in-platform `env.AI` binding (no
external API key, 100k requests/day on the free tier), wrapped in
a complete SaaS surface: **Neon Auth + Stripe credits ledger +
Neon Postgres image storage + gallery + (auth) and (protected)
Next.js route groups**. The interesting story is the **two
architectural pivots** the project went through to get here:
it started 2024-11-27 as a Vercel-hosted Next.js demo using a
paid AI provider, ran in that shape for ~16 months gathering
iteration ideas, then on **2026-04-09 pivoted to Cloudflare
Workers with free-tier FLUX.1 Schnell** (commit `3aec6ce`,
*"migrate from Vercel to Cloudflare Workers with free AI image
generation"*), and on **2026-04-10** — one day later —
transformed from "free demo" to **full SaaS in a single commit**
`8626be4` *"add authentication, credits, payments, and image
storage"*. UI is a deliberate **Mondrian-inspired** geometric
composition (later refined to minimalist grayscale on
2026-04-20). Stack: Next.js 15.5 + React 19.2 + Drizzle ORM +
Neon Postgres + Better Auth + Stripe 22 + OpenTelemetry +
`@opennextjs/cloudflare` adapter. Live at
[image-generator.chanmeng-dev.workers.dev](https://image-generator.chanmeng-dev.workers.dev/).
Apache-2.0 with patent grant. 24 solo commits across ~17 months
of sustained iteration.

narrative.impactHeadline: Full-stack text-to-image SaaS pivoted onto Cloudflare Workers AI's FLUX.1 Schnell free tier (100k req/day) — auth, credits ledger, Stripe-integrated payments, image storage, gallery; 17 months of sustained development from Vercel demo to free-tier production SaaS.
narrative.problem:
Text-to-image AI tools sit in an awkward economic position:
the model inference (DALL-E, Stable Diffusion XL, Midjourney
API) costs real money per image, so every demo on the
internet is either rate-limited to single-digit images per
user, hidden behind a sign-up wall, or quietly burning
someone's API budget on every public generation. Building a
consumer-facing image generator that's **actually free at
meaningful volume** requires either subsidising the inference
cost out of pocket or finding a provider whose free tier is
large enough to underwrite a public deployment. At the same
time, the few "free" public image generators that do exist
cap at a few hundred generations per day and ship as
single-page demos with no persistence — you can't see your
history, can't save generations to an account, can't share
a gallery, can't upgrade to higher volume even if you
wanted to pay. The product gap is **a full-stack image SaaS
whose unit economics work because the inference layer is
actually free at the volumes hobbyists need, with an upgrade
path for users who want persistence + paid credits**.
narrative.audience:
Three concentric audiences. (1) **Casual creators**
(designers, content makers, hobbyists) who want to generate
images for blog headers, social posts, mood boards, or
decoration without signing up for a hosted image service or
wrestling with self-hosted Stable Diffusion — the free tier
covers them. (2) **Power users** who hit the free tier ceiling
and want a credit-based top-up path with persistent gallery
and account-tied image history — Stripe credits ledger
covers them. (3) **Engineers studying the project as a worked
example** of (a) discovering a free-AI free-tier large enough
to underwrite a public product, (b) layering a full SaaS on
top in 48 hours, (c) the @opennextjs/cloudflare adapter
pattern for Next.js 15 App Router on Cloudflare Workers.
narrative.productLogic:
The product surface is intentionally split between **free
anonymous mode** and **authenticated SaaS mode** through
Next.js 15 route groups. The default `/` route is
unauthenticated: a Mondrian-inspired geometric background
with a single text-area prompt input, generate button, and
image display. Anyone can generate images via the
`/api/generate` POST endpoint which routes to the
Cloudflare Workers AI binding (`env.AI.run('@cf/black-
forest-labs/flux-1-schnell', { prompt, steps: 4 })`), returns
a base64-encoded PNG, and the client renders it via a
`data:image/png;base64,…` URI. **Free tier ceiling**:
100,000 image-generation requests per day, enforced by
Cloudflare's Workers AI quota — that's enough to underwrite
a public deployment indefinitely for hobbyist traffic. Sign
in (via the `(auth)/` route group) and the same prompt
input now lives in the `(protected)/` route group with
added persistence: every generation goes through the
`/api/images` endpoint to write metadata + the base64 blob
to Neon Postgres via Drizzle ORM, indexed by a `cuid2`
collision-resistant ID, and shows up in the `/gallery`
view. Credits work as a usage cap on the authenticated
path — `/api/credits` reads the user's ledger, `/api/stripe/
checkout` creates a Stripe Checkout session for the chosen
credit pack, and the `/api/stripe/webhook` route receives
the signed event from Stripe (using the `stripe:listen`
and `stripe:trigger` dev scripts for local testing) and
atomically debits/credits the ledger. **The auto test/live
Stripe mode switch** (commit `00791c0`) detects the
`STRIPE_SECRET_KEY` prefix and routes traffic accordingly
so demos can run against test keys without environment
re-config.
narrative.businessLogic:
The architectural insight that makes the project viable:
**the Cloudflare Workers AI free tier underwrites the
inference path on public traffic**. 100,000 generations/day
is the load-bearing constraint that lets the surface stay
open while a credits-ledger upgrade path scales power-user
capacity. Without that free-tier ceiling the choice would
have been: cap everyone, or rate-limit so aggressively the
demo isn't useful. The pivot to Cloudflare Workers AI (commit
`3aec6ce`) was the structural decision that made the
whole SaaS shape workable. Above the free tier, a
Stripe-integrated credits ledger (test/live mode
auto-switching, webhook-verified) provides the capacity
upgrade path. Storage on Neon's serverless Postgres keeps
persistence light at typical hobbyist volume. Net: the
product is **open for the long-tail majority of users** and
scales gracefully for the small minority who exhaust that
free-tier headroom. Apache-2.0 with patent grant — explicitly
licensed for commercial use, fork, and re-deployment.
narrative.technicalApproach:
**AI layer.** Cloudflare Workers AI is accessed through
the **in-platform `env.AI` binding** declared in
`wrangler.jsonc` (`{"ai": {"binding": "AI"}}`) — no
external API key required, no network hop to a third-party
provider, all inference runs inside Cloudflare's edge.
The model is **FLUX.1 Schnell** (12B parameters,
`@cf/black-forest-labs/flux-1-schnell`) called with
`steps: 4` (1-8 range — fewer steps = faster, more
steps = higher quality). Response is base64-encoded PNG
returned as a JSON string, rendered client-side via a
`data:image/png;base64,…` URI for zero-additional-request
display. **Full-stack SaaS layer.** Next.js 15.5 App
Router with `(auth)/` and `(protected)/` **route groups**
for clean separation of unauth/auth surfaces;
`/api/generate` (free public path), `/api/auth/[...path]`
(Neon Auth handler), `/api/credits` (ledger reads),
`/api/images` (gallery persistence), `/api/gallery`
(public gallery), `/api/stripe/checkout` + `/api/stripe/
webhook` (billing). **Database**: Neon Postgres via the
`@neondatabase/serverless` HTTP driver (better fit for
Workers' stateless runtime than connection-pooled drivers
— no pool exhaustion, no pgbouncer hop), schema in
Drizzle ORM. **Auth**: Neon Auth (Better Auth beta) with
a committed `better-auth-migration.sql` for schema setup.
**IDs**: `@paralleldrive/cuid2` for collision-resistant
URL-safe identifiers (better than UUIDs for public-facing
image gallery URLs). **Observability**: `@opentelemetry/
api` instrumentation hooks. **Deployment**: standard
`@opennextjs/cloudflare` 1.18 adapter — `npm run deploy`
runs `opennextjs-cloudflare build && opennextjs-cloudflare
deploy`, building the Next.js app and bundling it through
the OpenNext compiler into a Workers-compatible
single-file bundle. Same deployment pattern Chan uses on
sunostats, send-joy, chinese-redefine, and the event-qa
template — the pattern is now a portable skill. **UI**:
deliberate **Mondrian-inspired geometric composition** in
the initial design phase (`MondrianBackground.tsx` is a
first-class component), later refined to a **minimalist
grayscale aesthetic** on 2026-04-20 (commit `10c5a0d`).
Both layers are intentional design choices, not
accidental theming. **Build**: `npm run cf-typegen` runs
`wrangler types --env-interface CloudflareEnv` to
regenerate the TypeScript bindings file for the AI
binding, keeping the type system in sync with the live
Cloudflare environment.
narrative.outcomes:
Live at
[image-generator.chanmeng-dev.workers.dev](https://image-generator.chanmeng-dev.workers.dev/)
with the full SaaS surface active and operating on the
Cloudflare Workers free tier across both inference (Workers
AI 100k/day) and database (Neon free tier). **17 months of
sustained development across 24 solo commits**, with the
defining narrative being the **two architectural pivots in
48 hours**: 2026-04-09 (`3aec6ce`) migrated the inference
layer from Vercel + paid provider to Cloudflare Workers +
free FLUX.1 Schnell — cutting unit cost to zero; 2026-04-10
(`8626be4`) added the entire SaaS layer (auth + credits +
Stripe + image storage) in a single commit, transforming a
free demo into a fully-monetisable product with an upgrade
path. The Mondrian-inspired design layer was added across
the 2024-12 window (commits including `7717b8f` "Add logo")
and refined to minimalist grayscale on 2026-04-20
(`10c5a0d` *"redesign UI/UX with minimalist grayscale
aesthetic"*). Stripe test/live auto-mode-switching
(`00791c0`) followed the next day. **Apache-2.0 with
patent grant** licensing makes the codebase explicitly
fork-and-deploy-able as a worked example for engineers
wanting to ship their own free-tier AI SaaS on Cloudflare
Workers — the entire deployment fits inside Cloudflare's
and Neon's free tiers for typical hobbyist volume.
narrative.myRole:
Sole product designer, sole engineer, sole maintainer.
Identified the Cloudflare Workers AI free-tier free-FLUX
opportunity in early 2026 and executed the pivot from
Vercel + paid AI provider to Workers + free Workers AI
binding within a single commit (`3aec6ce`); designed the
SaaS layer extension (auth + credits + Stripe + image
storage) and shipped it the next day (`8626be4`);
designed the dual-surface UX with Next.js 15
`(auth)/`/`(protected)/` route groups so anonymous users
keep the free generation path; designed the
Mondrian-inspired geometric UI and the subsequent
minimalist-grayscale refinement; engineered the Drizzle
ORM + Neon Postgres + Neon Auth (Better Auth)
persistence stack; wrote the Stripe test/live auto-mode
switching logic; configured the @opennextjs/cloudflare
deployment pipeline. Maintains the live deployment today.

projects.name: Chinese Redefine 汉语新解 — AI Word Redefinition
projects.tagline: 汉语新解 — a full-stack AI app that reinterprets everyday Chinese vocabulary with sharp, humorous, philosophically-loaded takes (productizing Li Jigang's 李继刚 viral prompt) via Google Gemini, exports shareable SVG cards, gated by a two-layer rate-limit quota system, deployed on Cloudflare Workers via OpenNext. Low on raw technical difficulty, high on cultural/philosophical depth
projects.publicSummary:
Full-stack AI application that reinterprets traditional Chinese
vocabulary through Google Gemini and exports the results as
shareable, vermillion-seal-themed SVG cards. Production deployment
includes user authentication (Neon Auth, migrated from self-hosted
Better Auth), Neon Postgres + Drizzle ORM for cards and quota
persistence, a two-tier rate-limit system (per-user free/pro quotas
plus a global daily/monthly circuit breaker), public gallery,
"My Cards" private collection, Resend-powered OTP email
verification, and Cloudflare Workers deployment via
`@opennextjs/cloudflare`. Modern Chinese Ink aesthetic UI
redesigned March 2026.

narrative.impactHeadline: Full-stack AI app reinterpreting traditional Chinese vocabulary through Google Gemini — exports shareable SVG cards, gated by a two-layer rate-limit quota system, ~95% solo across 60 commits, deployed on Cloudflare Workers via OpenNext.
narrative.problem:
Generic LLM chat windows are bad at *creative cultural
reinterpretation*: ask Gemini directly to "redefine 婚姻" and
you get a paragraph of generic prose with no shape, no
shareability, no persistence, and no protection against
adversarial users burning through API quota. The
Chinese-language ecosystem in particular needs a tool that
treats vocabulary as a *cultural object* — produces a result
worth screenshotting, saves it for the user to revisit, and
lets the curated best ones surface to a public gallery — all
while staying within a sustainable cost envelope for a solo
operator.
narrative.audience:
Three groups. **Chinese language learners and cultural
researchers** who want fresh perspectives on traditional
vocabulary — the core product use case. **Content creators
and designers** who want a one-tap path from word → AI
interpretation → exportable shareable visual card. **Engineering
portfolio audiences** (recruiters, founders, the Engram-style
Claude-Code-sourcing case) who use the project as proof that
Chan ships full-stack AI applications end-to-end — not just
prompts, but the auth + DB + quota + deployment scaffolding
around them.
narrative.productLogic:
A user lands on the site, types a Chinese word in the input
form, and sees a real-time Gemini-generated reinterpretation
rendered as a stylised card. They can preview the SVG version,
download it as a high-resolution shareable image, or — once
signed in via OAuth or email-OTP — save it to their personal
"My Cards" collection. The public **Gallery** surfaces curated
community cards. A live **Quota Display** widget shows the
user's remaining daily and monthly allowance. Authentication
uses Neon Auth's pre-built flows (login, register, email
verification via Resend-sent OTP, forgot-password, OAuth
callback).
narrative.businessLogic:
Theory of design for a solo-operator AI product: build the
capacity-control layer *into the application* from the start.
The two-layer quota system — per-user tier (free: 1/min ·
3/day · 30/month; higher tier: 5/min · 30/day · 300/month)
plus a **global circuit breaker** (150 generations/day ·
1,500/month across all users) — means Gemini capacity is
bounded structurally, not by trust. The product itself is
free to play with so the gallery accumulates real content;
higher tiers exist as the capacity surface for power users;
the global cap prevents a single bad actor (or a viral moment)
from triggering runaway capacity drain. The architecture is
the access model.
narrative.technicalApproach:
**Next.js 15 App Router** with grouped routes:
`(auth)` — login, register, verify-email (OTP input),
forgot-password, debug; `(protected)` — account / cards (the
signed-in dashboard); top-level `gallery` (public). API routes
under `app/api/`: `generate` (Gemini call), `cards`,
`gallery`, `auth`, `debug`. UI built with **shadcn/ui** atop
Radix primitives + **Framer Motion** animations + a
deliberate **Modern Chinese Ink aesthetic** (vermillion seal
logo, ink-wash typography, redesigned end-to-end March 2026).

**Persistence layer**: **Drizzle ORM** over **Neon serverless
Postgres** with three core tables — `cards` (the saved AI
outputs), `userUsage` (per-user daily/monthly counters keyed
by date), `globalUsage` (the circuit-breaker counters).
Drizzle Kit for migrations. CUID2 (`@paralleldrive/cuid2`)
for ID generation — collision-resistant, sortable, shorter
than UUIDs.

**Auth layer**: **Neon Auth**, migrated from a self-hosted
Better Auth implementation in March 2026. The migration
rewrote middleware whitelists (callback URLs for OAuth,
verification-flow query parameters, static assets like
`icon.svg`), formalised the email-OTP flow with explicit
success guidance and resend capability, and switched social
OAuth to absolute callback URLs to work correctly under
Cloudflare Workers' edge-runtime constraints.

**AI layer**: Google **Gemini API** for generation, fronted by
the two-layer rate-limit module in `lib/rate-limit.ts`. Every
generation request: (1) checks user tier limits against
`userUsage`; (2) checks the **global circuit breaker** against
`globalUsage`; (3) only if both pass, calls Gemini; (4) writes
the result to `cards` and increments both counters atomically.

**Email**: **Resend** for transactional OTP delivery during
email verification and password reset.

**Deployment**: **Cloudflare Workers** via
**`@opennextjs/cloudflare`** + **Wrangler** — a non-trivial
migration from the original Vercel deployment, because Workers'
edge runtime requires absolute URLs for OAuth callbacks and
differs from Node-runtime assumptions in subtle ways (every
edge-runtime crash during the migration is in the commit log
as a separate fix).
narrative.outcomes:
Live at **chinese-redefine.chanmeng-dev.workers.dev**. Solo
development: **60 commits, ~95% Chan-authored** across two
GitHub identities. The Vercel → Cloudflare Workers migration
and the Better Auth → Neon Auth migration were both completed
in a focused March 30, 2026 sprint — the commit log captures
each edge-runtime crash and OAuth callback bug in sequence as
they were fixed. Modern Chinese Ink aesthetic redesign
(vermillion seal logo, ink-wash typography) shipped the same
day. The gallery serves as a living portfolio of AI-generated
Chinese-word reinterpretations.
narrative.myRole:
Sole engineer and sole designer. Architected the Next.js App
Router structure, wrote the two-layer rate-limit / quota
engine, designed the three-table Drizzle schema, executed the
Better Auth → Neon Auth migration (including middleware
whitelists, OAuth callback rewriting, OTP verification flow,
and email-passing via URL params to the verify-email page),
executed the Vercel → Cloudflare Workers migration via
`@opennextjs/cloudflare`, built every shadcn/ui component
composition (`CardGenerator`, `CardThumbnail`, `ResultCard`,
`SVGPreview`, `DownloadButton`, `GalleryGrid`, `AuthForm`,
`QuotaDisplay`, `InputForm`, `DeveloperSection`, `Navbar`),
designed and implemented the SVG generation system in
`lib/svgGenerator.ts`, and shipped the Modern Chinese Ink
aesthetic redesign end-to-end.
