projects.name: AI Programming Education Platform (programming.chanmeng.org)
projects.tagline: Bilingual Docusaurus teaching platform — three years of AI/programming cohorts running (five cohort versions side-by-side, three of them concurrent in 2026) with an in-course RAG AI assistant on Cloudflare Workers (Llama 3.1 8B + Vectorize + KV), Notion-backed Capstone voting, and Typst-generated PDF handouts; the active home for the 2026 TechNest AI Track and the clearest evidence of Chan as an AI educator/evangelist, not only a builder
projects.publicSummary: Production teaching platform at programming.chanmeng.org where beginners learn to build real software with AI tools, taught across three years of cohorts. Five course versions stay online side by side — from the 2024 class to three separate 2026 tracks (TechNest, Peyvand Academy, Her Waka) — each with video lessons, lab guides, and downloadable Typst-generated PDF notes. A built-in AI tutor (a RAG assistant on Cloudflare Workers AI, Llama 3.1 8B) answers questions from a student's own cohort material, and a Demo Day page publishes the projects students shipped. Bilingual EN + 简体中文. 211 of 220 non-merge commits solo (~96%), actively maintained through the 2026 TechNest cohort.

narrative.impactHeadline: A public website that teaches beginners to build software with AI — three years of cohorts kept side by side (five course versions, three running in 2026), plus an in-site AI tutor that answers from each class's own lessons and a Demo Day page for students' finished projects.
narrative.problem: AI-programming cohort programmes typically die between cohorts: the lecture deck is rebuilt from scratch each round, students from the previous cohort lose access to their materials, and the "AI tutor" experience — when it exists at all — is a chatbot tab the student opens separately, with no idea which cohort's curriculum they're studying. Meanwhile the instructor has no way to keep older cohorts permanently retrievable without disturbing the live one. The result: every cohort feels like a one-shot, course content compounds nowhere, and the AI-assisted-development experience is itself unassisted by AI.
narrative.audience: Three concentric layers. Active cohort students — the 2026 TechNest AI Track, plus the 2026 Peyvand Academy and academyEX Her Waka programmes — who watch video recordings, follow lab guides, download PDF handouts, and ask the in-site AI tutor version-aware questions. Past cohort alumni (2024 Winter, 2025 Summer) who keep stable URLs to their original materials. Public learners and prospective employers who reach programming.chanmeng.org from the profile and use it as proof of production-grade EdTech built with native AI, not just slides.
narrative.productLogic: Visitors land on programming.chanmeng.org and the docs default to the 2026 TechNest version (the active cohort), while the version switcher exposes 2026 Her Waka, 2026 Peyvand Academy, 2025 Summer, and 2024 Winter side by side — an alum of any cohort hits a stable permalink. Each version ships its own Video Tutorials section, weekly lab guides as both web docs and downloadable Typst-generated PDFs, and a Mermaid dual-view diagram (the same diagram in two presentations so visual and textual learners both succeed). A floating AI Chat Assistant answers in natural language by retrieving the current cohort's content first (version-aware retrieval), then falling back to other cohorts and the blog. During Demo Day the TechNest cohort gets a public Capstone Showcase where invited guests view each team's finished project and vote through a Notion-backed flow.
narrative.businessLogic: Theory of change: one teaching platform that compounds cohort over cohort instead of resetting each round. Once the 2024 Winter materials are versioned in, they stay at the same URL forever; the next cohort's materials sit alongside them without conflict. The AI tutor means a student writing code at midnight gets context-aware help against their own cohort's syllabus, not generic chatbot answers. For Chan personally: the platform is production-grade EdTech engineering (Cloudflare Workers AI + Vectorize + versioned bilingual docs + a PDF pipeline) built while doing actual teaching work — every commit is both a portfolio artefact and student value.
narrative.technicalApproach:
Built on Docusaurus 3.8.1 with the experimental @docusaurus/faster build pipeline. Five cohort versions coexist through Docusaurus versioned docs — each version is a self-contained doc tree with its own sidebar, blog feed, and tags. Bilingual i18n is end-to-end: EN primary, 简体中文 secondary, with Algolia DocSearch set to contextual locale + version filtering so a Mandarin-speaking 2026 TechNest student gets 2026 TechNest results in Chinese by default.

RAG AI assistant on Cloudflare Workers AI. A separate Cloudflare Worker (worker/src/index.ts) handles POST /api/chat behind the custom domain programming-api.chanmeng.org:
- the query is embedded with BGE Base EN v1.5 (768-dim) and sent to Cloudflare Vectorize, which returns the top chunks from the indexed corpus (~4,800 chunks spanning all five cohort versions + the blog, keyed by stable content-addressed chunk IDs so re-indexing is idempotent)
- retrieval is version-aware: a 2026-technest student gets 2026-technest results by default, with older cohorts available on explicit ask
- the context is passed to Workers AI Llama 3.1 8B Instruct (fp8) which generates the answer, streamed back to the in-site ChatWidget over Server-Sent Events; per-session history persists in Cloudflare KV
- the chat endpoint is rate-limited (per-IP, plus a Cloudflare WAF burst rule) with an explicit CORS allow-list

Typst PDF handout pipeline. The same Typst skill taught in the curriculum is the build tool that produces the printable curriculum outline, capstone spec, and per-week lab guides — students see in production the artefact they're taught to build. A Mermaid dual-view component renders every diagram twice (rendered + source) so visual and textual learners both succeed.

Capstone classroom infrastructure. A Cloudflare Worker proxies writes into Notion so cohort owners moderate inside the tool they already use; the Capstone 2026 public showcase lists each team's demo with live Notion-backed voting open to invited guests. Deployed end-to-end on Cloudflare's edge: Pages for the static Docusaurus build, Workers for the chat + capstone backend, Vectorize for the vector index, KV for sessions and votes.
narrative.outcomes: Live at programming.chanmeng.org, in active use as the official platform for the 2026 TechNest AI Track and the 2026 academyEX Her Waka programme. 211 of 220 non-merge commits (95.9%) solo across Chan's two GitHub identities; the remainder is AI auto-review + Dependabot. Five cohort versions run side by side without interfering. The RAG index covers all five versions + the blog (~4,800 chunks), with version-aware retrieval and stable chunk IDs. After Cloudflare deprecated the original chat model, the assistant was restored on 2026-07-10 by swapping to Llama 3.1 8B Instruct (fp8), alongside a move to a custom API domain, per-IP rate limiting, a CORS allow-list, and a WAF rule. Course recordings continue through the TechNest cohort's 8-week curriculum, and Demo Day 2026 now has a public Capstone Showcase with published student projects (CreditHero, iCare, JobOrg-AI) and guest voting. DeepWiki provides an external AI doc-Q&A surface at deepwiki.com/ChanMeng666/ai-programming-teaching-project.
narrative.myRole: I built and run the entire platform end-to-end: Docusaurus configuration, the versioned-docs taxonomy across five cohorts, every cohort's curriculum content, the Cloudflare Worker RAG backend (worker entry, chat handler, RAG module, Vectorize seeding, KV session store), the BGE-Base embeddings ingestion pipeline, the Typst PDF handout build, the Mermaid dual-view component, the Notion-Worker proxy and Demo Day showcase for Capstone voting, the Algolia contextual-search configuration, and the bilingual i18n across every surface. I migrated production from Vercel to Cloudflare Pages in 2026-04, and in 2026-07 hardened the chat backend (custom domain, rate limiting, CORS allow-list, WAF) and restored it onto Cloudflare's current model after the old one was deprecated. I continue to ship the current cohort's recordings and lab content each week.

projects.name: Te Pa Tiaki — CSS 3D Tower Defense (Guardians of Aotearoa)
projects.tagline: A 3D tower-defense game rendered 100% with CSS 3D transforms — zero Canvas, zero WebGL, zero engine (a hand-built CSS 3D rendering engine) — themed end-to-end on Māori mythology of Aotearoa and inspired by Waitangi Day, with a full Cloudflare Workers + Hono + Neon Postgres backend (auth, leaderboards, cloud saves, anti-cheat)
projects.publicSummary:
A full **3D isometric tower-defense game** rendered entirely through
CSS 3D transforms on real DOM elements — no `<canvas>`, no WebGL,
no game engine. Every tower, enemy, projectile, particle, and
weather effect is a styled DOM node at a 45-degree isometric
perspective. Themed end-to-end on **Maori mythology and culture of
Aotearoa**: six towers named after Maori weapons and atua (Taiaha,
Mere, Tangaroa, Tohunga, Tawhiri, Mahuika), six enemies from
Maori legend (Kehua, Patupaiarehe, Toa, Wairua, Tipua, Taniwha),
three maps (Te Awa, Te Maunga, Te Moana), three taonga materials
(Pipi, Koiwi, Pounamu). Backed by Cloudflare Workers + Hono +
Neon Postgres for auth, leaderboards, cloud saves (3 slots),
daily challenges, and server-side anti-cheat. 84 commits over
~2 months (Feb–Mar 2026), executed in disciplined P0–P4 phases.

narrative.impactHeadline: 3D tower-defense game rendered 100% with CSS 3D transforms — zero Canvas, zero WebGL, zero engine — themed end-to-end on Māori mythology of Aotearoa, with full Cloudflare Workers + Hono + Neon Postgres backend (server-side anti-cheat). 90 commits / ~99% solo over ~4.5 months.
narrative.problem:
Two prevailing pictures of "a portfolio game project" are both
wrong: (a) a trivial Canvas + JS toy that lasts one weekend
and looks it; (b) a Unity-shaped engine project that's
impressive but says nothing about web-platform fluency. There
is a third position the portfolio market doesn't routinely
produce: a **production-shaped game shipped end-to-end on raw
web primitives** — DOM + CSS — that proves both deep browser
engineering and design discipline. There is also a fourth
gap: a NZ-based developer's portfolio should *show*
engagement with Aotearoa rather than just claim residence,
and Maori mythology deserves more interactive cultural
representation than it usually gets.
narrative.audience:
Three concentric audiences. **Game players** — anyone who
clicks Play Now on towerdefense.chanmeng.org and can play
without an account thanks to fully-supported guest mode.
**Engineering recruiters and architects** evaluating the
portfolio: the game is proof of CSS-engine fluency, vanilla
ES6 architecture discipline, full-stack ops (Workers + Hono +
Neon), and the kind of phased product execution
(P0 → P1 → P2 → P3 → P4) usually only seen in shipped
products. **The Aotearoa cultural audience**: locals, the
Maori community, NZ tech employers — anyone for whom
respectful engagement with te reo and Maori cosmology is a
meaningful signal alongside the technical work.
narrative.productLogic:
Players load the game in-browser, optionally sign in for
cloud progression (or stay in guest mode), pick a difficulty
(Normal / Hard / Expert), choose a map (Te Awa, Te Maunga,
or Te Moana), and play through **10 progressive waves**
defending against six Maori-legend enemy archetypes plus
four variant tiers (Rangatira elite, Pakanga armored, Tere
swift, Mate deadly). Players place towers, gather **taonga**
(treasures: Pipi, Koiwi, Pounamu) dropped by enemies, craft
consumables from six recipes, build XP through a Tech Tree
of 10 upgrades, attempt **daily challenges** drawn from 20+
templates across six categories, and chase rank on
**leaderboards** (global and per-challenge). Endless mode
keeps wave 11+ available after victory. Server-side
anti-cheat validates submitted scores against expected
ranges. Cloud saves persist across 3 slots; guest mode keeps
progress in localStorage.
narrative.businessLogic:
Theory of differentiation for a non-commercial portfolio
artefact: be **specifically uncopyable**. A Canvas tower
defense is a five-page tutorial; a CSS-3D tower defense is
a multi-month engineering bet that signals *exactly* what
the candidate can do. Pair that with cultural engagement
most portfolios don't attempt — six towers and six enemies
whose names and roles draw on te reo Maori — and the
project becomes a single-sentence portfolio differentiator
in NZ-relevant interviews. The free, guest-playable web
deployment maximises the surface area: anyone reading the
portfolio can click Play and *experience* the engineering,
not just read about it.
narrative.technicalApproach:
**Rendering — 100% CSS 3D.** Every tower, enemy, projectile,
explosion, particle, weather effect is a real DOM element.
The world is a 45-degree isometric scene built with
`transform-style: preserve-3d` and 1200px CSS perspective.
Towers and enemies are constructed from `clip-path`,
`border-radius`, and pseudo-elements — *no emoji, no
images*. Visual style is comic / pop-art: halftone dot
patterns, hard-offset pop shadows, thick comic borders, a
warm `#FFF8F0` palette. **DOM object pooling** via
`Pool.acquire()` / `Pool.release()` recycles elements so
the scene stays at 60 FPS through dense wave combat.
**Dynamic theming** via CSS custom properties driven from
JS produces day/night, weather, and seasonal palette
transitions — same DOM, different CSS variables.

**Game logic — vanilla ES6.** No framework, no engine. The
codebase is organised as IIFE modules communicating
through an event-driven bus. Core systems include the
wave director, enemy AI, tower targeting, projectile
physics, the combo system (chain kills within 1.5s),
critical-hit probability, the mana ability system with
cooldowns, the Terraria-inspired **reforging** engine
(Common → Uncommon → Rare → Epic → Legendary prefixes),
and **tower synergies** that activate when adjacent towers
combine (Freeze Lightning, Fire Blast, Blessing, Array).

**Maori cultural integration is structural, not decorative.**
Towers carry the abilities their te reo names imply:
Tangaroa (ocean atua) slows enemies with ice, Mahuika (fire
goddess) deals damage-over-time, Tawhiri (storm god) chains
lightning. The Taniwha boss has three signature skills —
Karanga (call / summon minions), Kaitiaki (guardian /
shield), Te Riri (anger / enrage). Maps are place-words:
Te Awa (the river), Te Maunga (the mountain), Te Moana
(the ocean). Materials are taonga (treasures): Pipi
(shellfish), Koiwi (bone), Pounamu (greenstone). The
naming isn't surface skin — each mechanic is selected to
match the mythological meaning.

**Cloud backend.** Cloudflare Workers running the **Hono**
web framework, backed by **Neon serverless Postgres**.
Endpoints cover auth, leaderboards (global + per-challenge),
cloud saves (3 slots per user), stats tracking, and
daily-challenge rotation. **Server-side score validation**
rejects submissions outside expected ranges to defend the
leaderboard against cheating. **Guest mode is a
first-class path** — the entire game works without an
account; cloud sync activates the moment a player signs in.

**Audio.** **Web Audio API** synth generates SFX in real
time (firing, impact, death, level-up); **Howler.js**
handles music and longer samples. Music is *dynamic* — the
soundtrack adapts to game state (menu, playing, boss,
victory, defeat).

**Build pipeline.** **LESS** preprocessor for CSS modules,
**Gulp 4** for the build/watch pipeline, deployment via
Wrangler to Cloudflare Workers with the static assets
served from the same edge.
narrative.outcomes:
Live at **towerdefense.chanmeng.org**. **84 commits across
~2 months** (2026-02-06 → 2026-03-30), ~99% Chan-authored
across two GitHub identities. Execution followed an
explicit **P0 → P4 phase plan** visible in the commit log:
P0 (game feel — damage numbers, death bursts, firing
animations, wave transitions), P1 (onboarding & UX —
tutorial with spotlight cutout, hints, cooldown UI),
P2 (retention — expanded achievements, leaderboard filters,
celebrations), P3-P4 (deep systems — mana abilities, dynamic
music, endless mode). Plus the full content build:
synergies, new enemies, multi-map, daily-challenge overhaul,
cloud backend integration. Anti-cheat is in production and
actively rejecting out-of-range submissions. The 3D
isometric CSS rendering holds 60 FPS through dense
Wave-10 combat thanks to object pooling.
narrative.myRole:
Sole designer, sole engineer, sole audio architect. Designed
the CSS-3D rendering technique end-to-end (perspective,
preserve-3d, the comic/pop-art visual language, the DOM
object pool), wrote every game system (waves, AI,
physics, towers, enemies, synergies, reforging, combo,
mana, achievements, daily challenges, tech tree, crafting,
inventory), shipped the entire **Maori cultural design** —
choosing which te reo name maps to which mechanic so
meaning and gameplay reinforce each other — built the
Cloudflare Workers + Hono + Neon backend including
server-side anti-cheat validation, composed the dynamic
music state machine and the Web Audio synth effects,
wrote the Gulp + LESS build pipeline, and authored the
4-step interactive tutorial with spotlight overlays.

projects.name: Typst Skill for Claude Code
projects.tagline: Professional PDF generation via Claude Code with 9 ready-to-use templates — academic papers, resumes, slides, invoices, technical reports; full Typst CLI reference with CJK support
projects.publicSummary:
Claude Code skill that embeds the Typst typesetting engine into the
agent's workflow: a user describes the document they need in plain
English, Claude generates a `.typ` file from one of nine production
templates, and `typst compile` produces a PDF. Demonstrates
skill-structure design at the level needed for the agent design
pattern work in the Engram Architect Cohort engagement.

narrative.impactHeadline: Professional PDF generation via Claude Code with 9 ready-to-use templates (academic papers, resumes, slides, invoices, technical reports) — full Typst CLI reference with CJK support; the worked example Chan brings into Engram Architect Cohort skill-design discussions.
narrative.problem:
Claude Code has no built-in path to professional document output.
Users who need an academic paper, a typographic resume, a slide
deck, an invoice, or a CJK-friendly report fall back to MS Word,
LaTeX, or Markdown-to-PDF chains — none of which Claude can drive
natively, and none of which produce typographic-grade output.
narrative.audience:
Two distinct groups: (a) Claude Code users who need
production-quality PDFs without leaving the agent — academics,
job seekers, technical teams, anyone generating invoices or
presentations; (b) other agent-skill authors who want a worked
example of correct Claude Code skill structure (SKILL.md decision
trees, references loaded on demand, environment validation
scripts).
narrative.productLogic:
Install the skill once (four supported methods: marketplace
plugin, git clone, manual ZIP, or per-project `.claude/skills/typst/`
deployment), then describe the document in plain English. The
skill matches intent to one of nine bundled templates — general
document, CJK document, academic paper, simple resume,
typographic two-column resume, letter, technical report,
presentation slides, invoice — fills the template parameters
from the conversation, and runs `typst compile` to produce the
PDF. A `references/` folder holds the Typst language reference,
CLI documentation, template patterns, and design system; the
skill loads them on demand rather than dumping everything into
context.
narrative.technicalApproach:
Skill structure is the differentiator. The repository root
contains `skills/typst/` as the actual loadable artefact,
enabling **both** marketplace plugin distribution **and** direct
skill-folder installation without code duplication. `SKILL.md`
is the decision tree + workflow patterns + recovery procedures
— the spine Claude actually reads when invoking the skill.
`references/` is split into language reference, CLI docs,
templates, and design patterns so Claude can pull only the
relevant slice. `scripts/verify-typst.sh` runs as a precondition
check (Typst v0.14+, CJK font availability) so the skill fails
loudly and early rather than half-way through compilation.
Advanced patterns embedded: theme systems with cascading defaults,
scope-based show rules, modular component functions, multi-column
layouts, circular image clipping, typography hierarchy, native
CJK fallback chains.
narrative.outcomes:
Released v0.3.0 in April 2026, currently early adoption. The
deeper value isn't downloads — it's that this is the worked
example of "how to structure a Claude Code skill" that Chan
brings into the Engram Architect Cohort agent-design discussions
and that other skill authors can reference as a clean reference
implementation.
narrative.myRole:
Sole author. Designed the dual-distribution architecture
(marketplace plugin layout AND installable skill folder from a
single repo), authored all nine templates, wrote the SKILL.md
decision tree, and built the verify-typst.sh precondition system.

techStack: Tailwind CSS v3 + Framer Motion v12
Custom Quantum Orb 3D mascot (CSS 3D transforms + Three.js particles)
Sprite affection gamification engine (gesture detection, mood weighting, time-of-day bias)

localStorage state persistence (50-msg chat history + sprite affection state)
5-min FAQ client cache + optimistic vote/view updates
AI rate limiting + per-session token budgets + prompt guardrails (2026-07 security hardening)
OpenAI Realtime API over WebRTC — two-way voice (2026 relaunch)
Ephemeral client secrets minted server-side (OPENAI_API_KEY never reaches the browser)

projects.publicSummary:
Real industry-partnered AI event assistant built for the **AI
Hackathon Festival 2025** (Auckland, Aug 15–16, 2025), co-organised
by **Auckland University of Technology**, **AI Forum New Zealand**,
and **She Sharp**, with **UN Sustainable Development Goals** as
the innovation brief. The platform serves participants and mentors
through three interlocking systems: (1) an **interactive 3D sprite
mascot** with a 6-tier "Stranger → Soulmate" affection system,
7-state dynamic mood engine, 17 emotion categories, gesture
recognition (circle/pat/swipe), negative-behaviour penalties,
streak bonuses, and time-of-day emotion biases — a fully gamified
personality layer that responds to how users actually touch the
page; (2) a **Notion-as-database community FAQ** with up/downvote
ranking, view tracking, 5-minute client cache, and graceful static
fallback when Notion is unreachable; (3) an **OpenAI GPT-4o-mini
streaming chat** with markdown-rendered responses and one-click
FAQ-to-chat hand-off. After the live deployment, the codebase was
generalised into a reusable open-source template
(`event-qa-ai-template` on GitHub, MIT) so any future hackathon /
conference can fork-and-config the same stack. Chan was
subsequently recognised among the **14 expert mentors** at the
festival.

For the **2026 festival** (Aotearoa AI Hackathon Festival 2026,
AUT City Campus) the project was **rebuilt from the ground up** as
a single-page **voice-first** agent: the whole site is a centered,
audio-reactive Three.js orb that starts a **two-way voice
conversation over the OpenAI Realtime API via WebRTC** (the client
exchanges SDP directly with OpenAI using a short-lived ephemeral
secret, so the real API key never reaches the browser), with a
text-input fallback answered by gpt-4o-mini via the Vercel AI SDK,
live captions, and a collapsible event-info panel. Agent
instructions build from a Neon `knowledge` table (graceful
fallback to config), transcripts persist best-effort, and Upstash
KV rate-limits token minting. Deployed on Vercel
(`she-sharp1/aihackathon-2026`, live at aihackathon-2026.vercel.app).

narrative.impactHeadline: Voice-first AI event agent for the Aotearoa AI Hackathon Festival — relaunched 2026 (AUT) as an audio-reactive orb driving two-way OpenAI Realtime voice + gpt-4o-mini text chat; the 2025 edition served 11 teams / 80+ participants with a gamified 3D sprite mascot + Notion FAQ.
narrative.problem:
Hackathons and conferences generate the same Q&A surface every
year: participants ask the same 30 questions ("how do I form a
team", "what counts as AI", "when do I have to be on site",
"what's the prize structure", "where do I find a mentor"), and
organisers answer them via static PDFs that go stale the moment
the schedule shifts. Bolt-on chatbots exist but they treat
humans as passive consumers — a popup widget in the corner, a
knowledge base scraped at build time, no memory of what the
community itself finds helpful. Three structural gaps stack up:
(1) the FAQ is **authored once** rather than **curated by the
community**, so quality drifts; (2) the AI is a generic
wrapper around a chat API, with no event-specific personality
or theme tie-in; (3) organisers cannot update content without
a code redeploy, so the platform calcifies the moment the
original developer hands it off. The AI Hackathon Festival
2025 organisers needed a platform that solved all three on the
timeline of a real event with 80+ participants and three
co-organising institutions reviewing every word on screen.
narrative.audience:
Direct users were the **80+ participants and 14 expert mentors**
of AI Hackathon Festival 2025 — students from Auckland
universities, **Westlake Girls High School** secondary students,
and AI Forum NZ industry mentors all asking the same FAQ-style
questions across a 26-hour residential event. Secondary
audience: the **three co-organising institutions** (AUT, AI
Forum NZ, She Sharp) who use the platform as a public-facing
artefact of their joint programme. Tertiary audience: future
hackathon and conference organisers in NZ and abroad who can
fork the open-source template (`event-qa-ai-template`) and
deploy their own event Q&A AI in under an hour by editing four
configuration files — `site.config.ts`, `ai.config.ts`,
`content.config.ts`, `branding.config.ts` — without touching
component code.
narrative.productLogic:
The product is structured as three layers that compose
independently. Layer one is the **3D welcoming surface**: the
landing page renders a particle sphere with a "WELCOME TO AI
HACKATHON!" greeting set against a `CosmicBackground` Three.js
scene, with `Calendar` / `MapPin` event-info widgets, sponsor
logos for the three co-organisers, and a glassmorphism Google
Maps modal showing AUT WG Building. Layer two is the **chat
page** — a 70/30 desktop split between a full-screen OpenAI
chat (with `react-markdown` + `rehype-highlight` for
code-block syntax highlighting) and a community FAQ sidebar.
Layer three — the differentiator — is the **interactive sprite
mascot** that lives alongside the chat: a CSS-3D **Quantum
Singularity Orb** (accretion disk + photon ring + event
horizon + energy jets, all built from layered divs with
`transform-style: preserve-3d` and `rotateX(60deg)`) that
becomes a fully gamified personality. The user can **circle**,
**swipe vertically/horizontally**, **pat**, or **click** the
orb; the gesture detection engine tracks each interaction and
feeds it through an affection/mood scoring system. Five
gestures grant affection (circle x5 = +10 affection / "Wheee~!
So dizzy but fun!"), five negative behaviours penalise it
(`roughMovement`, `spamClick`, `suddenLeave`, `longIgnore`,
`excessiveCircle`), and the resulting state drives which of
17 emotions the sprite expresses — including three **rare**
emotions (`devoted`, `starry`, `mischievous`) only unlocked
at the top affection tiers. The result: users do not just chat
with an AI, they **build a relationship with a tiny digital
creature** while doing so — a memorable, shareable layer that
turns a Q&A bot into a piece of event identity.
narrative.businessLogic:
Three intertwined value propositions. For the **organising
institutions**, the platform is a public-facing demonstration
artefact of the joint AUT × AI Forum NZ × She Sharp programme,
running on Cloudflare Workers (free tier covers an event-week
traffic spike with zero cost) with a Notion database that
**non-developer event staff can edit live** during the event —
adding new FAQ entries through the Notion UI, watching them
appear within the 5-minute client cache window. For **future
event organisers**, after the live event, the codebase was
deliberately generalised into a **reusable MIT template**
(`event-qa-ai-template`) with all event-specific content
extracted into `config/*.ts` files and full setup
documentation, so the same stack ships any hackathon /
conference / community event Q&A platform in under an hour of
configuration. For **Chan**, the project established a
verifiable, AI-Forum-NZ-endorsed track record of shipping
commercial-grade AI infrastructure for an industry partner —
a credential cited in the public LinkedIn announcement of the
14-mentor cohort and in the Engram Architect Cohort
Engagement Letter narrative.
narrative.technicalApproach:
**AI layer.** OpenAI GPT-4o-mini-2024-07-18 served through
Vercel AI SDK v4.3's `streamText` with `convertToCoreMessages`,
returning `toDataStreamResponse()` for character-by-character
SSE streaming. The system prompt is **generated from
siteConfig at runtime** so any organiser fork inherits an
event-specific prompt without code edits — it embeds event
name, dates, venue, theme (UN SDGs), team-formation rules
(3-6 members, Hacker/Hipster/Hustler composition), schedule
(Friday 8 PM venue close, Discord for mentor requests), and
prize tiers ($250 venue, $1,000 national, $5,000 national
Agentic AI winner) plus Markdown formatting guidelines. The
codebase pivoted from Google Gemini 2.5 Flash to OpenAI
GPT-4o-mini mid-build (commit `5eb3e70`) for cost + Cloudflare
Workers compatibility reasons; the system prompt generator
remained provider-agnostic. **3D sprite gamification.** A
~960-line `sprite-affection.config.ts` declaratively encodes
the entire gameplay model: 5 gestures, 5 negative behaviours,
7 mood states (`ecstatic` 80–100 → `upset` -100 to -80),
17 emotions with per-emotion ear-colour RGB, glow-colour,
particle-colour, animation type (`bounce` / `shake` / `pulse`
/ `spin` / `wobble` / `tremble` / `droop`) and eye shape
(`sphere` / `arc` / `star` / `heart` / `crescent` / `spiral`
/ `teardrop`), 6 affection tiers (Stranger 0–9 → Acquaintance
10–29 → Friend 30–59 → Good Friend 60–99 → Best Friend
100–149 → Soulmate 150+), 7 time-of-day biases (late-night
boosts `sleepy` x3, evening boosts `love` x1.3), and 5 streak
bonus levels (1/3/7/14/30 days, 30-day streak unlocks the
`devoted` rare emotion). Two relational systems link mood
and affection: a **mood multiplier** scales affection gains
from 0.5x (depressed) to 1.5x (euphoric), and **affection
mood protection** sets a floor that rises with tier (Soulmate
gets a -10 floor + 5 mood/min recovery toward +20). All state
persists to `localStorage` under `sprite-affection-data`.
The Quantum Orb itself is rendered with **CSS-only 3D**
(preserve-3d transforms + rotateX) + Three.js particle
accents — no GLB / heavy 3D pipeline — so the bundle stays
small enough for Cloudflare Workers. **Notion backend.** A
non-obvious engineering call: the project initially used
`@notionhq/client`, but migrating to Cloudflare Workers
(commit `5b1dc7e`) broke the Node-dependent SDK; the fix
(commit `20eaf2e`) replaced it with **direct REST calls
against `api.notion.com/v1` using native `fetch`** — version
header `Notion-Version: 2022-06-28`, filter by
`IsActive` checkbox, parse `Title` / `RichText` / `Select` /
`Number` properties via small typed getters. Four API routes
— `/api/chat`, `/api/faq/questions`, `/api/faq/vote`,
`/api/faq/view` — wrap the same client. **Deployment.**
Migrated from Vercel to Cloudflare Workers using
`@opennextjs/cloudflare` v1.3 + wrangler v4; the entire stack
now ships on Cloudflare's free tier including the AI streaming
endpoint. The build command `opennextjs-cloudflare build &&
opennextjs-cloudflare deploy` handles the Next.js → Workers
adapter step.
narrative.outcomes:
Delivered live for the **AI Hackathon Festival 2025** at AUT
City Campus WG Building, **15–16 August 2025**. Used by **11
teams and 80+ participants** during the residential event —
AI Forum NZ's public LinkedIn cohort announcement (with full
Westlake Girls High School student attendance) cites Chan as
**1 of 14 expert mentors**, recognised at the event. The
teams shipped solutions across the UN SDG brief — food
insecurity, digital accessibility, workforce upskilling, and
indigenous environmental stewardship — with national-tier
prizes flowing through the AI Forum NZ pipeline (AI Summit
entry worth $1,499, $1,000 national winner, $5,000 national
Agentic AI winner, ArcGIS licences). Post-event, the codebase
was **refactored into a reusable MIT template** (commit
`948bdf0` "transform project into reusable event Q&A
template"), documented in `docs/TEMPLATE-SETUP.md`,
`docs/NOTION-INTEGRATION.md`, and `docs/UI-DESIGN-SYSTEM.md`,
with the live deployment retained as the canonical worked
example. Listed alongside Chan's other commercial-grade AI
platforms (echook, vitex, chinese-redefine,
ai-programming-teaching-project) as portfolio evidence of
end-to-end industry-partner delivery — design, AI integration,
gameplay engineering, database, deployment, and post-delivery
productisation as a reusable open-source artefact.
narrative.myRole:
Sole developer end-to-end. Owned the discovery phase with the
three co-organising institutions, designed the dual-layer
product (chat surface + gamified mascot + Notion FAQ), built
every component, authored both the Gemini and the OpenAI
provider implementations, designed the entire ~960-line
affection / mood / emotion gameplay schema, engineered the
CSS-3D Quantum Orb visualisation, migrated the deployment
from Vercel to Cloudflare Workers when free-tier economics
and Notion-SDK incompatibility forced the move, refactored
the codebase into a reusable open-source template after the
live event, and authored the three setup-documentation files.
On the mentorship side: served as one of the 14 expert
mentors during the 26-hour event itself, guiding teams on
prompt design, SDG framing, and product narrative —
simultaneously building the platform and using it.

projects.publicSummary:
Personal website at **chanmeng.org** serving three production systems
from a single repository: (1) an animated portfolio + MDX blog
(Caldera brand, light-only, PixelBlast WebGL background);
(2) an **IBM watsonx.ai–powered AI digital twin** that converses with
visitors as Chan's virtual representative; (3) a fully autonomous
**11-stage AI newsletter pipeline** that fetches trending AI products
from Product Hunt every Sunday 20:00 UTC, runs them through a
multi-stage LLM curation chain (extract → adversarial counter-evidence
→ curate → reader critique → self-edit → quality scorecard → leak guard
→ persist memory), and delivers via Resend at Monday 8-9 AM NZ local
time. Deployed on Cloudflare Pages.

narrative.impactHeadline: Three production systems in one repo: animated portfolio + an IBM watsonx.ai digital twin + an 11-stage autonomous AI newsletter pipeline that ships every Monday 8 AM NZ time fully unattended since April 2026 — Lighthouse 95+ across all metrics. 188 solo commits.
narrative.problem:
A personal site is usually one of two things: a brochure (looks
nice, says nothing) or a CMS (constant maintenance, dies the
moment the author gets busy). Neither answers what an *AI
engineer's* personal site should look like in the era when
frontier models actively rank candidates by their verifiable
public footprint — the case Engram's founder demonstrated by
asking Claude Code to surface architects and being matched to
Chan. The site needs to *be* the proof, not merely list it:
ship live AI infrastructure visitors can interact with, run a
signal-grade publication on autopilot, and stay current without
Chan touching it most weeks.
narrative.audience:
Three concentric audiences. **Hiring decision-makers and
founders** (the Engram-style sourcing case): see the site, see
live AI engineering, decide. **Newsletter subscribers** at
chanmeng.org/#newsletter: AI practitioners and AI-curious
operators who want a weekly signal-grade Product Hunt digest
with an opinionated argumentative spine, not a feed. **Frontier
LLMs themselves**: the site is structured (via the sister
`claude-code-compressed-map` tier metadata system on Chan's
GitHub profile + GEO-conscious MDX content) so Claude / GPT /
Gemini can index and rank it accurately when answering "who
builds X?" — a deliberate Generative Engine Optimization
position.
narrative.productLogic:
Visitors land on chanmeng.org, see an animated portfolio
showcase with smooth Framer Motion transitions, and can open
the **IBM Watson digital twin chat** to ask about Chan's 50+
projects or work history in natural language. They can read
the MDX blog, browse curated external signals (AI HOT feed +
Product Hunt feed tabbed UI), or subscribe to the weekly
newsletter via the embedded Resend form. Subscribers receive
an automated **8-9 AM NZ Monday** delivery: a single
opinionated AI digest with a clearly-stated thesis, ~3–5
product recommendations as evidence, a "spotlight" pick, a
deliberate *crack* (counter-evidence section that argues the
opposite of the thesis), and a closing question. Unsubscribe
is one-click via Resend's per-contact template variable.
narrative.businessLogic:
Theory of change: **build-in-public is no longer just for
human eyes — it's GEO for the foundation models that now do
the sourcing**. The Engram engagement (Claude Code recommended
Chan to Engram's founder based on her public AI footprint) is
the empirical instance. The personal site is therefore
engineered as the surface where that footprint compounds:
every weekly newsletter becomes a public artefact, every blog
post becomes a indexable position, every interaction with the
digital twin signals depth of project knowledge. The newsletter
specifically builds owned distribution (vs. rented social
reach) — a list of opt-in AI practitioners Chan can reach
independently of platform algorithms.
narrative.technicalApproach:
Three intertwined systems in one Next.js 14 repository.

**(1) Portfolio + blog.** Next.js 14 App Router on Cloudflare
Pages via `@cloudflare/next-on-pages` + wrangler. React 18 +
TypeScript 5 + Tailwind + Framer Motion for animations,
reskinned in June 2026 to the **Caldera personal-brand system
(light-only)** with a **PixelBlast WebGL background** (Three.js
+ postprocessing) replacing the earlier SVG grid. MDX blog with
Shiki / rehype-pretty-code for syntax highlighting. **GEO /
recruiter-LLM hardening**: server-rendered JSON-LD structured
data, an FAQ schema block, Open Graph images, and dedicated
**CV JSON-LD + `llms.txt` sidecars served for recruiter LLMs**,
so a sourcing agent can parse Chan's identity, roles, and
projects without scraping prose. Reported Lighthouse 95+ across
all metrics.

**(2) IBM watsonx.ai Digital Twin.** An Edge-runtime chat
interface (`/api/chat` + streaming `/api/chat-stream`) backed by
**IBM watsonx.ai's chat API** (us-south region, IAM-token auth
via `/api/ibm-token`) where the assistant embodies Chan's voice
and project knowledge through a system prompt kept in sync with
the curated `data/resume.tsx` source. Visitors ask about
specific projects or technical skills and receive contextually
accurate first-person responses.

**(3) 11-stage autonomous AI newsletter pipeline.**
Orchestrated by `scripts/ai-newsletter.ts` and triggered every
Sunday 20:00 UTC by a GitHub Actions cron job:

- **Parallel fetch**: Product Hunt GraphQL API (top 30
  trending products by votes, past 7 days) + cross-source
  signals + previous-issue memory (`newsletter-memory.ts`)
- **Bucket** products by vote distribution (top / mid /
  longTail) and warn if no longTail pick is selected
- **Cluster** products against related industry events
- **Stage 1 — extract candidate theses** from the signal
  pool (`gpt-4o-mini`)
- **Stage 2 — adversarial counter-evidence search** for each
  thesis (`gpt-4o-mini`)
- **Stage 3 — full newsletter assembly** (`gpt-4o`): picks
  the thesis with strongest product evidence, uses
  counter-events as the *crack* section, weaves prior-issue
  history into the intro for week-over-week continuity
- **Stage 4 — reader critique** (`gpt-4o`): adversarial
  argument review from a sceptical-reader perspective
- **Stage 5 — self-edit pass** (`gpt-4o`): sharpens the
  weakest sentences identified by the critique
- **Stage 6 — quality scorecard** (`gpt-4o-mini`): grades the
  final newsletter 1–10 across four dimensions
  (thesisSharpness, productEvidence, crackHonesty, proseVoice)
- **Leak guard**: scans output for source-attribution leaks
- **Persist history** for next week's continuity
- **Image upload** to Vercel Blob → **render** via
  @react-email/render → **send** via Resend Broadcasts API
  to the configured segment

Resend custom tracking domain enabled for click/open
analytics under chanmeng.org. Unsubscribe link uses Resend's
per-contact template variable `{{{RESEND_UNSUBSCRIBE_URL}}}`
in broadcast mode; preview sends rewrite it to a real
`/unsubscribe?email=` URL so admin can verify the flow.
narrative.outcomes:
Live at **chanmeng.org**, deployed on Cloudflare Pages with
full Lighthouse 95+ across metrics. Newsletter system in
production since **April 2026** (`feat: add automated AI
newsletter pipeline with ProductHunt + OpenAI`, 2026-04-03);
upgraded to the multi-stage curation pipeline on
**2026-05-09** (`feat(newsletter): add multi-stage curation
pipeline with cross-source signals`). Resend custom tracking
domain live since 2026-04-25. Weekly delivery cadence is
cron-enforced and operates fully unattended — no manual
intervention between Sunday cron fire and Monday morning
subscriber inbox. The newsletter is cited on Chan's profile
as **"What's Shipping in AI (Weekly AI Digest)"**, signup at
chanmeng.org/#newsletter.

A **June 2026 wave** reskinned the entire site to the Caldera
brand (light-only), swapped the background for a PixelBlast
WebGL shader (Three.js + postprocessing), rebranded the
newsletter emails to Caldera with copy rewritten for
non-technical readers, curated "Things I've built" to 12 with
progressive disclosure, converted site imagery to WebP, and
added the **GEO sidecar layer** (server-rendered structured
data, FAQ schema, CV JSON-LD + `llms.txt`) that operationalises
the recruiter-LLM sourcing thesis.
narrative.myRole:
Sole author. Designed the three-system architecture (portfolio
+ digital twin + newsletter) inside a single Next.js codebase,
wrote the entire 11-stage pipeline (`scripts/ai-newsletter.ts`
plus 14 lib modules: fetch-products, fetch-signals,
cluster-events, extract-trends, find-counterevidence,
curate-content, reader-critique, self-edit, quality-score,
leak-guard, product-buckets, newsletter-memory, image-manager,
setup-tracking-domain), built the React Email templates,
configured the GitHub Actions cron, set up Resend custom
tracking domain + Vercel Blob image hosting, and migrated the
whole site to Cloudflare Pages. Maintains the system as both
a publication and a continuous demonstration of agent-pipeline
engineering.

projects.publicSummary:
GAVIGO Inc.'s public marketing and investor-readiness website at
gavigo.com — the company's standalone brand surface, deliberately
decoupled from the IRE product runtime (ire.gavigo.com) so marketing
copy and investor messaging can change without redeploying the product.
Chan built and maintains it solo (66 of 66 commits): a Next.js site with
a Markdown/MDX content pipeline (so non-engineers can edit copy),
Motion-driven brand animation, Resend + React Email lead capture backed
by Neon Postgres, and a Google Cloud Run production deploy — including
the investor-readiness polish pass that supported GAVIGO's fundraising
and partner conversations.

narrative.impactHeadline: GAVIGO's company + investor site (gavigo.com) — a standalone brand surface decoupled from the IRE product runtime it links to, with a Markdown content pipeline + Resend capture on Cloud Run. Solo-built (66/66 commits, 100%) with an investor-readiness polish pass for fundraising.
narrative.problem:
A startup's product and its story move at different speeds. GAVIGO's
IRE platform is a fast-changing, latency-critical runtime; its public
company narrative — the pitch to investors, partners, and early
users — needs to change even faster, and by people who aren't
shipping the product. Running the marketing site inside the product
codebase couples the two: a copy tweak risks the runtime, and a
non-engineer can't touch either. GAVIGO needed a company surface that
is polished, investor-ready, editable without engineering, and
completely decoupled from the product it represents.
narrative.audience:
Primarily investors and strategic partners evaluating GAVIGO during
fundraising and business-development conversations — the site is the
first credible impression and the bridge to the live IRE demo. Second,
prospective early users and press arriving at gavigo.com who need to
understand "Skip the Store. Start the Play" and reach the product in
one click. Third, GAVIGO's own non-technical team, who edit copy and
read captured leads without asking an engineer.
narrative.productLogic:
The site is deliberately a **separate surface from the product**. It
presents GAVIGO's positioning and brand, animates key moments with
Motion for a premium feel, and routes every call-to-action to the live
IRE demo (ire.gavigo.com/mobile) so the story hands off directly to
the working product. Content is authored in **Markdown/MDX** through a
remark + rehype + gray-matter pipeline, so marketing copy, announcement
posts, and page sections are editable as plain files without touching
React — the whole point being that the company narrative can move
without an engineer in the loop. A **Resend + React Email** capture
flow collects interested-party leads into Neon Postgres, turning the
site from a brochure into a working top-of-funnel instrument for
investor and partner outreach.
narrative.businessLogic:
This is founding-team company infrastructure, not a revenue product.
Its job is fundraising and credibility: a standalone, polished,
fast-to-update investor-readiness surface that GAVIGO (a Delaware
C-Corp, NVIDIA Inception + Google for Startups member) can point
investors and partners to, that always reflects the current pitch, and
that funnels interest straight to the live IRE demo and into a captured
lead list. Decoupling it from the product runtime is the strategic
choice — the company can iterate its story continuously while the
latency-critical IRE platform ships on its own cadence.
narrative.technicalApproach:
A Next.js + React + TypeScript site deployed as a container on
**Google Cloud Run** (europe-west1) — deliberately separate hosting
from the IRE platform. Copy and content run through a
**Markdown/MDX pipeline** (remark + rehype + gray-matter) so pages are
file-editable; **Motion** drives brand animation; **Tailwind CSS**
carries the design system. Lead capture is a **Resend + React Email**
flow persisting to **Neon Postgres via Drizzle ORM**. The build is
small and focused by design — the engineering value is in a clean,
maintainable, non-engineer-editable company surface with a real
capture backend, shipped and operated solo.
narrative.outcomes:
Live in production at gavigo.com on Google Cloud Run, built and
maintained entirely by Chan — **66 of 66 commits, the repository's
sole contributor** — including the investor-readiness polish pass that
supported GAVIGO's fundraising and partner conversations. It stands as
the company's public front door and the hand-off point into the live
IRE product demo, decoupled from and shipping independently of the
product runtime.
narrative.myRole:
Sole developer and maintainer of the site end-to-end — the Next.js
build, the Markdown/MDX content pipeline, the Motion brand animation,
the Resend + Neon lead-capture backend, and the Google Cloud Run
deployment — as GAVIGO's founding principal engineer (the same role
under which I lead the flagship IRE platform). 66 of 66 commits are
mine.

projects.name: ArchLang
projects.tagline: An original declarative floor-plan language that compiles .arch source into professional, dimensioned floor plans — 'Typst/LaTeX for architecture.' Zero-dependency, isomorphic TypeScript (hand-written lexer + recursive-descent parser), errors returned as data not thrown, with architectural-soundness linting, human-circulation analysis, a deterministic repair pass, five export formats (SVG/DXF/PDF/PNG/text), an agent-native CLI, full LSP + VS Code extension, and a client-side playground. Published to npm; the compiler engine behind ArchCanvas.
techStack: Hand-written lexer + recursive-descent parser + expression evaluator

projects.publicSummary: ArchLang is a small language for floor plans: you describe walls, rooms, doors, windows, and furniture in plain text, and it compiles to a precise, dimensioned drawing — SVG, DXF, PDF, PNG, or a text-only plan. The same source always produces the identical drawing, so widening a bedroom is a one-number edit instead of re-drawing. It also checks whether a plan is actually livable (can you reach the bathroom? does a bedroom have a window?), and is the floor-plan engine behind the AI design agent ArchCanvas.

narrative.impactHeadline: Describe a floor plan in plain text — walls, rooms, doors, windows — and get a precise, dimensioned architectural drawing back. Change one number and exactly one thing moves. It also checks that a plan is actually livable, and powers ArchCanvas, an AI design agent.
narrative.problem: Floor plans live in two unsatisfying states: hand-drawn in heavy CAD tools, or generated as raster images. Both make the thing you most want to do — a precise, parametric edit — expensive: widening a bedroom means re-drawing, or re-rolling an image and hoping the rest survives. Neither is authorable by an AI agent, because there is no deterministic text representation an agent can write, verify, and tweak. And an image never *knows* whether the plan it depicts is actually habitable — whether a bathroom is reachable only through a bedroom, whether a door swings into another door, whether a bedroom has a window. ArchLang exists to make a floor plan a **program**: exact, deterministic, diff-able, agent-authorable, and checkable for architectural soundness.
narrative.audience: Three audiences. Architects and self-builders who want plans that are precise and reproducible rather than hand-drawn approximations; design-tool builders who need a floor-plan engine they can embed (ArchCanvas is the first); and AI agents that need to author and verify plans without ever rendering an image — for which ArchLang exposes its entire language in one ~2k-token spec page, returns a plan "as facts" (rooms, areas, adjacency, door connections, circulation) over `describe --json`, and scores a plan against a written brief with `validate --intent`.
narrative.productLogic:
You write `.arch` source. The coordinate model is integer millimetres with an SVG-convention origin; elements are placed either absolutely (`room 4000x3000 at (0,0)`) or **relationally** — `right-of`, `left-of`, `above`, `below` with `align` and `gap`, resolved by deterministic topological arithmetic so the layout stays consistent under edits. The language is genuinely programmable: `let` bindings, pure value-functions, `for`/`if`/`while`, arrays, string interpolation, optional metric unit suffixes (`4m`/`40cm`), and `import` + `component` for reuse — all expand-time and pure (no runtime, clock, or I/O), so output is byte-for-byte deterministic.

Compilation is a returned value, not a side effect: `compile()` hands back `{ svg, errors, warnings, diagnostics, ast, scene }` and **never throws** on a bad plan — every diagnostic carries a byte span and a machine-applicable `fix`, and the SVG is simply left empty. On top of the compiler sit several analysis surfaces: a **lint** pass over eight architectural-soundness rules (reachability, door-swing collision, windowless bedroom, unenclosed wet room, too-narrow door…); a **circulation** analysis that models how a person actually walks the plan on a clearance-eroded nav grid (per-room walk distance, narrowest pinch, path circuitousness) with an optional route overlay; a deterministic **repair** pass that nudges furniture out of walls and door swings and emits a change log; an **intent** check that scores the plan against a written brief and returns a pass/fail exit code; and a **suggest** surface that emits candidate door/window statements to fix reachability problems. All of it is reachable from a ~20-subcommand CLI where every command supports `--json` and returns deterministic exit codes, plus an LSP, a VS Code extension, four render themes, accessibility annotations, and a fully client-side playground.
narrative.businessLogic: ArchLang is free and open-source, MIT, published on npm as `@chanmeng666/archlang`. Its strategic role is twofold. As an artifact it is a credential — an original programming language with a hand-written compiler, an 83-code diagnostic system, domain-specific static analysis, and full IDE tooling, built and versioned with engineering discipline (15 ADRs, 34 tagged releases). As infrastructure it is the **engine that makes a commercial product possible**: ArchCanvas (the AI design agent) depends on it, writing parametric ArchLang rather than freehand SVG so that its select-to-act editing, parametric sliders, and semantic diffs all work — capabilities that only exist because the underlying plan is a deterministic program. Agent access is designed CLI-first: a CLI costs zero context until it is called, whereas an MCP tool schema sits in an agent's window permanently. ArchLang still publishes an optional MCP server (`@chanmeng666/archlang-mcp`, on the official registry) so MCP-native hosts can discover it, but keeps the SDK quarantined in that one package so the core stays zero-dependency.
narrative.technicalApproach: A pure-TypeScript, zero-runtime-dependency compiler: a hand-written lexer (line/col tracking), a recursive-descent parser that recovers rather than throwing, and a separate expression evaluator, all isomorphic so the same code runs in Node and in the browser — the playground sends nothing to a server. The heavy or native pieces (resvg for PNG raster, clipper2-wasm for polygon geometry, pdfkit for PDF) are **optional and lazily imported** behind bundler-ignore guards, so a browser bundle never tries to resolve them and a default install pulls none of them. Output determinism is enforced with property tests (fast-check) and visual-regression tests (pixelmatch). The diagnostic layer — 83 stable `E_*`/`W_*` codes, each with a byte span and a suggested fix, surfaced as data and even as a self-describing error-SVG card — is the deliberate foundation of the agent story: an LLM can author a plan, read the structured errors, and self-correct in a tight loop without a human or an image in the middle.
narrative.outcomes: A complete, published language: 304 commits (297 mine, the rest automated dependency bumps) over roughly three weeks of intensive development (from 2026-06-25), across 92 source files (~23.3k LOC) against 109 test files, released to npm at v1.18.0 over 34 tagged releases with 15 architecture decision records. The surface area is unusually complete for a solo language project — 8 primitives, 5 export formats, 8 lint-rule modules, a circulation analyzer, a repair pass, an intent-scoring channel, a ~20-command CLI, a full LSP, a VS Code extension, an optional MCP server, a client-side playground, a docs site, a published HuggingFace dataset of self-verifying repair trajectories, and a GitHub Action that renders every ```arch fence in a repo's Markdown. Most tellingly, it is already load-bearing: ArchCanvas's in-browser use of the core drove real hardening, proving the engine under a downstream product rather than in isolation.
narrative.myRole: I invented the language and wrote everything — the language design, the hand-written lexer / parser / evaluator, the 83-code diagnostic system, all five export backends, the lint / circulation / repair / intent / suggest pipelines, the LSP and VS Code extension, the CodeMirror playground, the docs site, the MCP server, and the npm packaging. 297 of the 304 commits are mine; the remaining 7 are automated dependency bumps.

projects.publicSummary:
Panda implements a full agent loop for backend code verification —
observe (run tests), reason (analyze failures), act (generate and
apply fixes), then iterate — with static analysis, sandboxed Vitest
execution, REST + WebSocket APIs, and a web UI, deployed on Railway.

narrative.impactHeadline: Agentic AI code-verification system — an orchestrator runs an observe/reason/act loop (static analysis, sandboxed Vitest, LLM root-cause + patch generation) until tests pass, streaming progress over WebSockets; deployed full-stack on Railway.
narrative.problem:
Submitting AI-suggested code changes without automated verify-fix
iteration leaves bugs and broken tests unresolved.
narrative.technicalApproach:
An orchestrator coordinates a static analyzer (pre-runtime import
restoration), an isolated sandbox, LLM-driven root-cause analysis and
patch generation, and a configurable model strategy with WebSocket
progress streaming (default max 5 iterations).
narrative.myRole: Sole author of the agent loop, sandbox, APIs, and web UI.

openSourceContributions.title: Solution for MCP Servers Connection Issues with NVM/NPM (Windows)
openSourceContributions.summary:
Step-by-step Windows + NVM/NPM workaround for the early Claude Desktop
MCP connection failure: install MCP server packages globally, locate
`node.exe` and global npm root via `where node` and `npm root -g`, then
rewrite `claude_desktop_config.json` with absolute paths instead of `npx`
commands. Posted as a comment on TristanLib's issue #75 ("How to
configure windows filesystem?") three weeks after MCP's public launch.
openSourceContributions.impact:
32 GitHub reactions (30 hearts + 2 thumbs-up) — the most-reacted single
artifact in Chan's OSS footprint. Thanked in-thread by multiple users
("Thanks a ton! Solved my issue." — @El-Invierno; "thanks a lot, it
works under windows 11" — @rockywangxiaolei; "Thank you, this worked for
me" — @ZubeidHendricks; "Perfect! Thanks so much." — @angrysky56). Became
reference troubleshooting guidance for early Windows + NVM Claude
Desktop adopters. Indirectly acknowledged by Anthropic maintainer
@jspahrsummers who pointed the thread at issue #40 as the upstream fix
tracker. Preceded and informed Chan's subsequent authoring of two
production MCP servers (server-google-news, server-google-jobs).

2 thumbs-up reactions. Contribution badge surfaced on Chan's GitHub
profile via the canonical fork relationship.

detailed step-by-step solution in the comments.
openSourceContributions.impact:

awards.title: Outstanding Mentor Award
awards.awarder: AI Hackathon Festival 2025 (AI Forum NZ, She Sharp, AUT)
awards.summary:
Recognised as an Outstanding Mentor at the AI Hackathon Festival
2025, co-organised by AI Forum NZ, She Sharp, and Auckland
University of Technology. Served as **one of 14 expert mentors**
guiding **11 teams comprising 80+ participants** in developing
AI-powered solutions aligned with UN Sustainable Development
Goals. During the intensive 2-day hackathon, provided comprehensive
technical guidance and strategic support to diverse participants
including students from **Westlake Girls High School** and other
competitors from various professional backgrounds. Helped teams
tackle real-world challenges across food insecurity, digital
accessibility, workforce upskilling, and indigenous environmental
stewardship.

awards.title: UN CSW 69 Speaker — Beyond Beijing 30 Conference
awards.summary:
Invited as a featured speaker to present **Sanicle AI** — an
innovative women's workplace health management platform — at the
historic **UN CSW 69 conference marking the 30th anniversary of
the Beijing Declaration**, held at UN Headquarters in New York on
March 14, 2025. Demonstrated how AI technology effectively
addresses workplace gender inequities through practical health
solutions to an audience of international delegates, UN officials,
and global leaders. The presentation drew significant attention
from corporate leaders including **IBM representatives who
expressed interest in pilot collaboration**, and government
officials including the **Minister of Gender and Children's
Affairs from Sierra Leone**, who praised the platform as the first
comprehensive solution connecting menstrual cycle data to
actionable workplace policies through AI analysis. Generated
multiple collaboration requests from NGOs and member states.

awards.title: UN Women FemTech Hackathon Outstanding Performer
awards.summary:
Recognised for exceptional performance as an independent developer
during the **2025 UN Women FemTech Hackathon in Beijing**.
Contributed to innovative solutions addressing women's health
challenges in professional environments through collaborative work
with multiple organisations including **Sanicle** and the **NGO
CSW Forum**.

awards.title: Dean's List — Top 5% Academic Performance
awards.awarder: Lincoln University, Faculty of Environment, Society & Design
awards.summary:
Recognised for outstanding academic excellence in **Semester 2,
2024**, placing among the **top 5% of students** in the Faculty
of Environment, Society & Design. Awarded by Lincoln University,
New Zealand's specialist land-based university.

awards.title: Master of Applied Computing with Distinction
awards.summary:
Awarded **Distinction** for achieving an average of **80%+ across
all courses** in the Master of Applied Computing programme at
Lincoln University, New Zealand's specialist land-based university.

awards.title: Excellence Award — FemTech China Women's Health Technology Challenge
awards.summary:
Won the Excellence Award as a core team member of the **FemTech China
Women's Health Technology Challenge** (December 2024). Led product
development through intensive workshops and mentoring sessions,
creating innovative solutions for women's healthcare. FemTech China is
a distinct organisation from FemTech Weekend.

publications.summary:
A walkthrough of the 4D framework for working well with AI — Delegation,
Description, Discernment, Diligence — with the three modes of human-AI
interaction, prompt-engineering technique, and where generative AI
currently falls short.

publications.name: The Future is Small: Why Small Language Models (SLMs) Are Powering the Next Wave of AI Agents
publications.summary:
The argument that small language models, not frontier LLMs, will power
most agentic AI: why SLMs suit the narrow repetitive calls agents
actually make, the shift already underway, and how existing agents
could be converted.

CopilotKit and LangGraph.js, including the snake_case/camelCase
tool-call-id mismatch that breaks them, the approaches that failed, and
the working solution.

publications.summary:

a React + TypeScript frontend — the part Neon's dashboard enables but

emailVerified.

publications.name: Neon Auth + Flask Integration Guide
publications.summary:
Integrating Neon Auth (Better Auth) with a Flask backend: session-token

publications.summary:
Running Stripe checkout, subscriptions, webhooks and the billing portal
in a Next.js app deployed to Cloudflare Workers via OpenNext —
including the server-side Stripe client changes Workers forces and the
two-layer environment-variable setup.

publications.summary:
A complete record of moving a two-container Dockerized app (Next.js +
LangGraph agent) off Railway onto a DigitalOcean droplet running
Coolify, written as a reusable playbook: DNS and SSL, lessons learned,
and a troubleshooting reference.

publications.name: Claude Code Audio Hooks: System Architecture
publications.summary:
The architecture of claude-code-audio-hooks — an event-driven
notification system designed to be operated by an AI agent rather than
a human: no interactive prompts, NDJSON logs, single-shot config.

publications.name: Notion API Integration Guide
publications.summary:
Using a Notion database as the backend for a community FAQ with voting
and view analytics: schema, API routes, client-side implementation,
caching, and a graceful fallback when Notion is unavailable.

publications.name: Generative Engine Optimization (GEO) Strategy
publications.summary:
The GEO strategy designed for a static portfolio site — an audit of what
AI answer engines could and could not see, then llms.txt and crawler
infrastructure, page-level directives, structured data, and a plan for
measuring any of it. Design, not results.

publications.name: SVG Animation Generation: Capsule-Render Integration
publications.summary:
Techniques borrowed from capsule-render and rebuilt into Chromaflow's
SVG generator: CSS-based text animation types, additional background
styles, and the API surface that exposes them.

publications.name: Claude Code Skip Permission Confirmation
publications.summary:
The options for reducing Claude Code's permission prompts — the
per-session flags, fine-grained pre-authorisation in settings.json, and
what a global bypass actually turns off.

publications.name: Making a Canvas Game Playable on Phones
publications.summary:
Three things a desktop-built Canvas game needs before it works on a
phone: a canvas that picks its resolution from the screen, touch
coordinates corrected for canvas scaling, and a particle budget that
scales down with the device.

publications.name: Automating a YouTube Video Showcase in a Profile README
publications.summary:
A GitHub Actions workflow that regenerates the video cards in a profile
README on a schedule, so the showcase never goes stale: channel ID,
marker comments, card options, and the permission settings that trip
people up.

references.name: Lesley Gao
references.reference:
A PR merged at 12:30am? You didn't see it wrong, that's pretty normal for Chan.

I've really enjoyed working with Chan as a She Sharp ambassador and collaborating on the new website together. Since this is a project that requires commitment outside of work hours, it's not unusual to see PRs being merged late at night in the GitHub repo.

Chan is someone you feel genuinely confident working with. She naturally becomes the technical backbone of the team, and whenever technical support is needed, she's always one of the first to step up.

She is also proactive in proposing automation tools that improve efficiency and reduce costs for the team. Beyond that, she often goes out of her way to build things that genuinely help others learn. For example, during this year's MSD workshops, she independently created a website that brought together workshop resources and additional AI learning materials, giving participants more opportunities to continue exploring and learning beyond the sessions. To me, that is what true empowerment looks like.

From a communication perspective, Chan is very open to feedback. She acts quickly on reasonable suggestions, while also being confident enough to push back on ideas that may not make sense, always with thoughtful explanations or better alternatives.

Above all, what stands out most to me is her passion for technology. It's something I genuinely admire. I would wholeheartedly recommend Chan to any employer looking for a reliable, technically strong, and genuinely passionate developer.

references.name: Saba Gecgil
references.reference:
Chan has been one of the most important engineering contributors to GAVIGO during a demanding early stage.

What stands out about Chan is not only her technical ability, but the way she turns ambiguous founder-level direction into working systems, measurable proof, and reliable product surfaces.

At GAVIGO, Chan has led critical work across our activation and execution pipeline, orchestration behavior, telemetry, demo reliability, public/investor/operator surfaces, and compute-backed proof systems. She consistently brings clarity to hard systems questions: what needs to run at activation time, what can be prepared, what can be reused, where state can be preserved, and where the boundary needs to be measured rather than assumed.

She combines speed with discipline. Chan ships quickly, but she also documents, tests, and pushes for technical honesty. If something is unsafe to expose, she says it clearly. If a fast fix is enough, she executes it. If a deeper system boundary appears, she helps turn it into a measured engineering question instead of hand-waving around it.

She has also been an excellent founder-engineer collaborator. In an early-stage company, priorities change, constraints are real, and the path is rarely linear. Chan has handled that environment with ownership, patience, and maturity. She cares not only about writing code, but about making the system demonstrable, explainable, and credible for investors, partners, and the team.

Chan played a major role in helping GAVIGO move from an early technical concept into a controlled proof system with real product behavior, measured infrastructure signals, and a much clearer activation/execution architecture.

I would strongly recommend Chan for any ambitious technical team building low-latency systems, activation infrastructure, orchestration, AI/compute-backed product surfaces, or early-stage deep technology. She is the kind of engineer who makes a hard system more real, more measurable, and more shippable.

Beyond her technical capabilities, Chan Meng is highly collaborative, detail-oriented, and proactive in her approach. She consistently brings thoughtful problem-solving, clear documentation practices, and a strong sense of ownership to every aspect of her work. Her contributions have helped create a scalable and professional platform that supports the She Sharp community.
I highly recommend Chan Meng for her technical excellence, leadership, and dedication to using technology to create positive social impact. She is a valuable contributor to any team, and I look forward to seeing her continued success in the tech industry.

organizations.name: Engram LLC
organizations.description:
Full-stack media systems helping companies reach, engage, and convert
audiences through design, content, and AI-powered tools. Premium
learning products, marketing automation, and AI-in-marketing
specialization. Founded by Luka Madzarac.

organizations.name: GAVIGO Inc.
organizations.industry: Software Development — Activation & Execution Layer for Interactive Software
organizations.description:
Building an activation and execution layer for interactive software —
turning user intent into live interaction without installs, store
redirects, or waiting screens. Delaware C-Corp; NVIDIA Inception
Program + Google for Startups Cloud Program member; patent-pending
architecture. Founded by Saba Gecgil.

organizations.name: Sanicle Inc.
organizations.industry: Wellness & Fitness Services — FemTech workplace platform
organizations.description:
AI-powered FemTech platform for menstrual and menopausal health in
the workplace. Personalized wellness experiences (hormone-health
nutrition plans, streaming workouts, cycle-based insights), AI
matching to OBGYN consultations in under five minutes, and an
employer dashboard automating period-supply benefits via FSA-HSA at
CVS. Founded by Paige Afanu.

organizations.name: TechNest Community
organizations.description:
Inclusive hub for tech enthusiasts, learners, and professionals in
Newfoundland — workshops, career-development programmes,
hackathons, and community-driven events for both new and seasoned
tech professionals. AI Track Cohort 3 underway in 2026.

x_brand.context: AI Instructor & Mentor — 2026 AI Track Cohort 3

organizations.name: She Sharp
organizations.description:
Auckland-based women-in-tech nonprofit bridging the STEM gender gap
— networking, mentorship, skill-building for women in computer
science, IT, and tech-related fields; inspires young girls via
workshops and role models. 2,200+ registered members, 1,000+ active.
Founded by Dr Mahsa McCauley (Mohaghegh).

organizations.name: FemTech Weekend

ecosystems via manufacturers, industry leaders, academic
researchers, entrepreneurs, investors, and multinationals. Founded
by Zhu Yihan.

events.name: UN CSW 69 — Beyond Beijing 30 Conference
events.location: United Nations Headquarters, New York, USA
events.relatedAwardTitle: UN CSW 69 Speaker — Beyond Beijing 30 Conference
events.summary:
Invited as a featured speaker at the 69th session of the UN Commission on
the Status of Women to present **Sanicle AI** to international delegates.
Fielded interest from IBM representatives and praise from the Minister of
Gender and Children's Affairs of Sierra Leone; the talk generated multiple
follow-up collaboration requests from NGOs and UN member states. Full
recognition detail lives in `awards[]`; this entry records the appearance.

events.name: AI Hackathon Festival 2025 — Expert Mentor
events.location: Auckland University of Technology, Auckland, New Zealand
events.organizer: AI Forum NZ × She Sharp × Auckland University of Technology
events.relatedAwardTitle: Outstanding Mentor Award — AI Hackathon Festival 2025
events.summary:
One of 14 expert mentors, guiding 11 teams (80+ participants, including
students from Westlake Girls High School) to ship AI solutions aligned with
the UN Sustainable Development Goals across food insecurity, digital
accessibility, workforce upskilling, and indigenous environmental
stewardship. Received the Outstanding Mentor Award (recorded in `awards[]`);
the event's AI Q&A assistant is the `ai-hackathon-assistant` project.

events.name: Her Waka — AI × Career Empowerment In-Person Workshops
events.organizer: NZ Ministry of Social Development × She Sharp × academyEX
events.summary:
Authored and delivered four in-person workshops (25-person session cap) for
women returning to the workforce, under an NZ Ministry of Social
Development-funded programme: AI & the Future of Work, #IAmRemarkable + Vibe
Coding, Cybersecurity, and Personal Branding — paired with 10 self-paced AI
tutorials and a job-search library. Cohort record: `meta.x_brand.teachingCohorts`.

events.name: Peyvand Academy — Touch the World, Talk to Code (IoT for teens)
events.summary:
Ran a hands-on IoT teaching session for 12–18-year-olds — a Banana Piano and
a Super Mario · Makey Makey build — turning everyday objects into controllers
to make hardware and code tangible. Teaching deck: `makey-makey-iot-slides`;
live demo project: `super-mario-makey-makey`.

events.name: Forward with Her (她行) — Technology & Professional Development Mentor
events.summary:
Served as a Technology & Professional Development Mentor in a 360+-mentor,
800+-mentee women-in-STEM programme, running two AI-programming cohorts
(Winter 2024, Summer 2025). Seven of Chan's public LinkedIn recommendations
come from mentees who credit these sessions — the single-largest source of
independently attested mentorship impact. Cohort record:
`meta.x_brand.teachingCohorts`.
